AMZN — Amazon.com, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\AMZN (filings + transcripts) · _equity_calls · _briefings\by-ticker\AMZN.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

🔖 Themes: Custom ASIC / TPU (vs merchant GPU) · Tokenmaxxing / AI Monetization

Snapshot

Three reportable segments: North America (Q1'26 $104.1B rev, +12%, 7.9% OI margin), International ($39.8B, +11% ex-FX), and AWS ($37.6B, +28%, $14.2B OI). AWS is ~21% of revenue but the overwhelming majority of operating income and the entire valuation debate. On top of the retail flywheel (1P/3P marketplace, FBA, Prime, fast/quick commerce) sit three high-margin attach businesses — Advertising ($17.2B Q1'26, +22%), Prime Video, and now an AI-infrastructure complex (Trainium/Graviton custom silicon, Bedrock, Anthropic anchor). New optionality: Amazon LEO (satellite broadband, commercial launch Q3'26) and Amazon Supply Chain Services (3P logistics / LTL). The stock is positioned by the Street as the "infrastructure backbone of AI" — OpenAI + Anthropic both running on AWS.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 FY26 (call 2026-04-29): Revenue $181.5B, +17% y/y (+15% ex-FX); operating income $23.9B; operating margin 13.1% — highest ever. AWS grew 28% y/y, fastest in 15 quarters, +$2B q/q (largest Q4→Q1 step-up ever), $150B run-rate. AWS backlog $364B (Jassy, Q1) — and explicitly excludes the >$100B Anthropic deal announced subsequently. AI revenue run-rate >$15B; Bedrock customer spend +170% q/q, 125k+ customers, ~80% of the Fortune 100; OpenAI's GPT-5.4 added to Bedrock (5.5 imminent). Custom silicon >$20B run-rate, +40% q/q; Trainium revenue commitments exceed $225B; Trainium3 "nearly fully subscribed." Retail unit growth +15% (highest since COVID); grocery now #2 US grocer ($150B+ gross sales '25). Q1 cash capex $43.2B; FY26 capex guide ~$200B (Q4'25 call), predominantly AWS.

Q2'26 guide (Q1 call): net sales $194–199B; OI $20–24B. Headwinds flagged: ~$1B y/y LEO cost step-up and higher transport/fuel costs (partly offset by FBA surcharge); memory/component costs "skyrocketed." Bernstein (Mark Shmulik, 2026-04-30) calls the OI guide "very conservative" and reads the rev guide as implying continued AWS acceleration into "low-to-mid 30s." Jassy framed AI demand as "barbelled" (Q4'25 call) — AI labs + runaway apps on one end, enterprise productivity/cost-avoidance on the other — with the durable middle (enterprise production workloads + net-new AI-native apps) still to come.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: AWS re-acceleration is the core long. AI demand "exploding," dragging core compute with it (post-training, RL, agentic tool-use) — Jassy, Q1'26. Custom-silicon edge is the differentiator: Trainium 30–40% better price/perf vs comparable GPUs, lets AMZN "save billions on capex and extract points of margin leverage" (Shmulik, Bernstein 2026-04-30) — the only hyperscaler to hold capex flat while peers raised on input-cost inflation, signaling supply-chain discipline. Backlog breadth improving: ~$20B of the sequential step-up is non-frontier-lab, the highest on record (Shmulik). Jefferies named AMZN a top 2H26 pick (Brent Thill, briefing 2026-06-06); at the Jefferies internet conf (2026-06-01) AWS/Bedrock cited as the structural winner as enterprises push net-new app builds to the cloud. MS (Brian Nowak) flags SpaceX $50/watt compute deal as ~16% upside to AWS '27 rev if monetization reaches $25/watt (briefing 2026-06-18).
  • Bear: Capex/ROIC is the swing factor — $200B FY26 capex, capex/rev gone from 7%→20% (Q1'20→Q1'26), and Jassy concedes early-year FCF is "challenged." Memory/component cost inflation pressures unit economics. AWS web/SSO metrics softening: Bernstein (Shmulik/Arbsman) flagged non-AI cloud SSO growth "unusually flattened" in May with 4Q non-AI cloud growth at risk if it persists (briefing 2026-06-06); Bernstein (Shuster) earlier cut AWS web-metric color, 4-week weakening, "Q4 risk" (briefing 2026-06-05). Agentic-commerce threat to retail/ad funnel (JPM Horvers/Anmuth "Retail vs. AMZN" deep dive, 2026-06-16). Ownership signal mixed: Berkshire fully exited in Q1; Druckenmiller trimmed to a token position — offset by Ackman ($3.01B, top position) and Tiger (briefings 2026-05-18).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: BofA (Justin Post) Buy, PO $310 (briefing 2026-06-18). Barclays (Ross Sandler) OW, PT $330 (Leo + India, briefing 2026-06-09/06-11). Bernstein Outperform, $315 (Shuster, briefing 2026-06-05). Citi (Ronald Josey) Buy. JPM (Anmuth) OW. MS (Nowak) Attractive. Jefferies (Thill) top 2H26 pick. Broad-based bullish; the disagreement is on the 4Q non-AI cloud-consumption flag (Bernstein) vs. the AWS-acceleration narrative everyone else is underwriting.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Prime Day — begins June 23, 96 hrs, BofA est. ~$22B GMV (≈+5% y/y; Japan/Australia shifted to 3Q). Near-term retail check (briefing 2026-06-18).
  • Q2 FY26 print (no confirmed date in sources; BofA expects rev at/above high end of guide).
  • AWS backlog trajectory — does the >$100B Anthropic deal show up and does non-frontier-lab breadth keep building.
  • Amazon LEO commercial launch (Q3'26); capex capitalization begins 4Q26 (Barclays). Signed commitments: Delta (≥half fleet from 2028), JetBlue, AT&T, Vodafone, NASA, Apple (direct-to-device via Globalstar).
  • Trainium3 ramp / T4 in 2027 / possibility of selling Trainium racks externally (Jassy: "good chance in the next couple of years").
  • Non-AI cloud consumption into 4Q — Bernstein's flagged risk.
  • Agentic-commerce execution (Rufus: MAU +115%, engagement +400% y/y).

Risks

  • Capital intensity / ROIC: $200B FY26 capex, depreciation drag on AWS margin, suppressed near-term FCF (Q1'26 call; 10-K capex-cycle disclosures).
  • AI demand concentration: backlog skewed to a few frontier labs; Anthropic exposure both an asset and a single-point dependency.
  • Component/memory cost inflation and supply constraints squeezing infra unit economics (Q1'26 call).
  • Competition: Azure (MSFT AI ARR >$37B, +123% — Bernstein 2026-04-30) and GCP (backlog surge, TPU rack sales) contesting AI cloud; agentic commerce threatening retail/ad funnel.
  • Regulatory/antitrust, FX, energy/power availability, geopolitical (10-K FY25 risk factors; LEO/supply-chain execution risk).

Consensus estimates (BBG)

  • Equity calls: 2026-04-30 Bernstein hyperscaler review; 2026-06-01 Jefferies internet conf. Full list (20) in INDEX §AMZN.

GOOG — Alphabet Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\GOOG (filings + transcripts) · _equity_calls · _briefings\by-ticker\GOOG.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

🔖 Themes: Custom ASIC / TPU (vs merchant GPU) · Tokenmaxxing / AI Monetization

Snapshot

Alphabet is the full-stack AI franchise: Google Services (Search, YouTube, subscriptions/devices — ~$90B/qtr, the cash engine), Google Cloud (GCP + Workspace + Wiz security, now the marginal growth driver), and Other Bets (Waymo, etc.). The differentiated edge vs. other hyperscalers is owning the entire vertical stack — frontier models (Gemini), custom silicon (TPU, Axion CPU), networking/OCS, data centers, and apps/agents — which management frames as the source of leading power/perf efficiency and ROIC (Q1 FY26, 2026-04-29). Sits at the center of the AI capex cycle as both a demand anchor (its own ~$180-190B C26 capex) and an emerging merchant-silicon supplier (external TPU sales begin late C26).

Estado atual (Q1 FY26, 2026-04-29)

  • Consolidated revenue $109.9B, +22% (+19% cc); op income +30% to $39.7B, op margin 36.1%; EPS $5.11 (+82%, flattered by unrealized equity gains). 11th straight quarter of double-digit growth.
  • Search & Other +19% to $60.4B — fastest in ~5 years; queries at all-time high, AI Overviews + AI Mode framed as expansionary (AI Mode queries 3x longer, monetizing previously-hard long-tail). Coverage upside vs. historical ~20% (Schindler, Q1 FY26).
  • Google Cloud +63% to $20B (first time >$20B); op income tripled to $6.6B, margin 32.9% (vs 17.8% y/y). GenAI-built products +~800% y/y. Backlog nearly doubled q/q to $462B, ~50%+ to convert to revenue within 24 months. Backlog now includes TPU hardware sales to select customers' own data centers — small revenue late C26, majority in C27.
  • YouTube ads +11% to $9.9B (DR-led, the soft spot — brand muted; GS 2026-04-30 flags secular shift to subs). Subs/devices +19%; 350M paid subs; Gemini app 750M+ MAU (Q4 FY25); Gemini Enterprise paid MAU +40% q/q.
  • Capex $35.7B in Q1 (60% servers / 40% DC+networking); FY26 guide raised to $180-190B; C27 to "significantly increase." Compute-constrained near-term — Sundar: cloud revenue "would have been higher" if supply allowed. ~half of ML compute allocated to Cloud (Q4 FY25). $126.8B cash, $77.5B LT debt; +5% dividend.
  • Subsequent: $80B→$85B equity raise (Jun '26; $30B public + $40B ATM + $10B Berkshire PP at ~$351/$348) for AI infra — largest US corporate equity offering ever (briefing 2026-06-03/06).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull:
    • "Best asset on Earth," won the AI Game of Thrones — beauty-pageant Q1 print, TPU bull case now the base case, cloud guided to 80-90% growth next ~7 quarters per MS backlog file (Nowak, MS, 2026-05-08). Cloud → high-40s% of Alphabet EBIT by '28/'29.
    • GS (Eric Sheridan) PT $450, owned/liked — Q1 drove a positive revision cycle: ~30%+ cloud revenue revision in C27/28, mid-teens consolidated OI revision; ~$14 GAAP EPS '27 / ~$17 '28 (buy-side whisper $15+/$17.50-18+); sum-of-parts supportive >$450; custom-silicon mix + non-AI workload reaccel support durable cloud margins high-20s/mid-30s (GS, 2026-04-30).
    • TPU ROIC arbitrage — GOOGL renting ~110k GB300 from SpaceX at ~$50/watt implies line-of-sight to higher-monetizing products; MS model implies +27% upside to GCP growth at $25/watt monetization (Nowak, MS, briefing 2026-06-17). Barclays (Sandler) OW $405, AI Search = "biggest change in 25 years," higher CTR/coverage (briefing 2026-05-29). Citi (Josey) Buy $447 on AI Search ad formats (briefing 2026-05-21). BofA: "most comprehensive full-stack AI offering of any company" (briefing 2026-06-03). SpaceX 2015 stake = ~$131B embedded gain at rumored IPO (briefing 2026-06-04).
  • Bear:
    • Multiple, not fundamentals — Nowak ranks GOOG #3 of MAG (Meta>Amazon>Google): "hard to re-rate Google five turns on a $5T cap," especially with large AI IPOs (OpenAI/Anthropic) competing for capital; no near-term dry powder for multiple expansion vs. AMZN where Trainium third-party optionality is unpriced (MS, 2026-05-08).
    • Capex / FCF / dilution — C27 capex revised to ~$300B (MS, +57% y/y, Altimeter data, briefing 2026-06-13); FCF only $10.1B in Q1, buybacks shrinking, depreciation accelerating; $85B raise = "Mag7 morphing from cash machines into capital-intensive giants — weighs on multiples regardless of top-line" (Vital Knowledge, briefing 2026-06-02). Net share issuance historically tied to lower forward returns (Spectra/Donnelly, briefing 2026-06-03).
    • Talent / model risk — Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer leaving for OpenAI (<2 yrs after the $2.7B Character.AI deal) — near-term DeepMind overhang (Rothschild/Adley, Buy, briefing 2026-06-18). MoE/TPU competitiveness vs. NVDA GPUs questioned (firstadopter, briefing 2026-06-09).
    • Regulatory — UK CMA ordering Search-ranking disclosure (6mo) + data portability (3mo); ongoing US antitrust on ad-tech, Search distribution, Android/Play (10-K FY25). YouTube exposed to UK under-16 social ban.
  • Where the sell-side stands: Broadly constructive-to-bullish; PTs cluster $405-450 (Barclays $405 OW, Citi $447 Buy, GS $450). MS OW but relative-underweight within MAG on valuation/IPO-supply. Rothschild & Redburn Buy. No bear-rated desk in the inbox.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2 FY26 print (~mid-July 2026) — first read on TPU-hardware external revenue recognition + C27 capex quantification.
  • C27 capex number — guided "significant increase"; Street already at ~$300B (MS). Magnitude vs. FCF/dilution is the swing factor.
  • External TPU / merchant-silicon ramp — Blackstone TPU JV (500MW by '27), neocloud-style structures (MS Credit, briefing 2026-05-22); Samsung foundry engagement for Axion (~2028) + TPU I/O die work as TSMC advanced nodes tighten (Rothschild, briefing 2026-06-18; Jefferies on TPU v10 "Icefish," 2026-06-12).
  • Gemini app monetization / ads-in-AI-Mode — management "not rushing"; format that works in AI Mode expected to port to Gemini app (Q1 FY26).
  • Apple/Gemini deal economics — GOOG as Apple preferred cloud provider + powering next-gen AFM (Q4 FY25); economics TBD.
  • Waymo — 500k+ autonomous rides/week, 11 US cities, $16B round; international (UK/Japan) expansion.

Risks

  • Search ad concentration — bulk of revenue/profit from advertising; AI-driven query-behavior shift could pressure CTR/CPC mix even as volume grows (10-K FY25; "intense competition").
  • Antitrust / regulatory remedies — active US suits on ad-tech, Search distribution, Android/Play Store; adverse outcomes could force structural/behavioral remedies (10-K FY25). UK CMA Search/data-portability orders.
  • Capex digestion / depreciation drag — accelerating depreciation + energy/DC opex pressure P&L; ROIC depends on backlog converting and AI monetization scaling.
  • Compute supply chain — power, land, advanced-node (TSMC) and component constraints; near-term compute-constrained cloud.
  • Frontier-model / talent leakage — model leadership not guaranteed (Shazeer departure, MoE-vs-TPU debate); LLM leapfrogging risk.
  • YouTube brand-ad softness and FX (tailwind moderating to ~1pt in Q2 from 3pt in Q1).

Consensus estimates (BBG)

META — Meta Platforms, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\META (filings + transcripts) · _equity_calls · _briefings\by-ticker\META.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Family of Apps (Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Threads, Messenger) reaches 3.5B+ daily users; ~98% of revenue is advertising. Reality Labs (Quest, AI glasses, Horizon) is the secondary, loss-making segment. The stock is now a hyperscaler/AI-capex name as much as a digital-ads name: META is building frontier models inside Meta Superintelligence Labs (MSL), spending on NVDA GPUs + AMD + Broadcom custom silicon (>1GW own ASIC rolling out per Q1'26 call), and signing multi-year cloud deals. Chain position: ad platform + AI hyperscaler / GPU + ASIC buyer + power/data-center demand driver.

Current state (latest quarter — Q1 FY26, reported 2026-04-29)

  • Revenue $56.3B, +33% y/y (+29% cc); FoA ad revenue $55.0B, +33%. Impressions +19%, price/ad +12% (Q1 FY26 call, Susan Li). Strongest growth among Mag7 ex-GPU sellers.
  • Operating income $22.9B, 41% margin. EPS $10.44 GAAP — but $8.03B one-time tax benefit inflates it; clean EPS $7.31 (partially relieving the $15.93B '25 R&D-capitalization tax charge) (Q1 FY26 call).
  • Capex $19.8B in Q1; FY26 capex guide RAISED to $125–145B (from $120–135B), driven mostly by higher memory/component pricing, not more gigawatts (Q1 FY26 call; corroborated by Justin Post, BofA, 2026-06-11 equity call). FY26 total opex guide unchanged $162–169B; OI expected above FY25.
  • Q2 FY26 revenue guide $58–61B (~2% FX tailwind). Tax rate 13–16% remaining quarters.
  • AI/products: Muse Spark (first MSL model) shipped, now powering Meta AI across the family + standalone app; double-digit % lift in Meta AI sessions/user. Muse Spark NOT yet in the ads/recommendation stack — so the 33% growth is pre-AI-product (emphasized by bull on the BofA call, 2026-06-11). Adaptive ranking (trillion-param-class inference) live; GenAI ad-creative tools at 8M+ advertisers; business AIs 10M weekly convos (up from 1M YTD); value-optimization suite >$20B run-rate.
  • Reality Labs: Q1 rev $402M (-2%); FY25 op loss tracked ~$4B/qtr. Zuckerberg framed FY25 as peak RL losses, pivoting RL spend to glasses/wearables; glasses daily users tripled y/y (Q4 FY25 call, 2026-01-28).
  • Contractual commitments stepped up $107B in Q1 from multi-year cloud + infra purchase deals (Q1 FY26 call). Headcount 77.9k (-1% q/q); ~10% RIF executed in May (~8k) per Susan Li / Nowak.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull (Brian Nowak, MS — OW, PT $775; equity call 2026-05-08): META is his #1 Mag7 pick. Core moat is brute distribution — 3.5B DAU, ~1.5hrs/day in US, time-spent growing double digits; "a deep freaking moat." Catalysts: ship a "Meta Claw"/Muse-powered personal+shoppable agent (not priced; stock at ~15-16x is "saying he's burning money in a hole"); ad rev can stay high-20s and grow ~20-25% in '27 on core algo improvements alone before any AI product. Neocloud "backup optionality" = leasing excess capacity at ~$10B/GW = 8%+ upside to '28 EPS (MS note, briefing 2026-05-18). The MS hyperscaler-capex bull: convert capex to revenue/margin and you get EPS upside + a multiple re-rate. BofA (Justin Post) Reit. Buy, PT $835 (morning briefing 2026-06-16).
  • Bear (from BofA equity call 2026-06-11, multiple buy-side PMs): (1) Capital intensity + lack of visibility — capex from ~$30B to ~$150B for ~10pts of incremental growth; incremental ROIC deeply negative, and the MSL lab is a hidden ~$40B/yr loss inside the P&L by '27 (per "super-bull" framing, Grant). (2) "Trust-the-founder" fatigue with three more megacap AI IPOs coming for the same capital. (3) No enterprise/cloud story to drive a re-rate the way GOOGL's TPU narrative did — "earnings beats won't re-rate it; needs a real AI story." (4) Equity-raise overhang ("tens of billions," FT, Jun 5) seen as the near-term clearing event everyone wants resolved (Keith, same call). (5) Morale/talent: CTO Bosworth said internal morale "almost the worst it's ever been"; Head of Product for AI-for-Work resigned 2 months in; token-tracking + keystroke surveillance (Rothschild/Rowan Adley — Buy, briefing 2026-06-18; Nowak 2026-05-08).
  • Where the sell-side stands: Broadly constructive on rating, frustrated on near-term setup. MS OW $775 (Nowak), BofA Buy $835 (Post), Redburn/R&Co Buy "Top Idea" (Adley, briefing 2026-05-26). Bernstein (Shmulik/Seidman) monitoring/neutral. The 21-page MS thesis ("4 Products to Turn Meta Back into an AI Winner," briefing 2026-06-03) frames each lever (AI Search $10-30B TAM, subs, core ad upside, Neocloud) as +$1-3 to '28 EPS. Buy-side positioning split: Ackman (Pershing ~$1.6B), Tiger (~$1.9B), Loeb adding on the capex-guide selloff (briefings 2026-05-16/18). Stock de-rated to ~16-17x PE vs. historical low-20s (BofA call).

Catalysts / what to watch

  • September developer conference — Nowak: if no shipped agentic products by Sept, "we will be wrong" (2026-05-08). Watch consumer adoption of a shoppable Meta AI agent.
  • Equity-raise decision — resolution is the near-term clearing event (BofA call 2026-06-11).
  • Next print (Q2 FY26) — does ad rev hold high-20s; FY27 capex/opex framing ($200B vs $210B opex debate, Nowak); possible second RIF in 2H (Nowak hopes for it → bull case to ~$40 '27 EPS).
  • MSL model cadence — proof of a leading model; Muse integration into ads/reco stack (Gem/Andromeda/Silver Torch).
  • Custom silicon — >1GW Broadcom ASIC ramp + AMD; ~$13B AVGO XPU by FY28 (briefing 2026-05-31). Read-through: NVDA, AVGO, MRVL, AMD; power/DC (VRT, ETN, GEV, PWR, WMB).

Risks

  • Capex/ROIC: capital intensity (~$125-145B FY26) with no contractual revenue backlog vs. peers; incremental ROIC questioned hard by buy-side (BofA call). FY27 free-cash-flow estimates likely too high.
  • Ad-revenue concentration: ~98% of revenue is advertising; macro/demand and time-spent competition are existential (10-K 2026-01-29 risk factors; Nowak's "competing for how you waste your time").
  • Regulatory/legal: US youth-safety trials in 2026 ("may ultimately result in a material loss" — Susan Li, Q1 FY26); EU privacy/DSA headwinds; UK under-16 social ban (briefing 2026-06-16). 10-K flags privacy, antitrust, content regulation.
  • Talent/execution: model gap vs. OpenAI/Anthropic; morale strain post-RIF; key AI departures (Rothschild 2026-06-18).
  • RL drag: ~$4B/qtr operating loss; wearables thesis unproven at scale.
  • Founder control: super-voting structure limits shareholder recourse (BofA call).

Consensus estimates (BBG)

ORCL — Oracle Corporation

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\ORCL (filings + transcripts + decks) · _equity_calls · _briefings\by-ticker\ORCL.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

🔖 Themes: Tokenmaxxing / AI Monetization

Snapshot

Oracle is now a full-stack enterprise tech platform spanning (1) cloud infrastructure — OCI, including the GPU/AI "AI infrastructure" buildout; (2) cloud applications — Fusion ERP/SCM/HCM/CX, NetSuite, plus vertical/industry suites; and (3) the Oracle Database franchise, increasingly sold cross-cloud ("multicloud" on Azure/Google/AWS). The story has shifted decisively from a seasonal license business to a hyperscaler-scale, capital-intensive AI infrastructure provider riding committed customer demand. FY26 total revenue surpassed $67B (+16% non-GAAP op income), but the equity is now valued — and debated — almost entirely on the OCI/AI backlog ramp. Oracle co-CEOs Clay Magouyrk (infrastructure) and Mike Sicilia (apps/database) run the business; Larry Ellison remains Chairman/CTO; Hilary Maxson took over as CFO ~June 2026.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q4 FY26 (2026-06-10) — record quarter. Revenue $19.2B, +21% USD (Maxson). Cloud infrastructure revenue +93%; Cloud Apps +10% ($4.1B, SaaS deferred +16%); cloud database +29% with multicloud revenue +404% YoY and bookings +325%. Non-GAAP op income $8.6B (+22%); non-GAAP EPS $2.11 (+24%, or +20% ex one-time investment gains). FY26: revenue >$67B, non-GAAP op income $29B (+16%), non-GAAP EPS $7.63 ($6.83 ex Ampere/Bloom gains); CFO $32B (+54%). Gross margin stepped down ~5pts FY26 as expected on data-center ramp, offset by opex efficiency (Maxson).

The headline is RPO of $638B, +363% YoY (Maxson) — 12% recognized in next 12 months, 34% in 13–36 months, both expected to accelerate. Magouyrk: signed $67B in AI infrastructure contracts in the quarter, majority bring-your-own-hardware (BYOH) or prepaid; BYOH/prepaid book now $75B at "no degradation in margin." Delivered >1.2GW in FY26; Q1 FY27 delivery "approaching 1GW," ~= prior four quarters combined. Global GPU utilization 97.5%; of 35k GPUs up for renewal across 59 customers, 49% of customers renewed 92% of GPUs and most non-renewed GPUs were resold same quarter. Five flagship sites detailed (Abilene TX 42% delivered; Shackelford TX; Doña Ana NM on Bloom fuel cells; Saline County MI; Port Washington WI).

Guidance. FY27: total revenue +34% cc (above the 5-yr CAGR); non-GAAP EPS $8.05, +18% cc ex one-time gains; net cash outlay for capex ~$70B (reported capex higher by ~$20–25B of prepayments/timing) — reaffirms Analyst Day +31% revenue CAGR / +28% EPS CAGR through FY30 (Maxson). Q1 FY27: total revenue +27–29% USD, cloud +58–64%, non-GAAP EPS $1.72–1.76 (+17–20%); H2-weighted as capacity comes online. Funding: ~$40B debt+equity in FY27 incl. the announced $20B ATM equity; no additional debt in CY26. Steady-state project ROIC "high 20s," better on BYOH/prepaid (Maxson, Q4 FY26). Prior quarter (Q3 FY26, 2026-03-10): RPO $553B, AI infra revenue +243% YoY, multicloud DB +531%; delivered >400MW (90% on/ahead of schedule); AI-capacity gross margin 32% vs 30%+ guide; database multicloud now live across 33 Azure / 14 Google / scaling to 22 AWS regions; TikTok US separation gave Oracle a 15% equity stake.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: RPO/backlog is the entire bull case — $638B with contractual visibility, "the big get bigger" hyperscaler demand (Jefferies/GEV power call, 2026-05-08, noting Oracle's "half a trillion" backlog tracking Microsoft/Google). BYOH/prepaid model lets Oracle scale capacity without proportional cash capex or margin loss (Magouyrk, Q4 FY26). "Halo effect" — AI infra training colocated with apps/database drives multiproduct deals, sovereign/Alloy and multicloud DB at 60–80% margins (Sicilia, Q3 FY26). Execution proof point: DTE/Michigan site Oracle pulled a year ahead of a schedule DTE thought aggressive — a rare data-center acceleration vs an industry full of delays (Paul, Jefferies call 2026-05-08). Sell-side supportive: UBS (Keirstead) PT↑ to $285 "positive ahead of print, long-compute thesis" (briefing 2026-06-02); DB (Zelnick) Buy/$300 reaffirmed (briefings 2026-06-01/05/15).
  • Bear: Capex-to-sales "at dotcom peak" (MS / Wigg, briefing 2026-06-09) — the single most cited risk. FY27 gross margin steps down again on data-center ramp/mix before recovering; near-term margin compression is real (Maxson). Massive external funding need (~$40B FY27, incl. $20B ATM equity dilution) at a time component costs — memory, SSD, HDD — are spiking (DiFucci/Guggenheim Q&A, Q4 FY26). Competitive flood: Neoclouds, SpaceX, CoreWeave "sold out at $99B backlog," hyperscalers self-building (Moerdler/Bernstein Q&A; Jefferies call). Power/transmission and construction-labor constraints are the gating factor industry-wide (Jefferies/GEV 2026-05-08). Stock was -13.8% the week into the 6/15 briefing despite the Buy reiterations — the market is voting on the cash-burn/margin profile, not the backlog.
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: Clustered bullish but watching margins. DB (Zelnick) Buy, PT $300 — "margins under microscope," modeled 5th straight OCI accel quarter (~+89% YoY vs Street ~+92%), ~400MW/qtr (briefings 2026-06-01 → 2026-06-15, incl. post-virtual-IR "strong cloud DB/RPO positioning"). UBS (Keirstead) Buy, PT $285 (raised from $250, 2026-06-02). No bear-rated note in the on-disk set; the bear case lives in the MS capex-intensity flag and the price action.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q1 FY27 print: September 10, 2026 (AMC) — first look at the H2-weighted acceleration; watch cloud +58–64% guide delivery and capacity-online cadence.
  • Oracle Investor Day: October 28, 2026, Las Vegas (AI World) — reaffirmation/update of FY30 +31% rev / +28% EPS CAGRs; new CFO Maxson's first framing of ROIC and funding.
  • Capacity ramps at the five flagship sites (Abilene remainder; Shackelford/Doña Ana/Saline/Port Washington deliveries through 1H–2H CY27).
  • Funding execution: ~$40B FY27 raise incl. $20B ATM equity; investment-grade rating preservation.
  • Component-cost / memory-price trajectory vs. the pass-through contract mechanics; BYOH/prepaid mix shift (only ~12% of RPO is non-BYOH/prepaid per Materne/Evercore Q&A).

Risks

  • Capex intensity / cash burn — reported capex well above the ~$70B net outlay; funding via debt+equity, ATM dilution; capex-to-sales at dotcom-peak levels (MS, 2026-06-09).
  • Margin compression through the build phase before contracted revenue ramps; gross margin down ~5pts FY26, further step-down guided FY27 (Maxson).
  • Component/supply-chain cost inflation (memory/SSD/HDD) — mitigated by fixed-price-only-when-costs-known + pass-through clauses, but a live concern (Magouyrk, Q4 FY26).
  • Customer concentration / renewal — large AI deals concentrated; 4 customers each >$8B in Q4; renewal economics depend on multi-year service relationships (Magouyrk).
  • Competition & overcapacity — Neoclouds, hyperscaler self-build, new entrants pressuring price/share (Q4 FY26 Q&A).
  • Power/grid, construction labor and data-center delays — industry-wide gating constraints (Jefferies/GEV power call, 2026-05-08).
  • FX — large international mix; ~$2.4B earnings sensitivity to a 10% FX move (10-K FY25, Item 7A); credit-rating/leverage risk from the financing program.

Consensus estimates (BBG)

NOW — ServiceNow, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\NOW (filings + transcripts + decks) · _briefings\by-ticker\NOW.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Fidelity flag: the two transcripts used (Q1-26, Q4-25) are third-party reconstructions (fool.com / insidermonkey via WebFetch) — close paraphrase of the source, not verbatim, with quotation marks only where the source has them. Treat the numbers as directional, not as exact quotation.

Snapshot

ServiceNow is a cloud-based enterprise workflow platform, positioned as an "AI control tower" / platform consolidator — not a single-feature SaaS (McDermott, Q4-25, 2026-01-28). Its historical core is ITSM (the "CIO's system of record"), expanded into employee experience, AI-native CRM, AI security and workflow data fabric. The model is ~100% subscription. The FY25 10-K (2026-01-29) describes the mission as helping organizations "govern, secure and manage artificial intelligence" and digitize workflows on top of the ServiceNow AI Platform. McDermott frames a TAM of $600bn (Q1-26). Recent M&A (Moveworks, Armis, Veza, Pyramid Analytics) broadened employee works, AI security/identity and analytics.

Current state (Q1 FY26, call 2026-04-22)

Beat-and-raise. Subscription revenue $3.671bn (+19% y/y); cRPO +21% y/y (~100bps above the guide); RPO $27.7bn (+23.5%); FCF margin 44% (Mastantuono, Q1-26). McDermott cited a ~75bps headwind from delayed on-prem deals — "beat and raise, not an excuse."

  • FY26 guidance raised: subscription revenue lifted $205m to $15.735–$15.775bn (+20.5%–21%) (Mastantuono, Q1-26). Compare with the initial guide given at Q4-25 of $15.53–$15.57bn (+19.5%–20% cc) and an op margin target of 32% / FCF margin of 36% (Mastantuono, 2026-01-28).
  • AI monetization / pricing shift: the target for AI-specific commitments for 2026 was raised from $1bn to "$1.5 billion" — +50% (Mastantuono/McDermott, Q1-26). "50% of net new business now comes from non-seat-based pricing" (McDermott, Q1-26). Now Assist surpassed "$600,000,000 in ACV" with 35 deals >$1m in Q4 (McDermott, Q4-25). The model is hybrid (seats + assist packs / tokens), not pure consumption — customers want "flexibility and predictability"; pure consumption only where it makes sense (storage, workflow data fabric tokens) (Zavery, Q4-25).
  • Margins / LLM cost: the gross margin headwind comes from migrating more to hyperscalers, with initially lower margin — "margin business you'd want me to take every single day" (Mastantuono, Q4-25). Inferencing is still "very low as a percentage of cost or usage-wise"; frontier models are expected to dominate inference over the long term, with ServiceNow LLMs serving sovereign/on-prem (Zavery, Q4-25). Q4-25 op margin 31% (+1pt vs guide), FCF margin 57% (+950bps y/y).
  • Quality metrics (Q4-25): 244 deals >$1m NNACV; 98% renewal rate; 603 customers >$5m ACV; customers >$20m ACV +30% y/y; MAU +25%; global gov "+80% y/y" in EMEA/Asia.
  • M&A: Moveworks integrated in 3 weeks, already closing deals >$1m NN ACV and "quintupling" the Q1 business vs the entire prior year (McDermott, Q1-26). Krishna Gidwani took over M&A integration as "#1 priority" (Mastantuono, Q1-26). Armis ~1pt of contribution to subscription, up to 50bps of op margin headwind (Mastantuono, Q4-25). McDermott: "not have a large-scale M&A on the roadmap" going forward (Q4-25). Capital: new $5bn buyback authorization + immediate $2bn ASR (Q4-25).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: context/data moat — 22 years, "95 billion workflows and more than 7 trillion transactions"; one CIO said it would need to be "at least 10x more expensive" to replace (McDermott, Q1-26, 2026-04-22). AI reinvigorates the core (ITSM gains importance as AI complexity rises), it does not cannibalize (McDermott responding to Borges/GS, Q1-26). A platform open to any model "aligned to customers' policies, permissions and rules" wins in an environment of AI confusion (McDermott, Q1-26). BofA reinstated at Buy, PO $130, thesis "AI beneficiary, entrenched workflow position" (morning briefing, 2026-05-19).
  • Bear: (1) Risk of core pricing cannibalization as AI reduces seats — a premise Borges/GS raised and management rejected (Q1-26). (2) Negative stock reaction post-print despite good numbers; Keith Weiss/MS questioned when NOW would show acceleration comparable to "AI platforms" (Q1-26) — the "AI tax" debate on application software. (3) M&A cadence (Moveworks, Armis, Veza) generated "investor concern" about dependence on acquisitions vs organic growth (Samana/Jefferies, Q4-25); Veza/Pyramid contribution was minimal in Q1. (4) Structural gross-margin headwind from hyperscalers/LLM cost (Mastantuono, Q4-25). (5) Deals "more complex... longer, more expensive sales cycles, increased pricing pressure" (Risk Factors, 10-K FY25).
  • Where the sell-side stands: broad and constructive coverage on the calls — JPMorgan (Murphy), Deutsche (Zelnick), Goldman (Borges), Morgan Stanley (Weiss/Zukin), Jefferies (Samana), Wells Fargo (Turrin), AllianceBernstein (Weed), RBC (Hedberg), Oppenheimer (Schwartz), JMP (Walravens). Only PO on disk: BofA Buy $130 (briefing 2026-05-19). No equity calls attributed in the INDEX.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2 FY26 print (~Jul/2026, by historical cadence — Q2-25 was 2025-07-23). Watch: traction of the $1.5bn AI commitments target and the non-seat-based mix (already at 50% of net new).
  • Financial Analyst Day 2026-05-04 (deck on disk, NOW_Financial-Analyst-Day_2026-05-04.pdf) — not read here; likely a reset of medium-term targets.
  • Integration/revenue contribution of Moveworks, Armis (closed early 2026), Veza, Pyramid.
  • Pricing evolution: proof of agentic monetization (Now Assist / autonomous workforce — "Level 1 specialist resolves in <20 min vs 2 days", Zavery Q1-26) without erosion of the core seat-based business.
  • Gross margin trajectory as workloads migrate to hyperscalers.

Risks

  • Incorporating AI brings "operational, legal, regulatory, ethical" challenges (Risk Factors, 10-K FY25, 2026-01-29).
  • Failure to innovate in an "intensely competitive" market could hurt competitive position.
  • Growing dependence on a partner network for part of revenue.
  • More complex deals → longer/more expensive sales cycles + pricing pressure.
  • Exposure to the public sector / regulated entities (procurement, contracting) and to trade/sanctions/anti-corruption laws.
  • M&A: risk of failing to realize expected benefits, management distraction, dilution.
  • Cybersecurity / data-localization and regulatory expectations around data use and movement.

Anthropic — AI lab (private)

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · Private company — no public financials (no ticker, no SEC filing, no BBG consensus). Reference/ecosystem page. Sources: internal corpus (_equity_calls, _briefings, research_library) + web + company IR/press. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Anthropic is a frontier AI lab founded 2021 by ex-OpenAI researchers (Dario & Daniela Amodei). It builds the Claude model family — Haiku (fast/cheap), Sonnet (balanced), Opus (premium), and Fable 5 (the new top tier above Opus, released June 2026) — plus the Claude Code agentic coding harness and an enterprise API distributed through AWS Bedrock and Google Vertex (web: Tygart Media, 2026-06-10). Positioning vs peers: Anthropic anchors on enterprise + safety + coding/agents; OpenAI leans consumer/permissive (companionship tokens, ChatGPT scale); Google/Gemini leans cost-control + distribution via Workspace and TPU scale (Allen Roush AI-coding call, 2026-04-28, _equity_calls; BG2Pod, 2026-06-12, research_library). As of June 2026 Anthropic is the most-used AI vendor among US businesses (~41% of firms vs OpenAI ~39.5%) per Ramp data (Big Technology podcast w/ Ramp's Ara Kharazian, 2026-06-18, research_library).

Position in the value chain

Anthropic sits in the middle of the stack: it rents compute from cloud/chip suppliers, trains frontier Claude models, and monetizes them as tokens through API/agentic products and partner clouds. The defining structural fact is the deep Amazon relationship — AWS is Anthropic's primary cloud, Amazon holds a large equity stake (~$13B committed + a ~$20B option, plus a ~$25B Mississippi cluster build), and the Anthropic CFO has said Claude runs fungibly across AWS Trainium, Google TPU and NVIDIA GPUs — the only frontier model on all three clouds (AMZN by-ticker briefing roll-up, _briefings; @EugeneNg via Anthropic CFO Krishna Rao). Google is both a backer and a TPU supplier (Stratechery — Thomas Kurian, 2026-04-23).

Private snapshot

FieldDatapointSource
Founded2021 (Dario & Daniela Amodei, ex-OpenAI)company background
Last known valuation$965B post-money (Series H, May 2026); confidential IPO filing 2026-06-01Anthropic Series H; CNBC, 2026-05-28
Prior round$30B Series G @ $380B post-money (Feb 2026)Anthropic Series G
ARR / run-rate~$40–47B run-rate, June 2026 (vs ~$9B end-2025, ~$30B early-Apr-2026); turning profitableweb: TechCrunch, 2026-05-28; corpus: @EugeneNg "$40bn ARR, overtook OpenAI's $28bn" (_briefings 2026-06-18)
Key investorsAmazon (~$13B + $20B option), Google, Samsung (Series H infra partner)AMZN by-ticker roll-up (_briefings); @qq_timmy on Samsung Series H
Flagship modelsFable 5 (top, $10/$50 per MTok), Opus 4.8 ($5/$25), Sonnet 4.6 ($3/$15), Haiku 4.5 ($1/$5); + Mythos (cyber, unreleased)web: Tygart Media, 2026-06-10

State of play

  • Revenue: Run-rate scaled from ~$9B (end-2025) → ~$30B (early-Apr) → ~$40–47B (May–Jun 2026), with Anthropic overtaking OpenAI (~$28B) on ARR; the two together ≈89% of the ~$80B AI-startup ARR pool (TechCrunch, 2026-05-28; @EugeneNg, _briefings 2026-06-18). Targeting ~$26B+ recognized 2026 revenue and first operating profit on a ~130% surge (TipRanks).
  • Enterprise/API mix: Ramp data — Anthropic now the most-used AI vendor in US businesses (~41%), having overtaken OpenAI in May 2026; ~80% of business revenue is token-based, not subscription; per-token enterprise API is profitable while consumer subs ($20/$200) lose money (Big Technology w/ Ramp, 2026-06-18; Allen Roush, 2026-04-28). Models are sticky despite frequent compute-driven outages — Claude Code workflow lock-in outweighs reliability gaps.
  • Model releases (June 2026): Fable 5 is SOTA on nearly all benchmarks, 1M-token context, always-on adaptive thinking; Kaparthi flagged long-running task capability as the real differentiator (BG2Pod, 2026-06-12). Opus 4.8 is the workhorse. Mythos (cyber-capable) is unreleased — too dangerous/too compute-heavy to serve broadly (BTG Mythos expert, 2026-06-18).
  • Compute commitments: AWS primary cloud + Trainium/Bedrock anchor driving AWS margin expansion vs Azure/GCP (SemiAnalysis, 2026-05-27, research_library); ~$25B Mississippi cluster; multi-cloud across Trainium/TPU/GPU. xAI/SpaceX struck a compute deal with Anthropic monetizing at ~$22–23B/GW/yr (BG2Pod, 2026-06-12). JPMorgan's Harlan Sur has flagged an open question on whether Broadcom is involved in Anthropic ASIC design (_briefings 2026-06-18).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: Enterprise + coding leadership is real and compounding — #1 US business share (Ramp, 2026-06-18); Claude Code is the sticky agentic harness that won the coding workflow from Cursor/Copilot; token-based API economics are profitable and AI spend is the fastest-growing cost category Ramp has ever tracked (+15x Jan-2025→May-2026). Frontier value accrual is decisive — frontier models capture ~90% of economic value even as open-source captures the majority of tokens (BG2Pod, 2026-06-12). Safety brand is a feature for regulated enterprises, and the Fable-5/DoD "supply-chain risk" episodes paradoxically accelerated adoption (Big Technology, 2026-06-18).
  • Bear: Capital intensity / compute cost — chronic compute constraints cause persistent outages ("models down all the time"); Mythos can't be served economically (Allen Roush, 2026-04-28; Big Technology, 2026-06-18). Competition — GPT-5.5 has closed most of the gap with Opus at the top; Google is the underrated cost-control winner (multi-revenue model lets it route cheaply / loss-lead); xAI/SpaceX + Cursor data is a credible new frontier-coding entrant (BG2Pod, 2026-06-12). Model substitution / token-cost control — enterprises increasingly route to cheaper/open-source models (DeepSeek fastest-growing vendor, off a tiny base); neither Anthropic nor OpenAI offer spend-management controls, the #1 enterprise complaint (Big Technology, 2026-06-18). Tokenmaxxing overhang — alleged Amazon "tokenmaxxing" to pad Claude ARR (~$500M mystery bill) raises ARR-quality questions (@zerohedge via AMZN roll-up, _briefings).
  • Where ARR leadership stands: Anthropic overtook OpenAI on ARR/business-share in May–Jun 2026 and is turning profitable; the live debate is durability vs frontier-substitution and whether compute supply can keep pace.

What to watch

  • Funding / IPO: Confidential IPO filing (2026-06-01) at ~$965B — terms, lock-ups, public-market reception (BG2Pod treats Anthropic as "quasi-public" given serial liquidity).
  • Compute: Mississippi cluster ramp; Trainium share within Anthropic mix; xAI/SpaceX and Google TPU commitments; any Broadcom ASIC confirmation (Harlan Sur question).
  • Model launches: Mythos public release timing/safeguards; cadence of Fable/Opus successors; long-running-task benchmarks.
  • Regulation: Fable-5 / Mythos export concerns — White House export controls on Fable-5 access (June 2026), DoD "supply-chain risk" label, JPMorgan cutting Hong Kong staff access, India debate; Jefferies (W. Beavington, 2026-06-18) frames the controls as "likely unsustainable." Government equity-stake-in-AI discussions name Anthropic explicitly (_briefings 2026-06-18/19).

Sources

  • BG2Pod — SpaceX IPO, Fable 5, AI Capex (2026-06-12)
  • Big Technology — AI Fact or Fiction / Ramp (2026-06-18)
  • BTG — Mythos expert (2026-06-18)
  • Allen Roush — AI coding (2026-04-28)
  • AMZN briefing roll-up — Anthropic stake / Trainium / Bedrock / Mississippi DC (../_briefings/by-ticker/AMZN.md)
  • Daily briefings — ARR leadership, Fable-5 export controls, govt equity stakes (E:\briefings\2026\2026-06-18-company-specific.md, 2026-06-19-company-specific.md)
  • Research-library catalog item: "Anthropic — Driving Enterprise AI" (Research PDF, 2026-04-28); "Tokenomics, Claude Mythos & Infinite Demand" (2026-04-23) — see 00_CATALOGO.md

OpenAI — AI lab (private)

Reference page · generated 2026-06-19 · Private company — no public financials (no ticker, no SEC filing, no BBG consensus). Sources: internal corpus (equity-call transcripts, briefings, research library) + web + press, attributed inline. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Snapshot

OpenAI is the frontier AI lab behind the GPT and o-series models, ChatGPT (the consumer/enterprise app), and a developer API. It sits at the top of the model layer: it consumes enormous amounts of compute (GPUs/accelerators + power), trains frontier models, and monetizes them through (i) ChatGPT subscriptions (consumer Plus/Pro + enterprise/Team), (ii) API/platform usage, and (iii) large one-off licensing/infrastructure deals with enterprises and governments. Flagship model is GPT-5.5 ("Spud"), released 2026-04-23, positioned as the frontier model for complex professional/agentic work (OpenAI, "Introducing GPT-5.5"; Wikipedia: GPT-5.5). ChatGPT reported 230M+ weekly users mid-June 2026 (@OpenAI, per 2026-06-19-company-specific.md). It is the most prominent — but no longer the dominant-by-share — player in a market it created, now contested by Google Gemini and Anthropic Claude.

Position in the value chain

OpenAI is the model/application layer sitting between compute suppliers and end demand. Historically Microsoft Azure was the exclusive compute backbone; OpenAI has since diversified — adding AWS, NVIDIA, custom silicon with Broadcom, and Stargate (SoftBank/Oracle) capacity — while Microsoft remains the largest single backer (~26.8% economic interest) and a primary distribution channel via Copilot (Alpha Spread).

Microsoft relationship: largest backer (~26.8% economic interest, ~17.6x on ~$13bn invested at the $852bn mark) and a major distribution surface via Copilot; the Azure compute exclusivity has been relaxed as OpenAI diversifies to AWS, custom Broadcom silicon, and Stargate. (Alpha Spread)

Private snapshot

FieldValueSource
Founded2015 (as non-profit research lab)press / general
StructurePublic Benefit Corporation (for-profit) — restructuring completed 2025-10-28; ~26.8% Microsoft economic interestMarketing AI Institute; Alpha Spread
Last known valuation$852bn post-money (round opened Feb-2026 at $730bn pre / $110bn, expanded to $122bn)Tech Insider
ARR / run-rate~$28bn ARR (mid-2026, FinTwit @EugeneNg per 2026-06-18); exited 2025 >$20bn annualized (CFO Friar); ~$25bn run-rate Feb-2026 (Sacra)_briefings/by-ticker repo; Sherwood; Tech Insider
Key backersMicrosoft (~26.8%), Amazon (~$50bn), NVIDIA (~$30bn), SoftBank (~$30bn), a16zTech Insider
Flagship modelsGPT-5.5 / GPT-5.5 Pro (Apr-2026); GPT-5.4, GPT-5.3-Codex; o-series reasoningOpenAI; Wikipedia

State of play

  • Revenue / burn. ARR ~$28bn mid-2026 (@EugeneNg, 2026-06-18-company-specific.md), up from a >$20bn exit-2025 annualized rate disclosed by CFO Sarah Friar (Sherwood). Against that, spending hit ~$34bn in 2025 ahead of the planned IPO (FT via @C_Barraud, 2026-06-18). Friar has repeatedly framed the company as "constantly under compute" — compute, not demand, is the binding constraint (CFO interview, 2026-06-03, research_library/00_CATALOGO.md).
  • Market share inflection. ChatGPT fell below 50% AI market share for the first time — 46.4% ChatGPT vs 27.7% Gemini vs 10.3% Claude (@Hesamation, 2026-06-18-company-specific.md). "Distribution is everything." Separately, by ARR a FinTwit read claims Anthropic ($40bn) overtook OpenAI ($28bn), the two together ~89% of an ~$80bn AI-startup ARR pool (@EugeneNg, 2026-06-18).
  • Models. GPT-5.5 ("Spud") shipped 2026-04-23 as the frontier release, with GPT-5.5/5.5 Pro in the API the next day; leaderboard claims vs Claude Opus 4.7 and Gemini 3.1 Pro on Terminal-Bench / FrontierMath (OpenAI). GPT-5.5 Instant health update brought free-tier users to "frontier Thinking" parity for health questions (@OpenAI, 2026-06-19). Note the frontier is crowded — BG2Pod (2026-06-12) put four labs on the coding Pareto frontier: OpenAI, Google (Gemini 3.1 Pro), Anthropic, xAI/SpaceX.
  • Talent. Noam Shazeer (Gemini co-lead, "Attention Is All You Need" co-author, returned to Google via the $2.7bn Character.AI deal) left Google for OpenAI (The Information, 2026-06-17/18, 2026-06-19-company-specific.md). Framed as resolving a "pretraining scaling bottleneck" (@natolambert). Sam Altman: "Noam is one of the people I have most wanted to work with since the very beginning of OpenAI. Only took 10 years."
  • Compute commitments. Tied to the ~$500bn Stargate infrastructure build with SoftBank and Oracle (CMC Markets). On the semis side OpenAI is one of two anchor gigawatt-class ASIC customers (with Anthropic) feeding Broadcom's XPU pipeline — JPM's Harlan Sur models OpenAI + Anthropic at ~15GW in F28 at ~$15-17bn/GW (AVGO call, 2026-06-05). AMD signed OpenAI to a ~6GW / ~$100bn / 160M-warrant deal alongside a parallel Meta deal (AMD callback, 2026-05-06). NeoCloud/Crusoe capacity (e.g. Abilene with Oracle) is being stood up for OpenAI workloads (Rothschild, 2026-06-18).
  • Structure / IPO chatter. Now a for-profit PBC (completed 2025-10-28), which clears the path to a listing; WSJ reported groundwork for a possible Q4-2026 IPO, multiple outlets flag late-2026/early-2027 at a >$1tn valuation (Marketing AI Institute; Technerdo). Thomas Laffont's "$4T AI IPO Wave" (00_CATALOGO.md, 2026-06-04) puts OpenAI at the center of the public-market crossover.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: Category creator with the strongest consumer brand and largest weekly-active base (ChatGPT 230M+ WAU, @OpenAI 2026-06-19); frontier model leadership re-asserted with GPT-5.5 (OpenAI); deep capital base (~$852bn round, Tech Insider) and diversified compute (Azure + AWS + NVIDIA + Broadcom custom + Stargate); Microsoft distribution via Copilot; revenue scaling fast (>$20bn → ~$28bn ARR inside ~6 months, Friar/Sherwood + @EugeneNg 2026-06-18); landmark talent adds (Shazeer, 2026-06-19). The frontier-token revenue accrual thesis — that economic value concentrates at the frontier even if open-source wins token volume — favors OpenAI (BG2Pod, 2026-06-12).
  • Bear: Cash burn — ~$34bn 2025 spend vs ~$28bn ARR; structurally compute-short and capital-intensive (FT @C_Barraud 2026-06-18; CFO Friar 2026-06-03). Share erosion — ChatGPT below 50% for the first time and ceding to Gemini (distribution) and Claude (enterprise/coding); Anthropic reportedly overtook on ARR (@Hesamation, @EugeneNg 2026-06-18). Enterprise pricing power risk — MSFT reportedly weighing DeepSeek to replace "expensive OpenAI/Anthropic APIs" ("Goldman's Trillion Dollar Question," @zerohedge 2026-06-18), a direct read-through to API economics. Capital intensity / circularity — gigawatt deals with AMD/Broadcom carry warrant + financing structures that have drawn circularity criticism (AMD callback, 2026-05-06). Governance/structure — PBC conversion and the Microsoft economic split remain scrutinized (Empower).

What to watch

  • Funding / IPO: confirmation/timing of a Q4-2026 (or early-2027) listing and the headline valuation (>$1tn watched). (Marketing AI Institute)
  • Compute: Stargate milestone build-out (SoftBank/Oracle), Broadcom custom-ASIC gen-2 ramp into F28, AMD MI-series gigawatt deliveries, NeoCloud (Crusoe) standups — all gated by power and chip supply.
  • Model launches: cadence beyond GPT-5.5 (5.6 rumored), o-series reasoning, and whether GPT-5.5 holds the coding/agentic Pareto frontier vs Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Opus/Mythos, and xAI Grok.
  • Share trend: does the sub-50% ChatGPT share keep sliding to Gemini/Claude; ARR vs Anthropic; and enterprise API retention if hyperscalers (e.g. MSFT) substitute cheaper models (DeepSeek).
  • Microsoft relationship: any change to the ~26.8% economic interest, Azure commitments, or Copilot distribution as OpenAI diversifies compute and approaches a listing.

Sources

  • Research library — ../../research_library/Outros/2026-06-12_BG2Pod_SpaceX-Fable5-AI-capex.md (frontier Pareto, frontier-token accrual)

AMAT — Applied Materials, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\AMAT (10-K/10-Q + transcripts) · _equity_calls (compiled AMAT dossier, 13 calls). No _briefings\by-ticker\AMAT.md and no Outlook sell-side notes available this session (see Sources). Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Applied Materials is the broadest-line wafer-fabrication-equipment (WFE) vendor — the only player spanning nearly every front-end materials-engineering step: deposition (CVD/PVD/ALD/epi), etch, CMP, ion implant, rapid thermal processing, plus metrology/inspection and advanced packaging. Two reportable segments: Semiconductor Systems 73% and Applied Global Services (AGS) 23%, with Display/Other ~4% (10-K FY2025, 2025-12-12). FY2025 (ended Oct 26, 2025) revenue $28.4B (+4% y/y), GAAP GM 48.7%, op margin 29.2%, diluted EPS $8.66. Semi-Systems end-market mix: foundry/logic 67%, DRAM 26%, NAND 7% (10-K FY2025). It sits in front-end WFE — levered to leading-edge logic (gate-all-around) and the memory/HBM buildout, with the highest pocket share in PVD and a top-tier position in DRAM WFE.

Position in the value chain

AMAT buys precision components and process materials from a broad supplier base, integrates them into the industry's widest WFE toolset (deposition / etch / CMP / epi / ion-implant / metrology), and sells to the leading-edge logic and memory fabs. It owns more of the front-end process flow than any single competitor; the one major front-end step it does not play in is lithography (ASML). Levered to leading-edge logic + DRAM/HBM capacity adds; ~25% pocket share of every DRAM WFE dollar and ~82–84% PVD share (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15).

Current state (latest quarter)

Q2 FY2026 (quarter ended Apr 26, 2026; reported 2026-05-14). Record revenue $7.91B, +13% q/q, +11% y/y (10-Q, 2026-05-21). Non-GAAP GM ~50.0% (+80 bps y/y), op margin 32.1%, record non-GAAP EPS $2.86 (+20% y/y); on a GAAP basis the 10-Q shows GM 49.9%, op margin 31.9%, diluted EPS $3.51 (GAAP boosted by a discrete tax item). Semiconductor Systems $5.97B (+10% y/y), AGS $1.67B (+17% y/y, record). Market mix: foundry/logic 67%, DRAM 29%, NAND 4%; China 27% of total revenue (10-Q, 2026-05-21). FCF $210M — depressed by elevated capex ($635M) for the EPIC Center; returned $765M ($365M div + $400M buyback).

The print's signal was the raised CY guide: equipment growth lifted to ">30% this calendar year" (from ">20%" at Q1, 2026-02-12), with leading-edge foundry-logic + DRAM + advanced packaging driving 80%+ of WFE growth in 2026–27 (Q2 FY26, 2026-05-14). Q3 FY26 guide: revenue $8.95B ±$500M (+23% y/y), non-GAAP EPS $3.36 ±$0.20 (+36% y/y). Management reiterated unprecedented visibility — customers running "rolling 8-quarter forecasts" — and cited agentic-AI workloads as incremental demand. The key debate the print created: ">30%" implies shipments barely grow in CalQ3/Q4 after ~+$900M sequential adds in April and July, which Arcuri (UBS) read as deliberate conservatism (see Debate).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull:
    • WFE supercycle, AMAT a share-gainer. Arcuri (UBS) frames WFE to ~$200B in 2027 and ~$250B in 2028 (+$50B/yr); AMAT recovers ~half the 280 bps of share it lost in 2025, growing above-market in 2026–27 alongside Lam on rising depo/etch intensity (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15). Harlan (JPM) corroborates the direction, OW on all three large-cap semicaps (KLA/Lam/AMAT) (JPM, 2026-05-15 & 2026-05-22).
    • Unprecedented visibility. "Eight quarters plus of customer order backlog… the most visibility versus any prior cycles" (Harlan/JPM, 2026-05-15); Arcuri: "I've been covering this group for 30 years. I have never heard of getting eight quarters of visibility" (UBS, 2026-05-15).
    • Mix → margin. PVD is "the single highest-margin product in the entire industry," ~70%+ GM at ~82–84% share; bulk-DRAM capacity pulls systems GM up. EBIT could reach 38–40% by 2028 (Harlan/JPM, 2026-05-15).
    • Conservative guide = setup. ">30%" likely ends nearer +40%; "it is Applied being conservative… keeping a low bar" (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15).
    • Cheap on out-years. At $250B WFE, CY28 EPS power ~$35 (min $30+) implies the stock trades ~16x 2028 earnings; "right" multiple 23–25x (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15 & 2026-06-08). BofA: "Lam and AMAT are our top picks… capacity-driven cycle… perform better earlier" (BofA, 2026-04-16). Arcuri allocates the largest single position in his suggested book to AMAT (30–40¢ of every $10) (UBS, 2026-06-01).
  • Bear / risks to the thesis:
    • Crowded long. JPM spec-sales flags Applied as a "crowded long" alongside Intel/memory/foundry (JPM, 2026-06-11) — in tension with Arcuri's read that the buyside is under-positioned in equipment vs memory (UBS, 2026-05-15).
    • Memory-beta / China digestion. Semicap stocks "tend to be sensitive to memory stocks" (BofA conf wrap, 2026-06-08); ~0.9 correlation between AMAT and Micron (Arcuri, 2026-06-08). China remained ~27–30% of revenue but is digesting excess mature-node capacity, with WFE there expected lower in 2026 (10-K FY2025; Q4 FY25 call, 2025-11-13).
    • The H2 conservatism could be real, not sandbagging. AMAT credits cleanroom/floor-space bottlenecks and DRAM conversions; only 1 of 10 new fab programs is incremental to 2026, the rest 2027+ (Harlan/JPM & Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15). Gate-all-around revenue was trimmed $5B → $4.5B at Q3 FY25 on nonlinear customer commitments (2025-08-15).
    • GM step has been thin. Q4 FY25 GM up only ~10 bps disappointed; "when Gary sounds this bullish, it's time to sell" lazy heuristic dinged the stock post-print (Josh/JPM spec-sales, 2026-05-15).
  • Where the sell-side stands: Uniformly OW/Buy and a consensus semicap top pick. JPM (Harlan Sur) OW on KLA/Lam/AMAT (2026-05-15, 2026-05-22, 2026-06-05); UBS (Arcuri) AMAT his largest book position, prefers AMAT over Lam on DRAM share (2026-05-15, 2026-06-01); BofA top picks Lam + AMAT (2026-04-16). _No PT/rating from Outlook this session — mailbox not reachable; figures above are from the dated equity-call compilation._

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q3 FY2026 print (mid-Aug 2026): does the ">30%" CY guide move to +40%? Shipment trajectory into CalQ3/Q4 is the swing (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15).
  • EPIC Center launch (fall 2026): capex steps down once it opens → potential FCF/buyback unlock in H2; founding partners TSMC/Micron/Samsung/SK Hynix (Q2 FY26, 2026-05-14).
  • Advanced packaging ramp: guided >50% growth in 2026 toward a ~$2B business (~10% of systems); AMAT is the enabler for AVGO's packaging at its Singapore center, ramping H2 2027 for TPU/Tomahawk 6 (Harlan/JPM, 2026-05-15 & 2026-06-05).
  • Intel spend (mostly 2027+) and Terrafab (Texas filing, 70–80k wspm tranche 1) — "tens of billions" of WFE, not yet in numbers (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15).
  • China / export-control policy and license cadence; MATCH Act would be "modestly positive for the films guys" (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-04-06).

Risks

  • Customer concentration: two customers were ~21% and ~15% of revenue (six months ended Apr 26, 2026); two were ~19% and ~15% in FY2025 (10-Q 2026-05-21; 10-K FY2025).
  • Geographic / China: China 30% of FY2025 revenue (-16% y/y); export-control restrictions equate to >20% of China's WFE market; a $252.5M export-controls settlement accrual was taken (DOJ/SEC inquiries closed, no enforcement) (10-K FY2025; Q1 FY26 call, 2026-02-12).
  • Cyclicality / demand concentration: business is driven by lumpy capital-equipment investment by a small number of major semi manufacturers; backlog is "not necessarily indicative" of future sales and orders can be cancelled/delayed (10-K FY2025).
  • Memory-beta: stock trades with memory pricing/Micron; an AI-capex air-pocket would propagate to DRAM/HBM WFE orders.
  • Supply chain / single-source parts and rapid technology transitions (e.g., gate-all-around ramp timing) (10-K FY2025).

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • Jefferies (Will Beavington) — Global SEMIS Daily _(2026-06-16)_: AMAT named "Top pick" in the semicap complex.
  • BofA TMT sales _(2026-06-18)_: AMAT in the constructive semis tape (alongside MU/INTC) post-AAPL/Intel deal pop.

KLAC — KLA Corporation

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\KLAC (10-K/10-Q + transcripts) · _equity_calls · semicap-WFE theme. Master index: 00_INDEX.md.

Snapshot

KLA is the dominant process-control vendor in wafer-fab equipment — the inspection, metrology and defect-review tools (plus the software/analytics and services around them) that catch yield-killing defects across the fab, from R&D to high-volume production. It is the clear share leader, ~7x the size of its nearest process-control competitor, with share up ~360 bps since 2021 (Q3 FY26 call, 2026-04-29). Three reportable segments: Semiconductor Process Control (the core; inspection + metrology + software), Specialty Semiconductor Process (vacuum deposition/etch for MEMS/RF/power) and PCB & Component Inspection (10-K FY25, 2025-08-08). FQ3 FY26 product mix: Wafer Inspection 51%, Patterning 18%, Specialty 4%, PCB 3%, Services 23% (10-Q, 2026-04-30). Services ~22% of revenue and a 16-year run of annual growth (10-K FY25). The model is levered to leading-edge complexity (more EUV layers, larger AI die, HBM, sub-2nm nodes lift process-control intensity) and new fab starts — and the historical tie between process-control and litho intensity "has broken down," now driven by design proliferation and large-die AI adoption (Q4 FY25 call, 2025-07-31).

Position in the value chain

KLA is the quality-control layer of the fab: it sits between precision component suppliers (optics, sensors, lasers) and the chipmakers, and is uniquely levered to early-stage ramps of new nodes — where defect density is highest and process-control intensity peaks before yields mature.

Current state (latest quarter)

FQ3 FY26 / March-quarter (reported 2026-04-29): revenue $3.415B (+4% q/q, +11% y/y), above the $3.35B midpoint; non-GAAP EPS $9.40; non-GAAP GM 62.2% (GAAP GM 61.1%, 10-Q 2026-04-30); FCF $622M (TTM $4.0B, 31% margin); capital returned $875M (Q3 FY26 call). Growth led by memory (DRAM/HBM) plus foundry/logic and rising service revenue (+16% y/y) off a larger installed base (10-Q, 2026-04-30). By region (ship-to): Taiwan 25.5%, China 24.3%, Korea 19.9% (+80% y/y on DRAM/HBM), North America 12.0%, Europe/Israel 7.2% (10-Q, 2026-04-30).

Guidance (Q3 FY26 call, 2026-04-29): FQ4 FY26 revenue $3.575B ±$200M, GM 61.75% ±1pt, non-GAAP EPS $9.87 ±$1. For CY2026: WFE market expected to exceed $140B (raised through the year from a ~low-$120B view at Q2 FY26, 2026-01-29, and mid-to-high-single-digit growth at Q1 FY26, 2025-10-30); KLA process-control systems revenue growth >20%, company-wide "high-teen percentage growth"; sequential q/q growth across the year. CY26 customer mix has shifted hard to leading edge: foundry/logic ~82%, memory ~18% of process-control systems (DRAM 84% of memory) — vs. ~60/40 at Q2 FY26 — reflecting the sub-2nm node wave (Q3 FY26 call). Advanced packaging ~$635M (2025) → ~$1B (2026E), +~70% y/y. Management committed publicly that CY2027 growth > CY2026 growth, citing backlog and "unprecedented" visibility (Q3 FY26 call) — the only large-cap to put that stake in the ground (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15). Raised the through-2030 model to a 13-17% revenue CAGR, >90% of FCF returned, +150 bps further WFE share; 17th straight dividend hike + incremental $7B buyback.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: Process-control share leader levered to exactly what's inflecting — leading-edge complexity and new-node ramps. KLA is "the only company that put a stake in the ground and said WFE growth next year is going to be stronger than this year," with better visibility than peers and exposure to sub-2nm programs firing in CY27 (TSMC A14, Intel 14A, Samsung/Rapidus 2nm expansion, initial Terafab orders) — "they're more leveraged to early-stage ramp-up of next-generation technologies" (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15). KLA's advanced-packaging business grew ~70% in 2025 on TSMC CoWoS (2.5D), where peers are under-indexed, and is ~60-70% growth again in 2026 (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15; Q3 FY26 call). BofA flagged KLA specifically as the semicap name with "very bullish commentary" that has lagged the SOX — "catch-up potential" (Scemama/BofA conference wrap, 2026-06-08). JPM segment call: CY26 favors depo/etch (LRCX/AMAT) but CY27 rotates to the new-node leaders, KLA and ASML — KLA the call for positive revisions into 2027 (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15). Demand is capacity-constrained, not capex-constrained: lines "virtually sold out" in H1, optical-component lead times the binding constraint (Q2 FY26 call, 2026-01-29).
  • Bear: Buy-side bars on WFE are "very, very high" — the group sold off on AMAT's print despite a guide-up because expectations were already there (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15); semicap stocks "do tend to be sensitive to memory stock movements" (Scemama/BofA, 2026-06-08), so a DRAM/HBM pricing wobble hits the multiple. Near-term margin is capped: elevated DRAM-component costs are a ~75-100 bps GM headwind through CY2026, plus a 50-100 bps tariff drag (Q2/Q3 FY26 calls) — GM guided down sequentially to 61.75% even on strong revenue. China is normalizing from ~39-41% peak toward mid-20s% and now "fairly immaterial" incrementally (Q1/Q3 FY26 calls); export controls cost an estimated $300-350M of revenue through end-CY2026 (Q1 FY26 call, 2025-10-30). High-NA litho pushout is a modeled-but-real timing risk (Q3 FY26 call). CY26 revisions lag peers — depo/etch intensity is higher this year, so "KLA revisions lag 2026 and come back strongly in 2027" (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15).
  • Where the sell-side stands: JPM (Harlan Sur) Overweight, KLA a top semicap pick alongside LRCX/AMAT (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15; 2026-05-22). BofA (Scemama) constructive on semicap as a group, singled KLA out as the lagging name with bullish management tone and catch-up potential (2026-06-08). No KLA-specific PT was in the equity-call repo; Outlook returned no notes (see Sources). _No live market-data access — PTs/consensus quoted only where present in a dated source._

Catalysts / what to watch

  • FQ4 FY26 print (late July 2026): June-quarter guide $3.575B / $9.87 EPS; watch the GM trajectory vs. DRAM-cost headwind and any further CY26 WFE upgrade (KLA has raised the WFE number every quarter this fiscal year: mid-to-high-single-digit → ~low-$120B → >$140B).
  • CY2027 framing: management has committed CY27 growth > CY26; watch for quantification and sub-2nm order confirmation (TSMC A14, Intel 14A, Terafab) — the Street's revision driver into 2027 (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15).
  • Advanced-packaging trajectory toward ~$1B (2026E) on CoWoS/HBM; the differentiated growth leg vs. peers.
  • China / export controls: any further BIS rule changes vs. the $300-350M already modeled; China mix settling toward mid-20s%.
  • Memory capex cadence: DRAM/HBM is the engine of the incremental WFE dollars; sensitive to memory-pricing and any greenfield NAND (first NAND greenfield not until CY28, Sur/JPM 2026-05-15). MU F3Q26 print 2026-06-24 a near-term read-through (semicap-WFE theme).

Risks

  • Customer & geographic concentration: sells to a small set of leading chipmakers; international ~89% of revenue (10-K FY25, 2025-08-08); China 24.3% of FQ3 revenue (10-Q, 2026-04-30) — directly exposed to BIS export rules and tariffs.
  • Export controls / geopolitics: BIS rules restrict sales/service to certain China customers; tariffs an ongoing 50-100 bps GM drag (10-K FY25; Q2/Q3 FY26 calls).
  • Cyclicality + memory beta: WFE is cyclical and AI-capex-dependent; semicap multiples track memory-equity moves (Scemama/BofA, 2026-06-08). Pushouts/cancellations create earnings volatility and inventory-charge risk (10-Q, 2026-04-30).
  • Near-term margin compression from elevated DRAM component costs (~75-100 bps through CY26) and slightly weaker systems mix.
  • Technology-adoption timing: delays in customer adoption of new nodes/methods, or High-NA litho slippage, can dent process-control capital intensity (10-Q, 2026-04-30; Q3 FY26 call).

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • Morgan Stanley TMT (T. Wigg) _(2026-06-15)_: ONTO (Onto Innovation) initiated Overweight — process-control/metrology peer read-through into KLAC.
  • Jefferies/BofA semicap dailies _(mid-Jun 2026)_: KLA carried in the WFE complex; process-control the differentiated CY27>CY26 leg.

TER — Teradyne, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\TER (filings + transcripts) · _equity_calls · _briefings. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Teradyne is the #2 global automated-test-equipment (ATE) vendor — a back-end semiconductor name levered to test intensity, not wafer starts. Three reportable segments: Semiconductor Test (~87% of revenue: SoC + Memory + Integrated System Test/IST), Robotics (Universal Robots cobots + MiR autonomous mobile robots, ~7%), and Product Test (board test, wireless, PIC/silicon-photonics test, defense/aerospace, ~6%) (TER 10-Q, Q1 FY26, 2026-05-01). The structural story is the swing from a mobile-driven mix to an AI-dominant one: AI-related demand was ~70% of total revenue in Q1 FY26, up from ~60% in Q4 FY25 and ~50% in Q3 FY25 (TER Q1 FY26 call, 2026-04-29). Duopoly in SoC/memory ATE vs Advantest; Robotics competes with FANUC/ABB/KUKA/Yaskawa (TER 10-K, 2026-02-19).

Position in the value chain

Back-end test. Teradyne buys instruments and handler/prober/robotics components, integrates them into ATE platforms, and sells to foundries, OSATs, IDMs, memory makers and fabless/HPC/mobile customers. Levered to the rising test intensity of complex AI chips, HBM stacks, and co-packaged optics (CPO) — more chiplets, more die-stacking and tighter latent-defect tolerance mean more test insertions per device (TER Q3 FY25 call, 2025-10-29).

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 FY26 (reported 2026-04-29, quarter ended Mar 29 2026) — a record. Total revenue $1,282.5M, +87% y/y / +18% q/q ($200M above the prior 2021 peak); GAAP diluted EPS $2.53 (vs $0.61 Q1 FY25), non-GAAP EPS $2.56; non-GAAP gross margin 60.9% (+370bps q/q); non-GAAP operating margin a record 37.5% (TER Q1 FY26 call; 10-Q 2026-05-01). GAAP gross profit $780.9M (61%), GAAP operating income $473.0M, net income $398.9M (10-Q). Segment split: Semi Test $1,110.8M (+104.8% y/y) — SoC $882M (~75% AI-compute), Memory $203M (near-record on HBM/DRAM), IST $27M; Robotics $91.3M (+32% y/y, 4th straight q/q growth); Product Test $80.4M (+8% y/y, defense/aerospace led). Geography heavily Asia: Taiwan 41%, Korea 19%, China 11% of revenue (10-Q). Guidance Q2 FY26: revenue $1.15–$1.25B, non-GAAP EPS $1.86–$2.15, GM 58–59%; H1-weighted (~55–60% of FY26 in H1). FY26 evergreen target reiterated: ~$6B revenue / $9.50–$11 non-GAAP EPS (TER Q4 FY25 call, 2026-02-03). Capital return modest: $5.5M buyback + $20.4M dividends ($0.13/qtr) in Q1 (10-Q).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull (UBS / Arcuri): Arcuri ranks TER among his top picks — "Teradyne at 320, I like that as well… that's one I like a lot," flagging it as a late-cycle laggard that hadn't ripped (UBS, weekly preview, 2026-05-18). Thesis: AI test intensity (chiplets, HBM, CPO) structurally lifts the ATE TAM; the CPO/silicon-photonics option is real — four test insertions, $300–700M mid-term TAM, first-mover via Fi-Con Tech partnership + Quantifi/Photon 100 (UBS–Teradyne CPO call, Shannon Poehlen, 2026-05-04). Merchant-GPU (Nvidia) ramp toward 30–40% share over ~3 years is upside not in numbers.
  • Bull (JPM / Samik): "sounding a bit better about memory demand being strong" → H2 upside; Google ASIC (VIP compute) qualification finishing ~end-3Q, modest 4Q revenue then ramping into 2027; Intel potential merchant-test shift is an out-year option. Net: H2 numbers should come up when they report (JPM, memory/HDD preview, 2026-06-12).
  • Bear: (1) Customer concentration — top-5 direct customers = 44% of FY25 revenue; "vast majority of tester demand driven by ~2 customers"; hyperscalers pushing dual-sourcing (10-K; TER Q3 FY25 call). (2) Visibility/lumpiness — Smith: "we don't have meaningful commitment from customers around what they'll buy and when"; 12–18-wk lead times mean no clean H2 reset on the print (TER Q1 FY26 call; JPM 2026-06-12). (3) H1-weighted year (VIP compute split ~85/15 H1/H2) → optically softer H2 (JPM, 2026-06-12). (4) Mobile soft; memory pricing/availability pressuring non-iOS demand (TER Q1 FY26 call).
  • Where the Street stands: UBS constructive (TER a top pick, ~$320 ref, 2026-05-18); JPM constructive on H2 upside (2026-06-12). No TER broker PT in the 2026-06-17 inbox (TER flagged "zero email coverage" that day) — so no fresh, attributable PT/rating in-house beyond the UBS/JPM call commentary above.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2 FY26 print (late July 2026) — H1/H2 shape, memory-demand tone, whether H2 guide ticks up (JPM expects it to).
  • Google VIP-compute qualification completing ~end-3Q FY26 → modest 4Q revenue, 2027 ramp (JPM, 2026-06-12).
  • Merchant GPU (Nvidia) multi-system production orders → multi-year ramp toward 30–40% share (TER Q1 FY26 call; JPM).
  • CPO / silicon photonics insertion mix evolution; ramp weighted to H2 (UBS CPO call, 2026-05-04). Watch SemiAnalysis CPO skepticism noted in the tape (briefing, 2026-06-17).
  • Intel possible shift from in-house to merchant test — out-year, "not imminent" (JPM, 2026-06-12).
  • Robotics "plan of record" customer ramp later 2026 + new US robotics facility (TER Q2 FY25 call, 2025-07-30).

Risks

  • Customer + geographic concentration (top-5 = 44% FY25; Taiwan/Korea/China = 71% of Q1 FY26 revenue) (10-K; 10-Q).
  • Semiconductor cyclicality / over-supply swings inherent to ATE (10-K risk factors).
  • China export-control / tariff exposure; competitors not always under the same restrictions (10-K).
  • Reliance on a small set of AI/VIP programs whose timing drives lumpy orders; tester lead-times limit forward visibility (10-K; TER Q1 FY26 call).
  • CPO/merchant-GPU TAM still early-stage; share split with Advantest/FormFactor unsettled across insertions (UBS CPO call, 2026-05-04).

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • Samik Chatterjee (JPM) preview _(2026-06-12)_: Teradyne 2H improvement "nuanced positive" — memory test demand stronger; 1H still leads 2H on VIP-compute split; Lumentum OCS-outsourcing read-through.
  • Susquehanna corporate access _(2026-06-18)_: TER in the test/semicap event calendar.

VECO — Veeco Instruments Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\VECO (filings + transcripts) · _briefings. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Internal coverage is thin — Veeco has no equity-call notes and only incidental briefing mentions in our repo; this page is built mostly from primary sources (SEC filings + earnings transcripts).

Snapshot

Veeco is a smaller-cap semiconductor process-equipment maker ($664M FY2025 revenue, -7% y/y; 10-K, 2026-02-25) that holds leading positions in a handful of niche front-end and advanced-packaging process steps rather than competing across broad WFE. Reports as one segment, sold into four end-markets: Semiconductor $477M / 72%, Compound Semiconductor $60M / 9%, Data Storage $39M / 6%, Scientific & Other $89M / 13% (FY2025; 10-K). Core franchises: Laser Spike Annealing (LSA) and next-gen Nanosecond Annealing (NSA); Ion Beam Deposition (IBD/Spectre) for EUV mask blanks, pellicles and advanced-DRAM bitline films; MOCVD/MBE epitaxy (GaN-on-Si, InP) for compound semi/photonics; and single-wafer wet processing + lithography for advanced packaging. Levered to the right secular vectors — gate-all-around (GAA) anneal, HBM/advanced DRAM, AI-driven advanced packaging, silicon-photonics InP lasers, and HAMR HDD — but with meaningful China mature-node and customer-concentration drag. CEO Bill Miller, CFO John Kiernan.

The dominant fact for the equity: VECO is being acquired by Axcelis (ACLS) in an all-stock merger. Each VECO share converts to 0.3575 Axcelis shares; combined company ~58.4% Axcelis / 41.6% Veeco fully diluted; both shareholder bases approved (2026-02-06). As of the Q1 print only one regulatory approval is outstanding — China SAMR antitrust — with close expected H2 2026 (10-K Note 17, 2026-02-25; 10-Q Note 10, 2026-05-05). VECO trades as a merger-arb / Axcelis-proxy, not a standalone fundamental story.

Position in the value chain

Veeco is a specialty process-equipment supplier sitting between precision-component/optics vendors and leading-edge chip, HDD, advanced-packaging and compound-semi manufacturers. It does not span broad WFE — it owns a few high-value niche process steps (anneal, ion-beam, epi, wet) that gate performance at advanced nodes. Smaller-cap; most levered to advanced packaging, HBM and GAA anneal.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 2026 (2026-05-05): Revenue $158M, within guide but down y/y; BIS export-license change removed ~$8M of LSA revenue from a China customer (CFO Kiernan). Non-GAAP GM ~36%, non-GAAP EPS $0.14 (GAAP near-breakeven / ($0.01) net loss, pressured by ~$2M merger costs and weaker mix — 10-Q). Mix: Semi $109M (69%), Compound Semi $19M (12%), Data Storage $10M (6%), Scientific $20M (13%). Cash & ST investments $383M. China collapsed to $20M / 13% of sales vs $71M a year ago (-72% y/y; 10-Q) as mature-node demand rolled off, offset by Rest-of-APAC $90M (+51% y/y).

Guidance: Q2'26 revenue $170–190M, GM 38–40%, non-GAAP EPS $0.20–0.32. FY2026 reaffirmed: revenue $740–800M (+11–16% y/y), non-GAAP EPS $1.50–1.85, GM 41–43% — second-half weighted, with semi ~mid-teens growth, compound semi ~+50%, and data storage roughly doubling to ~$80M (Q4'25 / Q1'26 calls). FY2025 exit backlog was $555M, +35% y/y (Q4'25 call, 2026-02-25). Biggest 2026 swing factor flagged on the call: >$250M of orders received for MOCVD + wet + IBD tools for customers' indium-phosphide laser manufacturing (silicon photonics), shipping from Q3'26 and ramping hard into Q1'27, with Spectre IBD capacity set to expand ~10x by early 2027 (CEO Miller, 2026-05-05).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull (standalone fundamentals): Multiple eval-to-production "inflection points" stacking into 2026–27 — LSA at all three Tier-1 logic customers + a leading HBM supplier, a second Tier-1 DRAM LSA eval with potential pilot/HVM orders in 2027, NSA at leading-edge logic for GAA low-thermal-budget anneal, and IBD300 at DRAM for bitline films (Q1'26 call). Advanced packaging more than doubled y/y on AI; data storage fully booked through 2026 into 2027 on HAMR; silicon-photonics InP order book (>$250M) is a genuine new growth leg. Management frames a >$5B pro-forma SAM and a "very positive WFE environment" into 2027. Internal: Citi (Atif Malik) Buy, reiterated — 2027E upside on silicon photonics (briefing, 2026-05-06).
  • Bear: Standalone economics are soft — FY2025 revenue -7%, GM compressed to 40% on mix + tariffs, Q1'26 a GAAP loss. China was 27% of FY2025 and is in structural decline (mature-node + BIS license drag). Heavy customer concentration — top-10 customers = 68% of receivables; one customer = 17% of FY2025 sales (10-K). Cyclical, sole-source-supplier-dependent, tariff-exposed cost base. As a "second provider" in epitaxy (vs Aixtron) and a niche player vs AMAT/LAM/KLAC, pricing power is limited. Most of the 2026 growth is second-half weighted and eval-dependent — execution/timing risk is high.
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: Effectively a merger-arb / Axcelis-proxy equity — the spread and the China-SAMR approval drive the stock more than the print. Internal coverage is thin: Citi (Malik) Buy reiterated (2026-05-06); a "Tom O'Malley updates post-print" mention (briefing, 2026-05-19); and a conference appearance (Jefferies Chicago Semis, Aug 24-25). No internal equity-call notes on the name.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • China SAMR antitrust approval — the last gate to the Axcelis merger; close guided H2 2026 (drop-dead Sep-30-2026, auto-extendable to Jun-30-2027 for regulatory; VECO termination fee $77.5M, Axcelis $108.7M — 10-K Note 17).
  • Q2 2026 print (~early Aug 2026): GM recovery toward 38–40%, China trajectory, and progress on the second-half-weighted FY guide.
  • Silicon-photonics InP ramp: >$250M order book — shipments from Q3'26, Spectre IBD ~10x capacity ramp into early 2027.
  • LSA/NSA eval conversions: second Tier-1 DRAM LSA → pilot/HVM in 2027; NSA at leading-edge logic for GAA; IBD300 DRAM bitline decisions (extended into 2026).
  • Data storage / HAMR: first 2026 shipment in Q2; order book into 2027.

Risks

  • Merger execution / China SAMR (10-K Note 17, 10-Q Note 10): deal could be delayed or blocked at China antitrust; standalone valuation reverts if it breaks. Management distraction / business disruption during the interim period.
  • China end-market (10-K): 27% of FY2025, structurally declining on mature-node roll-off + BIS export-license restrictions (cost ~$8M in Q1'26).
  • Customer concentration (10-K): top-10 = 68% of receivables; one customer 17% of FY2025 sales; small base of large manufacturers.
  • Cyclicality + eval dependence: WFE-cyclical, second-half-weighted FY26, growth gated on eval-to-production conversions.
  • Supply chain / tariffs (10-K, Q2'25 call): sole-source components; outsourcing risk; ~100bps tariff GM headwind; cost inflation on imported materials.
  • Competitive position: niche player vs AMAT/LAM/KLAC; "second provider" in epitaxy vs Aixtron — limited pricing power.

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • JPM Special Situations — Deal Survey of the Americas _(2026-06-12 / 06-02)_: tracks the Veeco / Axcelis all-stock merger (merger-arb / ACLS-proxy setup).
  • Crux Capital (optics, Substack) _(2026-06-19)_: names Veeco among the "upstream tool providers (Aixtron, Veeco, Riber)" levered to the compound-semi/optical build.
