AIXA — AIXTRON SE

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · German issuer (Prime Standard, Frankfurt; ticker AIXA GR) — NO SEC 10-K/10-Q/20-F. Built from earnings transcripts + Aixtron IR + BBG estimates (EUR). Internal coverage is thin — name appears only in briefing read-across, not in our filings/equity-calls corpus. Sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\AIXA\transcripts · E:\briefings\2026. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Snapshot

AIXTRON makes MOCVD / epitaxy deposition equipment (metal-organic chemical vapour deposition) for compound semiconductors — the tools that grow the III-V and wide-bandgap layers on wafers. It does not make chips; it sells the capital equipment to chipmakers. Four demand pillars: optoelectronics (lasers/EML/PIC, VCSEL, photonics for AI-datacenter optics), GaN power, SiC power, and LED/MicroLED. FY2025 revenue EUR 557m, -12% yoy, GM 40%, EBIT EUR 100m (Aixtron FY25 call, 2026-02-26). HQ Herzogenrath, Germany. Flagship platform: G10 family (G10-AsP for lasers/photonics, G10-GaN, G10-SiC). Structurally a small-cap semicap with near-monopoly niche share — historically >90% in InP/laser epitaxy, >85% in GaN epitaxy (Q3/25 call, 2025-10-30).

Position in the value chain

AIXTRON sits one step upstream of the compound-semi fabs: it buys precision mechanical, optical and gas-handling components and integrates them into epitaxy reactors, which it sells to makers of lasers, power devices and LEDs. The investable read-through runs two ways — AI-datacenter optical (800G→1600G transceivers, EML/PIC lasers; the current order driver) and 800V DC power (GaN + SiC for the NVIDIA-style grid-to-chip power chain). See theme: 800V DC power and optical / CPO.

Current state (latest quarter — Q1 FY2026, reported 2026-04-30)

A textbook "weak P&L, strong bookings" quarter. Revenue EUR 59m, -47% yoy (within EUR 65m ±10m guide) on seasonality + soft power electronics; gross margin 18% (-12pp) on negative operating leverage plus a mid-single-digit EUR m one-off personnel charge; EBIT EUR -22m (Q1/26 IR call, 2026-04-30). The signal is in orders: intake EUR 171m, +~30% yoy, >2/3 from optoelectronics — what the CEO called "a clear inflection point," driven by laser-related Datacom for AI data centers as the architecture shifts copper→optical at 800G/1600G. G10-AsP was the majority of volume orders; many multi-tool orders extend beyond 2026. Management raised FY26 guidance mid-April: revenue EUR 560m ±30m (was 520m), GM ~42%, EBIT margin 17–20%; Q2/26 revenue guided EUR 110m ±10m. Balance sheet is fortress-grade: cash EUR 273m, equity ratio 85%, and a EUR 450m 5-yr 0%-coupon convertible placed in April (accretive day-one). Mix in Q1: opto 52% of equipment rev, LED/MicroLED 31%, GaN+SiC 17%. After-sales (EUR 24m, more stable) was 40% of revenue.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull — multi-year AI-optical capex play with monopoly share. Jefferies (Beavington) raised PT to EUR 73 on 2026-06-19, modelling transceiver units ~5x 2025→2028 against AIXA's ~100% InP MOCVD share, +8%/+16% above 2028 rev/EPS consensus, with GM upside on tool volumes + rising G10 mix (E:\briefings\2026\2026-06-19-morning.md). CEO: of the top-10 AI-datacom device makers, "80%, 90%, maybe 100%" are AIXTRON customers, ~90% on G10-AsP (Q3/25 call). Optionality from 800V DC power (GaN+SiC) into 2027/28 and super-junction SiC favouring the batch reactor.
  • Bear — concentration on one cyclical wave + soft power core. Opto strength masks SiC ("down ~20pp of group mix in 2026," CEO at Q3/25 call) and only-stabilising GaN. Management itself flags opto duration as "the trillion-dollar question…how long the AI bubble will last" (CEO to BofA's Scemama, Q3/25). Optoelectronics is "extremely fragmented" and historically cyclical; FX (USD/JPY) is a recurring 1pp GM drag; Q1/26 GM of 18% shows the operating-leverage downside in trough quarters. Geostrategic risk (Iran/Middle East) explicitly called out.
  • Where the sell-side stands: Constructive into the AI-optical cycle. Jefferies Buy, PT EUR 73 (Beavington, 2026-06-19). BofA (Scemama), Oddo, UBS, Kepler, Citi, HSBC, DB all engaged on the Q3/25 call. Q1/26 read-across was tagged "positive across Optical and Power" in our briefings (2026-05-11). BBG spot EUR 59.7 (estimates.json snapshot). No internal Capstone rating on file.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2 FY2026 print — late July 2026 (revenue guided EUR 110m ±10m); first read on whether the opto order surge sustains into a second quarter.
  • Order intake / book-to-bill each quarter — the live indicator; opto >2/3 of intake in Q1/26.
  • GaN inflection — CEO expects pickup "later 2026 / H1 2027"; 300mm GaN qualifications (Infineon) flagged for "Q3/Q4 2026."
  • 800V DC power ramp — NVIDIA-style architecture → SiC/GaN volume "around 2027/28" (Rubin Ultra timing); super-junction SiC adoption.
  • Convertible use of proceeds — potential M&A / buybacks. Malaysia (Penang) site live 2027. Italy site sale in progress.
  • Conferences: Aixtron at Jefferies German & Swiss Corporate Conf (Baden-Baden, Jun 23-24) and BofA London TMT (Jun 9-11) (briefings, 2026-05-19/05-22).

Risks

  • Single-cycle dependence: opto/AI-datacom is now the swing factor; a capex pause hits orders fast. Management can't see beyond near-term visibility.
  • Power core soft: SiC overcapacity + EV slowdown; GaN digesting installed base. 2026 SiC mix down sharply.
  • Margin cyclicality: trough-quarter operating deleverage (Q1/26 GM 18%); ~1pp recurring FX (USD/JPY) drag.
  • Customer concentration / China: large SiC orders skew to China (overcapacity risk); concentrated opto customer base under NDA.
  • Geopolitics / trade: Middle East/Iran flagged; US-tariff exemption assumed but monitored; export-control exposure on advanced tools.
  • No SEC disclosure: German reporting (half-year detail, lighter quarterly statements) — less granular than US peers; modelling relies on IR decks + transcripts.

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • Jefferies (Will Beavington) — "AIXTRON: The Multi-Year OPTICAL Play" _(2026-06-19)_: Buy, PT raised to €73; US optical model has transceiver units +~5x 2025-2028; ~100% InP MOCVD share, opto >2/3 of order intake.
  • Capstone Morning Briefing _(2026-06-19)_: AIXTRON PT↑ Jefferies Buy €73, +8%/+16% vs consensus FY28 rev/EPS.
  • Jefferies Global SEMIS Daily _(2026-06-19)_: "AIXTRON — A Multi-Year A.I. Capex Play?"; Crux Capital (optics) _(2026-06-19)_ lists Aixtron among upstream optical tool providers.

LRCX — Lam Research Corporation

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\LRCX (10-K/10-Q + transcripts) · _equity_calls · semicap-WFE theme. Master index: 00_INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Lam Research is the leader in the etch + deposition legs of wafer-fab equipment — the tools that pattern and build up the 3D structures inside a chip. Product families: Deposition (ECD/SABRE, CVD/ALD tungsten & molybdenum/ALTUS, PECVD & ALD-gapfill/VECTOR, Stryker, SPEED) and Etch (conductor etch Kiyo/Versys/Acara, dielectric etch Flex/Vantex, TSV etch Syndion) plus wafer clean and the Customer Support Business Group (CSBG) — spares, upgrades, services and refurbished non-leading-edge tools (10-K FY25, 2025-08-11). Primary competitors: Applied Materials, Tokyo Electron, Hitachi in etch; AMAT, ASM International, Wonik IPS in ALD/PECVD (10-K FY25). International is the substantial majority of revenue; the two most significant customers in FY25 were Samsung and TSMC (10-K FY25). The model is levered to rising deposition/etch intensity as the industry goes 3D — 3D NAND layer-stacking, DRAM 4F²/capacitor scaling and HBM, and gate-all-around logic — and is the highest-conviction WFE share-gainer in the complex (ship-share of WFE +>1pp y/y in 2025; SAM into the mid-30s% of WFE, target high-30s%) (Q2/Q3 FY26 calls).

Position in the value chain

Lam sits in the middle of the WFE stack: precision-component suppliers feed it, and it sells the etch/depo toolsets to the chipmakers who actually run the fabs. It is the etch+deposition leg of the AMAT / KLAC / LRCX US WFE trio (KLA = process-control/metrology; AMAT = depo/PVD/CMP-plus), and is the most memory-capex levered of the three — NAND layer conversion and DRAM/HBM are its dollar drivers.

Current state (latest quarter)

FQ3 FY26 / March-quarter (reported 2026-04-22): revenue $5.84B (+9% q/q, +24% y/y), above midpoint — third consecutive record-revenue quarter; GAAP GM 49.9% (high end), operating margin 35% (high end), record diluted EPS $1.47 (above the high end of the guide) (Q3 FY26 call; figures consistent with 10-Q FQ3 FY26, 2026-04-23). DRAM hit an all-time-high 27% of systems (from 23%), +45% y/y, on capacitor scaling (Stryker ALD) and HBM electroplating/TSV etch. Advanced packaging guided to exceed +50% in CY2026. CSBG $2.1B (record, first $2B+ quarter, +25% y/y). By region: China 34% (from 35%), Korea and Taiwan each 23% (record dollar levels) (Q3 FY26 call). Capital return 139% of FCF; $4.3B buyback remaining.

Guidance (Q3 FY26 call, 2026-04-22): FQ4 FY26 (June quarter) revenue $6.6B ± $400M, GM 50.5% ± 1pp, operating margin 36.5% ± 1pp, EPS $1.65 ± $0.15. CY2026 WFE raised to ~$140B (from $135B at Q2 FY26, 2026-01-28, off ~$110B in CY2025) — Archer: spending projections "have moved higher across all device segments." NAND is the structural new leg: a ~$40B NAND conversion with the "majority of spending occurring before the end of CY2027" (pulled forward), data-center NAND bits now exceeding PC + mobile combined. Watch item: management flagged China revenue declines in the June quarter, and customer down payments fell to a ~4-year low (Q3 FY26 call). Second Malaysia facility (~same size as the first) ramps in H2 to scale into next year's demand.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: This is a capacity-driven cycle and the early-cycle, capacity-adds beneficiaries are the etch/depo names — "Lam Research and AMAT are our top picks... these names tend to perform better earlier in the cycle when you're just adding a lot of wafer capacity" (BofA, 2026-04-16). Lam was "the biggest beneficiary in our coverage from the last couple years for 3-nanometer... very etch-and-depo intensive node," and that intensity story extends through the next couple of years (BofA, 2026-04-16). UBS pairs LRCX + AMAT as "the two stocks you want to own" and the two growing faster than WFE in 2026 and 2027 (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15; "I like Semi Caps, AMAT, LAM. I've been pushing those now for a while," 2026-05-18). JPM is OW all three large caps — "overweight... KLA, Lam, and Applied Materials" (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15; 2026-05-22). The WFE TAM ceiling keeps rising — UBS holds $200B CY27 / $250B CY28 and reads Lam's capacity actions as confirmation: "they talked... about building a second facility in Malaysia... they are talking about building a third site... they wouldn't be talking about a third site unless they thought the number was going to go to 250 and potentially beyond" (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-18). Memory mix is inflecting Lam's way — DRAM/HBM scaling, GAA (~$1B incremental Lam SAM per 100k wfr/mo), and a NAND layer-conversion wave pulled into CY27 — and Lam keeps gaining ship-share of WFE.
  • Bear: Buy-side bars on WFE are "very, very high" — AMAT's guide-up sold off because expectations were already there (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15), and semicap multiples "do tend to be sensitive to memory stocks" (BofA conference wrap, 2026-06-08), so a DRAM/HBM pricing wobble hits the group. China is Lam's single largest region at 34% and management guided it down in the June quarter, with down payments at a ~4-year low (Q3 FY26 call) — clearance for now, but the most exposed of the trio to BIS/affiliate-rule timing. NAND greenfield is still absent — Lam's near-term NAND is upgrade/conversion, not greenfield (first greenfield not until CY27/28, Q2 FY26 call; Sur/JPM 2026-05-15). On segment leadership, CY26 favors depo/etch (LRCX/AMAT) but CY27 rotates to the new-node leaders KLAC/ASML as sub-2nm fires (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15) — a forward relative-revisions headwind vs. KLA. If asked to pick one depo/etch name, UBS marginally prefers AMAT on higher DRAM/PVD share (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15).
  • Where the sell-side stands: No LRCX-specific PT was in the equity-call/briefing repo, but the directional read is uniformly constructive: BofA top pick (with AMAT) for the capacity cycle (2026-04-16); UBS top-two pick (2026-05-15/18); JPM OW (2026-05-15/22); the AMAT print read directly positive to LRCX ("AMAT mgmt confirmed Lam / KLA seeing same near-term step-up," briefings, mid-May 2026). LRCX appeared on 52-week-high lists through May (briefings) and is a top-10 position in Tiger Global's book (~$1.12B) and held in Third Point's semi-equipment sleeve (briefings, May 2026). _No live market-data access — PTs/consensus quoted only where present in a dated source; the AMAT-comp PTs ($454–550, multiple banks, briefings 2026-05-15) are read-through, not LRCX targets._

Catalysts / what to watch

  • FQ4 FY26 print (late July 2026): June-quarter guide $6.6B / EPS $1.65 / GM 50.5%; watch GM trajectory (Bettinger: GM "going to level out... for the rest of the year") and any further CY26 WFE upgrade past $140B.
  • China trajectory: management guided June-quarter China down off 34%; watch the magnitude and any affiliate-rule / BIS news — the trio's most China-levered name.
  • NAND conversion ramp — the ~$40B layer-conversion wave with majority of spend pulled into CY27; the structural new leg vs. the 2025 NAND-light base. First greenfield timing (CY27/28) a watch item.
  • DRAM/HBM cadence — DRAM now 27% of systems and rising; HBM4/HBM4E + 16-high stacking lift electroplating/TSV-etch SAM. MU F3Q26 print 2026-06-24 a near-term read-through (semicap-WFE theme).
  • Capacity build-out — second Malaysia fab ramps H2; UBS flags a third site being discussed (ex-Malaysia) as a $250B-WFE signal (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-18).
  • Segment rotation into CY27 — whether the depo/etch leadership (LRCX/AMAT) hands off to KLAC/ASML on the sub-2nm node wave (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15).

Risks

  • China / export controls: China is the largest region (34% in FQ3, 10-Q 2026-04-23); directly exposed to BIS rules and affiliate-rule timing (the Q2→Q3 swing from 43%→34% was affiliate-rule-driven; Q2 FY26 call).
  • Customer & geographic concentration: international is the substantial majority of revenue; the two largest customers in FY25 were Samsung and TSMC (10-K FY25, 2025-08-11) — orders from a small set of leading chipmakers.
  • Cyclicality + memory beta: WFE is cyclical and AI-capex-dependent; semicap multiples track memory equities (BofA, 2026-06-08). Pushouts/cancellations create earnings and inventory volatility.
  • Technology-adoption timing / competition: success depends on continued etch/depo-intensity gains and new-product wins against AMAT, Tokyo Electron, Hitachi, ASMI (10-K FY25); a slip in GAA, DRAM 1c, or NAND-conversion timing dents the SAM story.
  • NAND greenfield absence: near-term NAND is conversion/upgrade, not greenfield — leaves the NAND leg dependent on technology migration until CY27/28 (Q2 FY26 call; Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15).
  • Forward relative revisions: segment leadership may rotate toward process-control/litho (KLAC/ASML) in CY27 as sub-2nm ramps (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15).

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • BofA (TSMC/ASML review) _(2026-04-16)_: LRCX + AMAT flagged top picks — biggest 3nm beneficiaries, 'etch-and-depo intensive.'
  • UBS (G. Vella) — 'WFE Update: Early Innings' _(2026-06-10)_: constructive WFE-cycle read-through; LRCX the most memory-capex-levered of the trio.

Sources

  • Filings: 10-K FY2025 (2025-08-11) — business, products (deposition/etch/clean/CSBG), competitors, customer concentration, risk factors · 10-Q FQ3 FY2026 (2026-04-23) — March-quarter financials, region/market mix · 10-Q FQ1 FY2026 (2025-10-24) — September-quarter financials.
  • Transcripts: Q3 FY26 (2026-04-22) · Q2 FY26 (2026-01-28) · Q1 FY26 (2025-10-22) · Q4 FY25 (2025-07-30).
  • Equity calls: 2026-04-16 BofA — TSMC/ASML review (LRCX+AMAT top picks, 3nm beneficiary) · 2026-05-15 Sur/JPM — semicap/AMAT (OW all three; segment leadership by year; NAND greenfield CY28) · 2026-05-18 Arcuri/UBS — weekly (LRCX+AMAT; Malaysia 2nd/3rd site = $250B signal) · 2026-06-10 Micron ex-VP — procurement (AMAT+Lam = next ~20% of memory tool spend after ASML) · 2026-06-08 BofA — conference wrap (semicap group). AMAT compilado cross-reads: _compilados/AMAT.md.
  • Theme: Semicap / WFE — LRCX in the cross-company WFE synthesis ($200B/$250B CY27/28).
  • Research library: SemiCap_2025_Actuals_and_Outlook.pdf (2026-04-28, incl. LRCX; E:\research_library, catalog 00_CATALOGO.md).
  • Briefings: LRCX read-through in E:\briefings\2026\*-company-specific.md (mid-May 2026, AMAT-print read-across; 52-wk-high lists; fund-holdings color). No dedicated LRCX roll-up in _briefings/by-ticker/.
  • Outlook: not available in this environment (no connected profile / two-pass outlook.py returns empty) — sell-side email flow to be added separately.

SAMSUNG — Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (KRX:005930)

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\SAMSUNG\transcripts (public English earnings transcripts) · Samsung IR · _briefings roll-up · BBG (KRW, auto-injected). Master index: ../00_INDEX.md.

No SEC filings (Korean issuer). Samsung Electronics files in Korea (KRX 005930) and via GDR (LSE: SMSN); it is not a US SEC registrant — no 10-K/10-Q/20-F on disk or on EDGAR. All financials below are from public English-language earnings-call transcripts and Samsung IR, in KRW, with the consensus block auto-injected from BBG. Transcripts are summaries, not verbatim. Byline: transcripts + IR + BBG (KRW).

Snapshot

Samsung Electronics is the world's largest memory maker and a vertically integrated tech conglomerate spanning four engines: Device Solutions (DS — DRAM, HBM, NAND + Foundry + System LSI), Mobile Experience (MX — Galaxy smartphones/foldables, networks), Samsung Display (SDC — mobile/large OLED), and consumer electronics (DX/VD). The investable story is overwhelmingly DS/memory: DS sales were KRW 81.7tn (~$55bn) in Q1-2026, of which memory KRW 74.8tn, +292% y/y (Q1-2026 call, 2026-04-30). Within memory, Samsung is the #1 DRAM/NAND maker by capacity but the laggard in HBM — it trails SK Hynix on the NVIDIA HBM ramp and only began HBM4 mass production in February 2026 (Q1-2026 call). Its Foundry is the credible #2 logic foundry behind TSMC, increasingly winning interest as TSMC advanced-node capacity is pre-booked (briefings, May–Jun 2026). The two debates that move the stock: HBM4 catch-up vs SK Hynix and Foundry share gains vs TSMC.

Position in the value chain

Samsung sits in the middle of the AI-memory chain: it buys equipment and wafers upstream, and sells memory (HBM/DRAM/NAND), foundry wafers, and finished devices/panels downstream. Its differentiation: the only player that is simultaneously a top-3 HBM supplier to NVIDIA and a logic-foundry alternative to TSMC and a device OEM — but it lags SK Hynix on HBM qualification and trails TSMC on foundry yield, so it competes largely on availability and price rather than leadership.

Foundry-vs-TSMC angle: TSMC advanced-node capacity is pre-booked through 2028+, pushing AMD, Google (Axion ~2028 / TPU), Tesla and others to engage Samsung Foundry on availability despite lagging yields (briefings 2026-05-07 / 2026-06-18). Cross-themes: HBM / memory super-cycle · semicap / WFE.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1-2026 (reported 2026-04-30) — record quarter on AI memory. Revenue KRW 134tn (+43% q/q); operating profit KRW 57tn (+185% q/q); operating margin 43% (from 21%); net profit KRW 47tn; EPS KRW 7,123 (Q1-2026 call). The OP ramp over four quarters frames the inflection: KRW 4.7tn (Q2-25) → 12.2tn (Q3-25) → 20.1tn (Q4-25) → 57tn (Q1-26) (transcripts).

  • DS KRW 81.7tn (~$55bn), +225% y/y; memory KRW 74.8tn, +292% y/y (Q1-2026 call).
  • DRAM: bit growth low-teens % q/q, ASP up "low 90% range" q/q. NAND: bit +low-20s %, ASP +high-80s % q/q (Q1-2026 call). Note: conventional DRAM currently more profitable than HBM (Q1-2026 call; corroborated wccftech 2026-04-30) — the cycle is being led by commodity DRAM, not HBM, at Samsung.
  • HBM: first to mass-produce/ship HBM4 in February 2026; "demand for HBM4 fully booked"; HBM4 >50% of HBM sales from Q3; HBM sales >3x y/y in 2026; HBM4E samples in Q2 (16 Gbps, 4.0 TB/s) (Q1-2026 call; Q4-2025 call reiterated "HBM triples in 2026").
  • Foundry: double-digit y/y revenue growth, earnings down q/q on seasonality; Taylor TX equipment move-in Apr 23, ops 2026 / mass production 2027; 1.4nm on track; 2nm GAA ramping (first-gen new products from Q4-25, Q3-2025 call).
  • MX: revenue KRW 37.5tn; MX+Network OP KRW 2.8tn; Q2 profitability decline "inevitable" on memory cost. SDC: mobile weak on seasonality + memory cost; premium/QD-OLED strategy.
  • Capex/return: Q1 capex KRW 11.2tn (DS 10.2tn); 2026 "substantial y/y increase." Quarterly dividend KRW 372/sh; KRW 9.8tn annual minimum payout (2024–26); share cancellation ~KRW 14.6tn (Q1-2026 call).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: Three engines firing from 2H26 — memory + foundry + robotics (Jefferies/Jeff Kim, 2026-05-11: Q2-26 OP KRW 100tn vs his KRW 84tn; "cheapest it will be" at 4.7x '27 P/E). The DRAM shortage has "no way out in coming years"; foundry optionality is incremental upside as hyperscalers diversify from TSMC (@techfund1, briefing 2026-06-18). Memory complex tape-positive: Bernstein bullish through 2027, MS sales reinforcing preference for memory suppliers incl. Samsung (briefings 2026-05-08 / 2026-05-21). Korea tape repeatedly rips on the name (Samsung +8.9% 2026-05-21; +4.8% overnight 2026-06-17 on Apple memory pass-through).
  • Bear: Samsung is the HBM laggard — HBM4 qualified but volume not ramped, product still trails SK Hynix on power/speed; SK Hynix is the relative-performance long (JPM memory-China, 2026-05-28; hbm-memory). The ex-Micron VP frames HBM as commoditizing once 2nd/3rd vendors qualify, but notes Samsung's cost structure is actually better than Micron's — i.e., in a commodity snap-back Samsung is relatively advantaged but still rides a cyclical top (2026-06-10). Foundry yields lag TSMC; wins are availability-driven, not leadership. Union strike risk: JPM flagged 7–12% downside to 2026 OP, an 18-day strike = >KRW 4tn opportunity loss (briefing 2026-05-08).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: Jefferies (Jeff Kim) raising estimates, Q2-26 OP KRW 100tn, 4.7x '27 P/E (2026-05-11). SK Hynix ~5.3x vs Samsung ~5.9x fwd P/E; FinTwit memory books underweight Samsung vs SK Hynix on HBM/yield edge (@paradislabs, 2026-05-07). Net: consensus likes the memory cycle, prefers SK Hynix for HBM purity, owns Samsung for the cheaper multiple + foundry/robotics optionality.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • HBM4 volume ramp at NVIDIA — qualification disclosed as "fully booked," but the share-gain proof point is volume share vs SK Hynix through 2H26 (Q1-2026 call).
  • HBM4E samples → qualification (Q2-26 samples, 16 Gbps / 4.0 TB/s) — the de-commoditization optionality (custom base die).
  • Foundry wins: AMD 2nm (post-Lisa Su Pyeongtaek visit), Google Axion ~2028 / TPU, Tesla — concrete contract confirmations (briefings 2026-05-07 / 2026-06-18). Taylor TX mass production 2027; 1.4nm.
  • Q2-2026 print (~late Jul 2026): test of Jefferies' KRW 100tn OP; DRAM ASP trajectory (server DRAM C3Q26 +30-40% q/q per @QQ_Timmy, 2026-06-16).
  • Union strike resolution / production impact (announced May 21–Jun 7 window; foundry/LSI unresolved per trendforce 2026-05-16).
  • 1d (7th-gen) DRAM mass production, earliest 1H27 (@jukan05, briefing 2026-06-17).

Risks

  • HBM share loss to SK Hynix — laggard on NVIDIA qualification/yield; SK Hynix has the multiyear NVIDIA tech partnership (hbm-memory; JPM 2026-05-28).
  • Memory commodity snap-back — current cycle led by conventional DRAM ASP (+~90% q/q); new fab capacity (Samsung/SK/Micron) landing ~CY27-28 could break pricing (ex-Micron VP, 2026-06-10).
  • Foundry yield gap vs TSMC — wins are availability-driven; profitability/utilization remains the swing.
  • Korea-specific: union strike (7–12% OP downside, JPM 2026-05-08); concentrated chaebol governance.
  • Macro: tariffs, FX (KRW), Middle East/oil, China demand; memory cost inflation pressuring own MX/SDC margins (Q1-2026 call).
  • Capex intensity: "substantial" 2026 increase into a potentially cyclical top.

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • Digitimes _(2026-06-19)_: Samsung Foundry signed a strategic deal to make 'Claros' power-management chips for AI data centers — foundry AI-DC traction.
  • Capstone Morning Briefing _(2026-06-19)_: memory super-cycle tape — conventional-DRAM ASP melt-up + HBM5 (2028-29) roadmap; Samsung framed as the HBM4 catch-up story vs SK Hynix.

SKHYNIX — SK hynix Inc. (KRX:000660)

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · Korean issuer — NO SEC filings (no 10-K/10-Q/20-F). Sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\SKHYNIX\transcripts (4 earnings calls, SK hynix holds English calls) · SK hynix IR (news.skhynix.com) · _equity_calls · E:\briefings\2026 · BBG (KRW). Master index: 00_INDEX.md · Theme: hbm-memory.

Snapshot

SK hynix is the world's #1 high-bandwidth memory (HBM) maker and the #2 DRAM/NAND producer overall, and is NVIDIA's primary HBM supplier (~90% of NVDA HBM historically; UBS sees ~70% HBM4 share on Rubin in 2026 — search digest, 2026-06). FY2025 revenue 97.1tn KRW (+47% YoY), operating profit 47.2tn (+101%), 49% OP margin (SK hynix IR FY25, 2026-01-29). Mix: DRAM (incl. HBM) is the profit engine; NAND/enterprise SSD via wholly-owned Solidigm, where the group's eSSD share reached 30.2% in Q4-2025 (search digest, 2026-06). It sits at the demand epicentre of the AI capex cycle: HBM has been "sold out since 2023," and full-year 2026 DRAM and HBM volume is contracted out (Q3 2025 call, 2025-10-29).

Position in the value chain

SK hynix converts silicon wafers and litho/etch/deposition tools (ASML EUV, AMAT, LRCX) into DRAM + HBM stacks and NAND/eSSD, then ships to NVIDIA (primary HBM customer, into the Rubin platform), other accelerator/hyperscaler buyers, and mobile/PC OEMs. Its moat is HBM process + advanced packaging lead (HBM3E 12-high, first-to-mass-production HBM4) plus a net-cash balance sheet (35tn KRW at Q1-2026) funding the M15X / Yongin / Indiana packaging build-out.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 2026 (reported 2026-04-23, English call): Revenue 52.6tn KRW (+60% QoQ, +198% YoY), operating profit 37.6tn (≈doubled QoQ), OP margin 72% (+13pp QoQ), net profit 40.3tn (77% net margin), EBITDA 41.3tn. Net cash 35tn KRW (cash 54.3tn). Management: AI-infrastructure demand drove "significant price increases for both DRAM and NAND"; customers "prioritize securing volume over price." HBM4 demand "far exceeding current supply capacity," supplying HBM3E + HBM4 simultaneously. NAND shipments ~-10% QoQ on a high prior-quarter base (SK hynix IR / Yahoo Q1 FY26; CNBC, 2026-04-23). Trajectory across the 4 saved calls: OP margin 41% (Q2'25) → 47% (Q3'25) → 58% (Q4'25) → 72% (Q1'26) — a vertical margin ramp on HBM/DRAM ASP.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull — structural HBM/DRAM supercycle, sold-out book. "Sold out 2026" confirmed at DRAM + HBM level (Q3'25 call); tight supply expected into 2027; HBM4 in mass production ahead of NVDA Rubin. JEF (Conor, 2026-04-07) flagged a Jefferies KBI note arguing Korea memory can out-earn the hyperscalers; SK Hynix ~20% pullback called a buy opportunity (JEF Titu/Wong, briefing 2026-06-19). Digitimes: NVDA-SK Hynix multiyear tech partnership reinforces HBM leadership vs Samsung/Micron (briefing 2026-06-19). DRAM ASP +50-60% QoQ in 2Q26, +20% QoQ 3Q26; NAND +75-100% QoQ in 2Q (memory complex table, briefing 2026-06-19). Korea overnight on the AAPL pass-through story: Hynix +6.5%, Samsung +4.8% (briefing 2026-06-18). @rwang07 (Ray Wang): "SK Hynix is the next 3 trillion-dollar company" (briefing 2026-06-19).
  • Bear — it's still a commodity, peak-margin valuation. Ex-Micron VP procurement (2026-06-10 expert call): HBM becomes a commodity once 2-3 vendors are qualified; "your $900 stock becomes a $50 stock again" once capacity normalizes — risk inside CY26/early CY27. Jefferies (Conor, 2026-04-07): mid-cycle correction risk, customer concentration into data center has risen (Samsung: hyperscalers can bear 30-35% of OCF on memory; already ~20% in Q1 and climbing). Competitive: Samsung nearing a deal for >30% of NVDA HBM4 in 2026; Micron's HBM4 product is competitive and overtook Samsung (search digest, 2026-06) — SK hynix's ~90% NVDA share could compress toward ~50-70%. Elevated DRAM/NAND pricing risks PC/mobile demand destruction (Q1'26 call; Tim Cook WSJ "once-in-100-year flood," briefings 2026-06-18/19).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: No SK Hynix-specific broker email in inbox to date (Outlook unavailable in this environment — user to append). Closest reads: JEF buy-the-dip; GS (Schneider) bullish memory tightness "through CY27, significant upside to Street" via the MU preview read-across (briefing 2026-06-19); MS (Nowak) >$1trn hyperscaler capex '27 underpins memory demand (briefing 2026-06-18).

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2 2026 earnings: late July 2026 (English call; prior Q2 was 2025-07-24) — HBM4 ramp progress, DRAM ASP, capex number.
  • HBM4 / NVDA Rubin ramp through 2026; whether SK hynix holds ~70% HBM4 share vs Samsung's pursued >30% NVDA slot.
  • Samsung/Micron HBM4 qualification at NVDA — the single biggest share-erosion risk.
  • 2026 capex disclosure — M15X (Cheongju) early ramp, first Yongin cluster fab, Indiana advanced-packaging expansion.
  • MU print (this week) as the cleanest near-term memory demand/pricing tell.
  • Consumer demand elasticity — PC/mobile cuts (NVDA SOCAMM2 cut, AAPL prepayment/balance-sheet talk; briefings 2026-06-18/19).

Risks

  • Commodity cyclicality / ASP reversal — peak ~72% OP margin is not a through-cycle level; capacity additions (own + China CXMT/YMTC) normalize pricing.
  • Customer concentration — heavy data-center/NVDA dependence; HBM4 share loss to Samsung/Micron.
  • Demand destruction — elevated DRAM/NAND prices crimp PC/mobile bit demand.
  • Capex intensity — large fab + packaging build-out (Yongin, M15X, Indiana) into a possible cycle peak.
  • Korea/macro & FX — KRW reporting, geopolitical/export-control exposure, China memory entrants.
  • Solidigm/NAND — fragmented, structurally less profitable than DRAM; HBM-driven DRAM mix-shift squeezing NAND wafers cuts both ways.

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • UBS (R. Ruple) SemiBytes / Memory Monthly _(2026-06-08)_: memory/HBM up-cycle commentary — SK Hynix the HBM share leader / NVDA primary supplier.
  • Capstone briefings _(mid-Jun 2026)_: SK Hynix featured across the HBM/memory super-cycle flow (sold-out HBM, margin ramp 41%→72%).

KIOXIA — Kioxia Holdings Corporation (TSE: 285A)

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · No SEC filings — Japanese issuer (TSE Prime: 285A), IPO Dec-2024; thin coverage. Sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\KIOXIA\transcripts (IR results briefings) + E:\briefings\2026 roll-up + BBG (JPY). Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Kioxia is the ex-Toshiba Memory NAND flash pure-play — one of the scaled NAND suppliers alongside Samsung, SK Hynix/Solidigm and Micron, and the manufacturing partner of SanDisk (ex-Western Digital) in the long-running Yokkaichi/Kitakami flash JV. It is the only large NAND maker without a captive DRAM business, so it is the cleanest listed proxy for the NAND/SSD cycle. Revenue splits into SSD & Storage (~55–60% — datacenter/enterprise/client SSD) and Smart Devices (mobile/consumer NAND), plus Other (FY25 results, 2026-05-15). The thesis runs through AI: NAND demand for cloud/AI-server SSD has tightened supply and re-priced the franchise; management targets datacenter + enterprise >60% of revenue by FY2028 (Investor Day, 2026-06-02). IPO'd on TSE Dec-2024; filing to list ADRs in the US to broaden the investor base (Q4 FY25, 2026-05-15). Reports in English; fiscal year ends March. See cross-theme: hbm-memory.

Position in the value chain

Kioxia sits in the middle of the NAND chain: it buys wafers and WFE/equipment from upstream suppliers, fabricates 3D-NAND (BiCS) in the SanDisk JV fabs, and sells flash/SSD to datacenter, hyperscaler and client customers. It is NAND-only (no DRAM/HBM), so its read-through to the HBM trade is via the broader memory-tightness narrative, not direct HBM supply.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q4 FY2025 (quarter ended Mar-2026; reported 2026-05-15) — a blowout. Revenue ¥1,002.9bn, +84.5% QoQ; net income ¥407.7bn (vs ¥87.8bn prior quarter); non-GAAP operating profit ¥599bn vs consensus ¥519bn (+15% beat, briefing 2026-05-15). The move was ASP-driven: ASP +100%+ QoQ — the highest among NAND peers (Korean peers ~80%) — partly offset by bit shipments −10% QoQ on planned maintenance. SSD & Storage revenue ¥600.3bn, +99.8%; Smart Devices ¥337.3bn (+81%). DOI built 87→103 days (strategic supply build). Full-year FY2025: revenue ¥2.337tn (+37% YoY), net profit ¥554.5bn (vs ¥272.3bn). CEO Hiroo Oota: "We are riding the wave of AI demand… the strength of the flash memory market is expected to continue."

Guidance — Q1 FY2026: operating profit ~¥1.3tn vs buyside ¥1.1–1.2tn (briefing 2026-05-15); OP run-rate flagged as higher than Toyota; OP margin guide ~74% (Investor Day, 2026-06-02). Prior quarter (Q3 FY25, reported 2026-02-13): revenue ¥543.6bn (+21.3% QoQ), net income ¥89.5bn; management confirmed CY2026 NAND capacity entirely sold out with the market "tight" on datacenter demand.

Investor Day (2026-06-02): datacenter + enterprise targeted >60% of revenue by FY2028; NAND market CAGR 20%+ (up to 46%) 2025→2028; BiCS Gen.10 (332 layers) sampling summer 2026, mass production within a year; capex averaging ¥470bn/yr FY26-28, R&D rising to ¥230bn/yr; ROIC >60%, equity ratio toward ~60%; capital return up to 50% of net cash flow with progressive dividends from FY2027; shift to multi-year LTAs with hyperscalers/enterprise.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: Cleanest NAND cycle proxy at the most violent point of the up-cycle. JPM (Mio Shikanai / Jay Kwon, OW) holds the Street-high PT, raised ¥38,000 → ¥80,000, FY26-28E EPS +63% to +80%, on ASP/bit +250%+ CY26E and OP 65–90% above consensus FY26E/27E; SSD mix ~60% (briefing 2026-05-17). BofA/JPM team Buy, FY3/27 OP/EPS +52%/+54% (briefing 2026-05-18). MS (Amerian) "very positive — watch IR Day." Structural framing: CY2026 capacity sold out, LTAs (multi-year, prepay, penalties) becoming the "this time is different" catalyst across Samsung/Hynix/Kioxia/MU (JPM Global Memory, briefing 2026-05-18). 2D NAND/MLC exits across the industry (Kioxia phasing out 2D NAND + Gen-3 BiCS by end-2028) are tightening legacy supply, MLC 64Gb spot +300% since end-2024 (briefings 2026-05-08/05-13). FinTwit high-conviction list pairs SNDK/Kioxia as 2-3x candidates (@ParadisLabs, briefing 2026-06-19).
  • Bear: NAND is still a commodity and Kioxia carries the structural geopolitical discount. @MF_Camillus (briefing 2026-06-19): "Kioxia probably won't close the gap with $SNDK… geopolitical premium [stays] in US-domiciled memory names — don't wish for multiple convergence." ADATA chairman thesis (briefing 2026-06-16): AI has broken the 4-year cycle but supply crunch eases ~2028 — i.e. the up-cycle is finite. NAND-only means no HBM/DRAM offset when NAND rolls over; bit shipments already −10% in Q4 means the entire beat is price, which mean-reverts. US ADR listing introduces rotational risk vs WDC/SNDK/MU (briefing 2026-05-16).
  • Where the sell-side stands: Uniformly bullish on disk — JPM OW Street-high ¥80,000 (2026-05-17), BofA/JPM Buy (2026-05-18), MS positive (2026-05-15). Stock limit-up in Tokyo on the May print. The debate is duration (tightness "tight through 2027" per Investor Day) and the structural valuation discount to US-domiciled peers, not direction.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q1 FY2026 print — Aug 7, 2026 (next reporting date per IR). Watch ASP trajectory past the +70% guided QoQ, bit-shipment recovery post-maintenance, and OP vs the ~¥1.3tn guide.
  • US ADR listing — timing/structure; potential rotational flow vs WDC/SNDK/MU.
  • LTA disclosures — multi-year hyperscaler/enterprise agreements into 2027; JPM calls LTA cadence/quality the next major stock driver for the memory complex.
  • BiCS Gen.10 (332L) — sample shipments summer 2026, mass production within ~12 months; cost/power competitiveness vs Samsung/SK Hynix V-NAND.
  • Capital return: first progressive dividend from FY2027; possible stock split / higher equity comp.
  • Capacity adds and CY2027 NAND WFE up-cycle (BofA Hosseini flagged $200bn+ annualized WFE into 2H CY27, briefing 2026-05-15) — supply-discipline watch.

Risks

  • ASP/cyclicality — Q4 beat was entirely price (ASP +100%+, bits −10%); NAND ASP mean-reverts, and NAND-only leaves no DRAM/HBM offset on the downturn.
  • Commodity normalization — supply crunch may ease ~2028 as new capacity lands (ADATA thesis, briefing 2026-06-16); 2D-NAND/Gen-3 BiCS phase-out to end-2028 is a transitional cost.
  • Geopolitical/valuation discount — Japanese issuer perceived to carry a persistent multiple discount vs US-domiciled SNDK/MU (@MF_Camillus, briefing 2026-06-19).
  • JV dependence — flash fabrication shared with SanDisk (Yokkaichi/Kitakami); capacity/capex split and any JV friction affect output.
  • Thin/short public history — IPO Dec-2024, no SEC filings, limited financial track record; disclosure depth below US peers.
  • Customer concentration / demand elasticity — hyperscaler datacenter SSD demand drives the mix; AI-capex air-pocket would hit the most-priced part of the book.

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • JPM 'Bits, Broadcasts & Bandwidth' _(2026-05-29)_: NAND/flash pricing up-cycle read-through across the storage ecosystem.
  • _Direct desk coverage of Kioxia is thin in the inbox (Japanese issuer, IPO Dec-2024) — mostly NAND-complex read-through._

  • Filings: none — Japanese issuer (TSE Prime: 285A), IPO Dec-2024; no SEC filings exist.

  • Investor Day: Kioxia Investor Day 2026 (2026-06-02) — capacity/BiCS roadmap, capex ¥470bn/yr, capital-return policy (via Quartr summary).
  • Briefings: E:\briefings\2026 — Kioxia datapoints in 2026-05-15/05-16/05-17/05-18/05-08/05-13 (earnings, JPM Street-high PT, memory complex) and 2026-06-16/06-19 (SNDK/Kioxia gap, FinTwit conviction).

SNDK — SanDisk Corporation

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\SNDK (filings + transcripts) · _briefings (well-covered: NAND/memory). Master index: ../00_INDEX.md. Theme: HBM / Memory super-cycle.

Snapshot

SanDisk is a pure-play NAND flash maker and storage-solutions vendor — flash components plus SSDs, memory cards and drives sold under the SanDisk and WD brands (FY25 10-K, 2025-08-21). It is the standalone flash business spun out of Western Digital, which separated on Feb 21, 2025; trades on Nasdaq as SNDK with only a short post-spin filing history (one 10-K, six 10-Qs as of Jun-2026). It runs a single reportable segment but reports three end-markets: Data Center, Edge (PC/mobile), and Consumer (10-K; Q3 FY26 transcript, 2026-04-30). Bits come from the long-standing Kioxia "Flash Ventures" JV (BiCS NAND fabs in Yokkaichi & Kitakami, Japan; JV extended to Dec-2034). It competes with Samsung, SK Hynix and YMTC in flash (FY25 10-K). The thesis: NAND is being repriced from a commodity into a strategic AI-storage input — enterprise/QLC SSD for the data-center capacity tier and KV-cache, plus on-device-AI premium storage at the edge.

Position in the value chain

SanDisk sits in the middle of the flash chain: it co-develops and co-manufactures BiCS NAND with Kioxia (Flash Ventures JV) plus buys equipment/wafers, turns it into flash + SSD/storage solutions, and sells to datacenter/enterprise (hyperscalers), client OEMs and consumer channels. Short post-spin history (standalone since Feb-2025) means limited standalone comparables — model off the WDC-era flash segment plus six quarters of SNDK filings.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q3 FY26 (reported 2026-04-30, quarter ended Apr 3, 2026) — a blowout. Revenue $5.95B, +97% q/q, +251% y/y, well past the $4.4–4.8B guide. Non-GAAP gross margin 78.4% (vs 51.1% prior q; guide 65–67%); non-GAAP EPS $23.41 (vs $6.20). Adjusted FCF $2.955B (49.7% margin); cash $3.735B. Crucially, bits were flat y/y and down high-teens q/q — the move is entirely price/mix, not volume (Q3 FY26 transcript). Data center $1.467B, +233% q/q (TLC enterprise SSD + QLC "Stargate" readiness); Edge $3.163B, +118%; Consumer $820M, −10%.

Business-model shift: five multiyear supply agreements (NBMs) signed — 3 in Q3, 2 in Q4 — with $42B minimum contractual revenue (Q3 contracts) and >$11B financial guarantees (incl. ~$400M prepayments), covering >1/3 of FY27 bit supply (Goeckeler/Visoso, 2026-04-30). Kioxia JV extended to Dec-2034; $1B Nanya investment for DRAM supply.

Guidance — Q4 FY26: revenue $7.75–8.25B, GM 79–81%, EPS $30–33 (158M shares). New $6B buyback authorized; remaining $650M term loan repaid; capex held to ~4% of revenue. CY26 data-center growth raised to mid-70s% (from 60s%). The trajectory off the spin is steep: Q4 FY25 GM 26.4% / EPS $0.29 (2025-08-14) → Q3 FY26 GM 78.4% / EPS $23.41 in three quarters.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: AI is turning NAND structural. The disk transcripts frame NAND as "the only economically viable solution" for trillion-parameter model capacity (Goeckeler, 2026-04-30), with KV-cache alone an estimated 75–100 incremental exabytes in 2027, doubling thereafter (Q2 FY26, 2026-01-29). The NBM/LTA layer ($42B backlog, >$11B guarantees, >1/3 of FY27 bits locked) is the re-rating mechanism — management argues it makes earnings "durable, structurally higher… less cyclical." Sell-side validation: MS (Joseph Moore, OW) raised PT to $1,750 from $1,100 on 2026-06-02/03 ("+59% raise… match published estimates"; briefing 2026-06-03). GS went "full bullish mode on memory stocks," citing MU + SNDK (briefing 2026-06-01). SNDK is repeatedly named in the GOOG $80B AI-supply-chain map for NAND (ParadisLabs, briefings 2026-06-03). Tape: hit ATHs alongside the memory complex through May (briefings 2026-05-07).
  • Bear: This is still NAND, and the print is running entirely on price while bits shrink — the classic late-cycle tell. The cross-read from the HBM/memory theme and the on-disk Micron ex-VP call (2026-06-10) is that astronomical memory margins normalize hard once new fab capacity lands ~a year out; the AI buildout is a bull-whip several layers from end demand. FinTwit flagged the crowding: JEF called SNDK the template for "an extremely crowded stock running into the print with high expectations" (briefing 2026-05-07), and Burry/Krinsky's "Party Like It's 1999" analog cited SNDK +581% as 1999's 2nd-best NDX name (briefing 2026-05-07) — i.e., a momentum-blowoff comp. Hedgeye's "$25K → $1.06M in 12 months (43x)" is celebrated as a top-signal, not a fundamental (briefings 2026-05-06/07).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: Bullish and getting more so. MS OW PT $1,750 (2026-06-03), GS bullish (2026-06-01); SNDK structurally featured in hyperscaler-capex supply maps. The debate, as with MU, is no longer direction but duration — bulls underwrite multi-year tightness + LTA-locked bits; bears see a price-only blowoff in a commodity that always mean-reverts. No bear-side broker PT on disk; the bear case is voiced via FinTwit and the memory-theme read-through.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q4 FY26 print (fiscal year-end June; reported ~Aug-2026) — vs guide rev $7.75–8.25B / GM 79–81% / EPS $30–33. Watch GM past 80%, bit-shipment trajectory, and any further NBM signings / backlog disclosure.
  • NBM / LTA cadence — incremental multiyear agreements and how much of FY27/FY28 bits get locked (and at fixed vs variable pricing) is the durability catalyst.
  • BiCS 8 ramp — to majority of bit production exiting FY26 (Q1 FY26, 2025-11-06); QLC "Stargate" hyperscaler revenue ramp.
  • High Bandwidth Flash (HBF) — trademarked, "Best of Show" at FMS; samples expected 2027 — the bull-case optionality on AI accelerator-attached flash.
  • WDC stake unwind — UBS (Arcuri) tracked WDC exchanging SNDK shares for WDC stock toward full redemption by Sep-Q (briefings 2026-05-06/07); residual overhang/technical.
  • $6B buyback execution against the elevated multiple.

Risks

  • Commodity cyclicality — NAND ASPs remain volatile; current results are price-driven with declining bits, the canonical setup for a sharp reversal when capacity normalizes (theme read-through; Micron ex-VP, 2026-06-10).
  • Competition — Samsung, SK Hynix, YMTC and assemblers; YMTC (China) pressure on the low end (FY25 10-K).
  • Kioxia JV dependence — bit supply, cost and capex are tied to Flash Ventures; the $1.165B 2026–29 manufacturing payment + JV terms flow through COGS (Q2 FY26, 2026-01-29).
  • Customer concentration — top-ten customers a large share of revenue (FY25 10-K); hyperscaler demand is lumpy.
  • Short standalone history — limited as a public company since Feb-2025; thin comparables, evolving capital structure, residual WDC-separation items.
  • China / geopolitics — Unis Venture (China JV) restructuring; export/tariff exposure (FY25 10-K).
  • LTA execution risk — backlog ($42B) and guarantees are only as good as counterparty follow-through if pricing rolls over.

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • JPM 'Bits, Broadcasts & Bandwidth' _(2026-05-29)_: SanDisk benefits with WDC/Seagate from improving enterprise-storage demand + rising flash pricing + higher nearline capacity.
  • Morgan Stanley (per briefings): bullish NAND call, street-high PT ~$1,750 flagged — with crowding / all-time-high cautionary notes.

WDC — Western Digital

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\WDC (filings + transcripts) · _equity_calls · _briefings. Master index: 00_INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Western Digital is now a pure-play hard disk drive (HDD) company after spinning off its Flash/NAND unit as SanDisk on February 21, 2025 (FY25 10-K, 2025-08-14). It develops and manufactures substantially all of its own recording heads and magnetic media, selling capacity-enterprise (nearline) drives into three end markets — Cloud, Client, Consumer — but the business is now overwhelmingly a hyperscaler mass-storage story: Cloud was 89% of revenue in fiscal Q3 FY26 (10-Q for period ended 2026-04-03; Q3 FY26 call, 2026-04-30). It is one of three scaled HDD makers alongside Seagate and Toshiba (FY25 10-K). The thesis: HDDs remain the lowest-TCO medium for the petabyte-to-exabyte "cold/warm" tier that the AI data buildout is inflating — management frames long-term storage demand at >25% CAGR with exabyte shipments +34% y/y last quarter (Q3 FY26, 2026-04-30). Differentiation runs on areal-density transitions: ePMR/OptiNAND/UltraSMR today, HAMR (heat-assisted magnetic recording) for the 44TB+ → 100TB+ roadmap.

Position in the value chain

WDC is the manufacturing integrator in the HDD chain: it builds its own heads and media in-house (vertical integration) and assembles drives, then sells nearline/capacity-enterprise HDDs to hyperscalers and enterprise. Post-SanDisk spin it is pure HDD — no NAND/Flash — so it is the cost-of-bits alternative to (not a participant in) the NAND complex. Customers are concentrated: top 10 = 71% of revenue (10-Q, 2026-04-03).

Current state (latest quarter)

Q3 FY26 (reported 2026-04-30) — a beat-and-raise. Revenue $3.3B, +45% y/y, above the high end of guide; non-GAAP EPS $2.72 (~2x y/y); non-GAAP gross margin 50.5% (+1,040bps y/y, +440bps q/q); operating margin 38.6%; FCF $978M. Cloud $3.0B (89% of rev), +48% y/y. Volume 222 exabytes, +34% y/y; pricing per TB +9% y/y on "better TCO value." The 10-Q (period ended 2026-04-03) attributes the +45% to a 34% rise in exabytes sold plus a 9% ASP/TB increase "across all end markets." Net cash positive (~$450M) after SanDisk monetization cut $3.1B of debt. No planned increase to unit capacity — growth is areal-density + capacity-mix driven (Q3 FY26 call).

Guidance — Q4 FY26: revenue $3.65B ±$100M (~40% y/y at midpoint), gross margin 51–52%, EPS $3.25 ±$0.15 (Q3 FY26 call, 2026-04-30). Dividend raised 20% to $0.15/share; $752M buyback in the quarter.

Technology: HAMR now in qualification with four customers (44TB drives; roadmap >100TB); 40TB ePMR in qual with three customers, volume H2 2026; UltraSMR adopted by the three largest customers (Q3 FY26 call). LTAs now extend into CY2028–2029, structured as exabyte-volume commitments with pricing that may reset on new-capacity introductions — a notable lengthening vs Q2's "sold out for CY26 / agreements through CY27-28" framing (Q2 FY26 call, 2026-01-29).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: A structurally elongated cycle, not a normal one. MS's Erik Woodring (equity call, 2026-06-16) raised numbers "materially again" on Asia checks: demand is broadening from core cloud to AI training/inference, humanoids and robots; supply is capped at ~30-35% annual exabyte growth, so the imbalance elongates the cycle rather than adding near-term volume. The punchline is pricing: HDD vendors are targeting ~$25/TB blended nearline in CY27 and ~$30 in CY28 vs ~$14.50/TB today — potential to roughly double pricing in two years via sequential escalators; non-LTA customers are already paying ~+30% q/q and distributor/NeoCloud "spot" is $30-35/TB. Woodring's WDC CY27/CY28 EPS would be "a little over $45 and a little over $70" if checks held — sub-10x on CY28. WDC has the cleaner balance sheet and leading share longer-term (Woodring, 2026-06-16). Corroborating: HDDs running ~90% sequential incremental gross margins and managing price up "on a steady basis," no longer giving back generation-change discounts in a supply-constrained market (JPM/Samik via hbm-memory theme, 2026-06-12).
  • Bear: (1) Tactical second-fiddle to Seagate — Woodring keeps Seagate as top pick: STX reprices its Google HAMR "sweetheart" contract higher into Sept-26 and ramps 40TB HAMR in 2H CY26, whereas WDC hasn't qualified HAMR yet and ramps 44TB only in H1 CY27, so WDC lacks the same near-term pricing/GM juice (Woodring, 2026-06-16). (2) Valuation re-rating risk — the gap to memory is the running buy-side argument for HDD, but FinTwit bears (@MF_Camillus, @MF_Camillus, briefings 2026-06-16/19) flip it: "HDD TCO advantage overstated; the 40x+ vs HDD valuation gap is an aberration" that resolves toward DRAM/SSD. (3) Peak-earnings optics — same duration worry Woodring flags on servers applies to storage: if exabyte/order growth decelerates while EPS is still rising, the multiple de-rates regardless (2026-06-16). (4) Substitution from flash in lower-capacity/legacy form factors as NAND scales (FY25 10-K).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: MS (Woodring) Overweight on both HDDs, Seagate top pick, WDC 1B — both at ~11-12x earnings on bull numbers (2026-06-16). WDC repeatedly on Rothschild's 12-month relative-high screen (briefings 2026-06-17/18) alongside STX, AMAT, ASML. WDC is a standing name in BofA/Fenske's "REAL bogey poll" (true buy-side ests) for storage/memory (briefings 2026-05-11/15). No on-disk Outlook desk notes specific to WDC this window (see Sources).

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q4 FY26 print (next earnings, ~late July 2026): guide $3.65B / 51-52% GM / $3.25 EPS — watch GM trajectory toward the next leg and any CY27/28 LTA pricing disclosures.
  • HAMR qualification — conversion of the four customers in qual to volume; 44TB ramp timing (H1 CY27 per Woodring) is the gating item vs Seagate's 2H-CY26 40TB ramp.
  • LTA pricing resets — CY27/CY28 contract pricing (escalators toward the $25/$30/TB targets) is the single biggest EPS swing factor (Woodring, 2026-06-16).
  • SanDisk stake monetization — residual shares to be cleared (debt-for-equity swaps); de-levering progression.
  • Exabyte demand breadth — confirmation that AI training/inference/physical-AI sustains the >25% storage CAGR. See storage-demand read-through in hbm-memory and ai-datacenter-power.

Risks

  • Technology-transition execution — failure to ramp HAMR cost-effectively (FY25 10-K explicitly flags the HAMR areal-density transition as a key risk); WDC is behind Seagate on HAMR qualification (Woodring, 2026-06-16).
  • Customer concentration — top 10 = 71% of revenue (10-Q, 2026-04-03); hyperscaler capex/spending plans drive demand (FY25 10-K).
  • ASP / demand cyclicality — "highly competitive industry subject to variations in ASPs and demand"; systems-demand volatility has an exaggerated effect on component demand (FY25 10-K). Peak-pricing optics if exabyte growth decelerates.
  • Flash substitution — flash-based solutions compete in lower-capacity, smaller-form-factor and legacy markets as NAND scales (FY25 10-K).
  • Macro / tariffs / China — demand and pricing influenced by macro factors including tariffs and recession risk (FY25 10-K).
  • Separation overhang — newly standalone HDD cost/capital structure; residual SanDisk-stake disposition (FY25 10-K).

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • Morgan Stanley (Erik Woodring) — 'HDDs: The Next Leg Higher' _(2026-06-15)_: HDD pricing-upside idea (Industry View Cautious, but the long thesis is nearline pricing).
  • Erik Woodring expert-call recap _(2026-06-16)_: HDD pricing upside + server strength (WDC/STX, Dell/HPE, Apple cycles).

STX — Seagate Technology

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\STX (filings + transcripts) · _equity_calls · _briefings. Master index: 00_INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Seagate (Ireland-domiciled, "Seagate Technology Holdings plc") is one of three scaled hard-disk-drive makers (with Western Digital and Toshiba) and a vertically integrated one — it designs and manufactures substantially all of its own recording heads and media (FY25 10-K, 2025-08-01). The business is now overwhelmingly a hyperscaler mass-storage story: the Mass Capacity segment (high-capacity nearline drives + systems for cloud/enterprise) is the majority of revenue, and data center was 80% of revenue / 88% of exabytes in the March 2026 quarter (Q3 FY26 call, 2026-04-28). The thesis runs on areal density: Seagate's Mozaic platform is "the industry's first implementation of HAMR" (heat-assisted magnetic recording) (FY25 10-K), and per IDC hard drives store 87% of exabytes in large data-center deployments (FY25 10-K). It also sells SSDs and Lyve edge-to-cloud systems (Edge IoT, ~20% of revenue), but the equity is a play on nearline HDD demand from AI data creation. Acquired Intevac (thin-film processing systems) on 2025-03-31, deepening the upstream toolset (FY25 10-K).

Position in the value chain

Seagate is the vertically integrated manufacturer in the HDD chain: it builds its own recording heads and media in-house (a limited number of independent head/media suppliers exist; vertical integration lowers unit cost in up-cycles but raises underutilization charges in down-cycles — FY25 10-K), assembles drives largely in China/Thailand/Malaysia/Singapore/N. Ireland, and sells nearline/mass-capacity HDDs to hyperscalers and enterprise. Its differentiator is being ahead of WDC on HAMR: Seagate is already shipping Mozaic 3/4 HAMR for revenue and ramping 40TB-class drives in 2H CY26, whereas WDC has not yet qualified HAMR and ramps 44TB only in H1 CY27 (MS/Woodring equity call, 2026-06-16). HDDs are the lowest-TCO medium for the cold/warm petabyte-to-exabyte tier vs the NAND complex.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q3 FY26 (March 2026 quarter, reported 2026-04-28) — beat-and-raise. Revenue $3.1B, +44% y/y / +10% q/q; non-GAAP gross margin 47% (+180bps q/q, +480bps y/y); non-GAAP operating margin 37.5%; non-GAAP EPS $4.10 (+115% y/y). GAAP figures from the 10-Q (period ended 2026-04-03): revenue $3,112M, net income $748M, diluted EPS $3.27, GAAP gross margin 46.5%, operating cash flow $1.1B (10-Q, 2026-04-29). FCF $953M (~31% margin), highest in over a decade; capex $151M (~4% of revenue). Exabytes shipped 199 EB, +39% y/y, on flat units — growth is areal-density, not capacity-add ("increasing areal density over unit volumes"). Data center 175 EB (88% of exabytes, 80% of revenue), data center revenue $2.5B (+55% y/y); revenue/TB up mid-single-digits y/y. Balance sheet de-levered: gross debt $3.9B, net leverage 0.7x, and Fitch upgraded STX to investment grade (all Q3 FY26 call, 2026-04-28).

Technology / contracts: Mozaic 4 (4+TB/disk HAMR) began shipping for revenue late March, expected to be the majority of HAMR exabytes by end CY26; 75% of leading global cloud customers now on Mozaic for revenue; two largest CSPs qualified on 4+TB/disk. Mozaic 5 (50TB) "well to plan," qual shipments late 2027. Nearline almost fully allocated through CY2027; top-3 CSP remaining performance obligations nearly doubled to $1.1T (Q3 FY26 call, 2026-04-28).

Guidance — Q4 FY26 (June quarter): revenue $3.45B ±$100M (+41% y/y midpoint), op margin "lower 40% range," non-GAAP EPS $5.00 ±$0.20 (Q3 FY26 call). Long-term annual revenue growth target RAISED to "at least 20%" (from low-to-mid-teens at Q4 FY25), on demand acceleration + CY27 visibility. Trajectory in context: EPS has gone Q4 FY25 $2.59 → Q1 $2.61 → Q2 $3.11 → Q3 $4.10, GM 37.9% → 40.1% → 42.2% → 47% (transcripts 2025-07-29, 2025-10-28, 2026-01-27, 2026-04-28).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: MS's Erik Woodring (equity call, 2026-06-16) has Seagate as his top pick across HDDs and "raised numbers materially, again" on Asia checks. The mechanism is pricing: HDD vendors are targeting ~$25/TB blended nearline in CY27 and ~$30 in CY28 vs ~$14.50/TB today (potential to roughly double pricing in two years via sequential escalators); non-LTA customers already paying ~+30% q/q and distributor/NeoCloud "spot" at $30-35/TB. His STX CY27 EPS = $52 (base), $66 bull, and "if we took our checks as fact... more than $70 in CY27, more than $100 in CY28" → sub-10x on CY28 bull. STX's idiosyncratic near-term edge: the original Google HAMR "sweetheart" contract is believed to reprice higher from June into Sept-26 (incremental pricing/GM tailwind WDC doesn't get), and STX ramps 40TB HAMR aggressively in 2H CY26 vs WDC's 44TB in H1 CY27 (Woodring, 2026-06-16). Corroborating: JPM/Samik (equity call, 2026-06-12) on ~90% sequential incremental gross margins, STX guiding opex ~$300M/qtr (operating leverage), and "no need to give pricing back on generation changes" in a supply-constrained market. STX's own framing: demand broadening to AI inferencing/training, agentic AI and physical AI (Q3 FY26 call).
  • Bear: (1) GS desk prefers WDC to STX — Peter Callahan (GS, briefing 2026-06-18): "GS desk firmly WDC > STX," with WDC +48% in June vs STX "only" +27%; the tactical momentum/relative-value trade has favored WDC. (2) Peak-earnings optics — supply caps exabyte growth at ~30-35%/yr, so the cycle elongates rather than adding near-term volume; Woodring's own duration worry: if order/unit growth decelerates while EPS rises, the multiple de-rates anyway (2026-06-16). (3) HDD-vs-memory valuation debate — FinTwit (@MF_Camillus, briefing 2026-06-16) frames HDD at "7-10x the valuation" of memory as the bull case, but the reverse read is that the gap resolves toward DRAM/SSD as NAND scales. (4) NAND/flash substitution in lower-capacity/legacy form factors (FY25 10-K). (5) Vertical-integration downside — higher unit costs and underutilization charges when demand declines (as in FY23/FY24) (FY25 10-K).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: MS (Woodring) Overweight HDDs, STX top pick (1A), WDC 1B — both ~11-12x on bull numbers (2026-06-16). GS desk WDC > STX tactically (Callahan, 2026-06-18). STX a standing name on Rothschild 12-month relative-high screens alongside WDC/AMAT/ASML (briefings 2026-06-17/18) and in the HDD/memory cohort flagged on the briefings. JPM/Samik constructive on the HDD pricing/operating-leverage setup (2026-06-12). No on-disk Outlook desk notes specific to STX this window (see Sources).

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q4 FY26 print (next earnings, ~late July 2026): guide $3.45B / "lower 40%" op margin / $5.00 EPS — watch GM trajectory and the first CY27 LTA pricing disclosures.
  • Google/Mozaic HAMR contract reprice — Woodring expects the original HAMR "sweetheart" deal to reprice higher from June into Sept-26; the single clearest near-term GM/EPS swing factor for STX (2026-06-16).
  • Mozaic 4 ramp + 40TB HAMR — 2H CY26 volume ramp; conversion of the remaining CSP qualifications (75% of leading cloud customers already on Mozaic for revenue). Mozaic 5 (50TB) qual shipments late 2027.
  • CY27 nearline pricing — escalators toward the $25/$30/TB targets; "almost fully allocated through CY2027" with pricing still being set.
  • Exabyte demand breadth — confirmation AI training/inference/physical-AI sustains the cycle. Read-through in hbm-memory and ai-datacenter-power.

Risks

  • Technology-transition execution — HAMR/Mozaic areal-density ramp must stay cost-effective; the FY25 10-K flags failure to advance areal density / technology transitions as a key competitive risk.
  • Customer concentration — one customer was ~10% of consolidated revenue in FY25; demand is driven by hyperscaler capex (FY25 10-K). Top-3 CSP RPO of $1.1T concentrates both upside and risk.
  • ASP / demand cyclicality — "intense competition" and demand variations; vertical integration amplifies underutilization charges when systems demand falls (FY25 10-K). Peak-pricing optics if exabyte growth decelerates (Woodring, 2026-06-16).
  • Flash / SSD substitution — NAND-based solutions complement and compete, especially as solid-state advances in many end markets (FY25 10-K).
  • China / tariffs / geopolitics — most assembly and substantial sales are ex-US (China, Thailand, Malaysia, Singapore, N. Ireland); changes in US trade policy/tariffs may materially impact operations (FY25 10-K, 10-Q 2026-04-29 "Recent Developments").

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • Morgan Stanley (Erik Woodring) — 'HDDs: The Next Leg Higher' _(2026-06-15)_ + call recap _(2026-06-16)_: nearline pricing escalators + HAMR ramp; Seagate the HAMR front-runner (MS top pick).
  • JPM 'Bits, Broadcasts & Bandwidth' _(2026-05-29)_: Seagate in the improving-storage-pricing ecosystem.

ADI — Analog Devices, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\ADI (10-K FY2025, 10-Q FQ2 FY26, transcripts) · E:\equity_calls_transcripts · E:\briefings\2026. Master index: 00_INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Analog Devices is one of the world's largest high-performance analog / mixed-signal semiconductor companies — ICs that sense, measure, interpret, connect and power the physical world (data converters, amplifiers, power management, RF/microwave, edge processors, isolation, sensors/MEMS). FY2025 revenue $11.0bn, +17% y/y (10-K, 2025-11-25), mix: Industrial 45%, Automotive 30%, Consumer 13%, Communications 13% (10-K FY25). The franchise is built on sticky, long-life-cycle catalog analog with very high margins (FY25 EPS +20%+); the live story for a tech/semis book is the broad-based industrial cyclical recovery layered on a data-center growth engine (AI ATE, datacenter power, optical connectivity) now ~20% of revenue / ~$2bn run-rate (Q1 FY26 call, 2026-02-18). Diversified-analog peer set: TXN, NXPI, ON, MCHP, IFX, STM, MPWR.

Position in the value chain

ADI runs a hybrid manufacturing model: it operates internal wafer fabs (Wilmington MA, Camas WA, Beaverton OR, Limerick Ireland) but sources more than half its annual wafer requirements from third-party foundries — chiefly TSMC — plus internal/subcontract assembly & test (10-K FY25). It sits in the middle of the chain: buys wafers/substrates upstream, designs and manufactures high-performance analog/mixed-signal/power ICs, and sells into industrial, automotive, comms/datacenter and instrumentation customers. The incremental driver is AI datacenter power — the $1.5bn Empower Semiconductor deal (IVR + silicon caps for vertical power delivery into accelerators) extends ADI into the GPU/ASIC power stack, with ADI already shipping VPD into an ASIC customer "believed to be Google's TPU" (MS, briefing 2026-05-20).

Current state (latest quarter)

FQ2 FY26 (quarter ended 2026-05-02, call 2026-05-20): Revenue $3.62bn, +15% q/q, +37% y/y — above the high end of guide. Non-GAAP EPS $3.09 (record), +67% y/y; non-GAAP gross margin 73.0% (+360 bps y/y), operating margin 49% (+780 bps y/y). On a GAAP basis the 10-Q shows gross margin 67.3%, operating income $1,379.7m, net income $1,176.4m, diluted EPS $2.40 (10-Q, 2026-05-20). End markets: Industrial $1.80bn (+20% q/q, +56% y/y; A&D/ATE/energy strong); Automotive $872m (+8% q/q, +2% y/y; BMS back to y/y growth first time in 2 years); Communications $555m (+22% q/q, +79% y/y; datacenter ~75% of comms, +>90% y/y); Consumer $398m (flat q/q, +23% y/y) (10-Q + call). FQ3 (Jul) guide ~$3.9bn / EPS ~$3.30 vs Street ~$3.62bn / $3.01 — an ~8% revenue beat to consensus and a ~9th consecutive above-seasonal quarter (GS/JPM/Citi recaps, 2026-05-20/21); gross margin guided -50 bps as one-time channel-repricing rolls off. Announced Empower Semiconductor for $1.5bn all-cash (close ~2H CY26; minimal FY26, "significant revenue" 2027). TTM FCF $4.6bn (36% of rev); declared dividend $1.10/qtr (paid 2026-06-16, ~$535.8m) (10-Q). Capacity supports the $20bn revenue target by 2030.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: Cleanest read on a broadening industrial cyclical recovery plus a real datacenter growth layer. JPM (Harlan Sur) calls ADI "top pick in the broad-based diversified semiconductor segment," PT $450 (from $400), OW — industrial recovery broadening beyond ATE/A&D, auto on a path to double-digit y/y in FY26, Empower closing a vertical-power gap for AI accelerators (briefing 2026-05-23). Jefferies (Blayne Curtis) raised to $475 Buy ("Cooking with Gas," record bookings, comms +90% y/y on DC); Citi (Atif Malik) to $460 Buy (28x C27 ~$16, Empower layers DC-power share '27); MS (Joseph Moore) to $428 OW (9th consecutive above-seasonal q, "more room to run") (briefing 2026-05-21). Equity-call framing: ADI is the buy-side "quality" analog name alongside TXN, with ~20-25% AI/DC exposure that is "not so overt" you get socket-by-socket GPU/ASIC noise (Vivek Arya/BofA, equity call 2026-04-23); Curtis: "ADI has been our favorite idea... best end-market exposures" (equity call 2026-05-22).
  • Bear: Overcrowded and well-owned at peak expectations — the print was "good enough but not a strong positive catalyst for a very well owned name," "a bit of a knife fight on elevated expectations" (Barclays desk / Jefferies sales, briefing 2026-05-20). The pair-trade structure is the actionable bear: investors are increasingly long TXN / short ADI — TXN is earlier in recovery with more GM torque and the chance to "double" datacenter-power exposure, vs ADI which is already one of the highest (Barclays Rand, briefings 2026-06-03 & 2026-06-15; Citi Malik flags TXN gaining DC-power share vs IFX/MPWR/Renesas). UBS (Tim Arcuri) is Buy but prefers TXN/MCHP for operating leverage / later-downcycle play (PT $490, briefing 2026-05-19). DB (Ross Seymore) stays Hold $365 — "firing on all cylinders but premium valuation already reflects it" (briefing 2026-05-21). Pricing-led growth (channel repricing) and the FQ3 -50 bps GM step-down are watch items.
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands (post-print): JPM $450 OW · Jefferies $475 Buy · Citi $460 Buy · UBS $490 Buy (prefers TXN) · MS $428 OW · GS $415 Buy · DB $365 Hold (briefings 2026-05-19 → 05-23). Long-only managers were underweight every analog name and have been selling since 2021 peak ownership — a positioning tailwind if they mean-revert to benchmark (Vivek positioning chart, equity call 2026-04-23).

Catalysts / what to watch

  • FQ3 FY26 print (~Aug 2026): delivery vs the ~$3.9bn / $3.30 guide; whether the above-seasonal streak extends and the -50 bps GM step-down lands as guided.
  • Empower Semiconductor close (~2H CY26): integration detail; FY27 vertical-power-delivery ramp into AI accelerators (TPU/GPU power) and read-through vs MPWR/IFX.
  • Datacenter-power share narrative: the TXN-long / ADI-short pair trade resolves on relative DC-power content gains (Barclays/Citi, briefings 2026-06-03/15); BofA pegs AI-DC analog TAM ~$8bn (2025) → ~$27bn by 2030 (~28% CAGR), ADI among beneficiaries (briefing 2026-06-09).
  • Auto inflection: path to double-digit y/y FY26 (BMS, ADAS, GMSL) as China stabilizes.
  • Pricing: roll-off of one-time channel repricing vs durable list-price gains (ADI took price Jan + Apr per distributor checks; equity call 2026-04-23).

Risks

  • Cyclicality / crowding: very well-owned analog leader at peak expectations; pricing-led upside (channel repricing) partly non-recurring (Barclays/Jefferies, briefing 2026-05-20; Q1/Q2 FY26 calls).
  • China & export controls: significant China revenue and operations; expanding US export restrictions, Section 232 semiconductor investigation, tariffs and retaliation are explicit 10-K risks that have already impacted China results (10-K FY25, Item 1A).
  • Foundry concentration: >50% of wafers outsourced, primarily to TSMC — Taiwan Strait / supply-chain and capacity-allocation exposure (10-K FY25).
  • Tariffs / macro: auto pull-in unwind on tariffs/macro hit FQ1 FY26 auto sequentially "well below seasonal" (Q1 FY26 call); broad trade-policy volatility (10-K).
  • Datacenter-power share loss: TXN/IFX/MPWR competing for the same 800V / vertical-power content; share, not TAM, is the swing (Citi/Barclays, briefings 2026-06-03/15).
  • Integration / valuation: Empower deal execution; premium multiple already discounts the recovery (DB Hold, briefing 2026-05-21).

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • 'VIVEK on Analog' expert call _(2026-06-09)_ + BofA TMT 'Analog Semis Call, APH CEO' _(2026-06-09)_: analog up-cycle dynamics; ADI the premium franchise — the TXN-long / ADI-short pair-trade is the live debate.
  • BofA Tech Conference lighting round _(2026-06-08)_: analog recovery + datacenter-power content (Empower/AI-power).

  • Outlook: not separately pulled for this page — the outlook.py launcher is not callable here (no working python alias; only py/WindowsApps python3). Sell-side stance is sourced from the dated briefing archive, which itself is built from the Outlook tape.

TXN — Texas Instruments Incorporated

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\TXN (10-K/10-Q + transcripts) · _equity_calls · E:\briefings\2026. Master index: 00_INDEX.md.

Snapshot

The world's largest catalog analog + embedded-processing semiconductor IDM — ~100,000 products, >80% direct-to-customer (incl. TI.com), longevity/diversity of sockets the moat. FY2025 revenue $17.68bn across two reportable segments: Analog (~81% of revenue) and Embedded Processing (~15%), plus Other (~4%) (10-K, 2026-02-06). End markets: Industrial 33%, Automotive 33%, Personal electronics 21%, Data center compute 9%, Communications equipment 3%, calculators ~1% (10-K, 2026-02-06). Industrial + automotive + data center now ~75% of revenue, up from 43% in 2013 (Q4 FY25 call, 2026-01-27). The objective management manages to is free-cash-flow-per-share growth over the long term; the live debate is whether the just-completed 300mm internal-fab capex super-cycle now converts to a steep FCF/share inflection as capex rolls over.

Position in the value chain

TXN is a fully internalized IDM: it runs its own 300mm wafer fabs (Richardson & Sherman TX, Lehi UT) — an unpackaged 300mm chip costs ~40% less than 200mm — plus internal assembly/test, selectively supplementing with foundries; it designs and builds catalog analog + embedded-processing chips and sells direct into industrial, automotive, personal electronics, and enterprise/data center (10-K, 2026-02-06). The capital story sits squarely in the middle box: ~$109bn allocated 2016–2025, the 300mm build now largely done, and capex guided down to $2–3bn for 2026 (Q1 FY26 call, 2026-04-22) from a ~$5bn peak — the swing factor behind the $8 FCF/share target (Ilan, Q1 FY26 call).

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 FY2026 (quarter ended 2026-03-31, call 2026-04-22): Revenue $4.825bn (+19% YoY, +9% QoQ) — beat the $4.32–4.68bn guide. GM 58% (+210bps QoQ); operating profit $1.808bn (37.5%); net income $1.545bn; diluted EPS $1.68 (vs $1.36 guide midpoint). Segments: Analog $3.924bn (+22% YoY, 41.7% op margin), Embedded Processing $0.723bn (+12% YoY, 16.9%), Other $0.178bn (10-Q, 2026-04-24). End markets: industrial +30% YoY/+20% QoQ ("5 or 6 months of continued growth," Ilan); data center ~+90% YoY/+25% QoQ; comms +25% YoY; automotive mid-single-digit YoY (China down, RoW up); PE flat YoY. Capex cut to $2–3bn for 2026 (Lizardi) — Q1 quarterly capex $676m vs $1,123m a year ago. TTM FCF $4.4bn (vs $1.7bn a year ago), op cash flow $7.8bn; $6.0bn returned to shareholders TTM. FCF/share: "as long as revenue is growing mid- to high single digits, that $8 of free cash flow per share is very probable" (Ilan). Pricing flat YoY/QoQ; "prices may go up in the second half" (Ilan). Q2 FY26 guide: revenue $5.0–5.4bn, EPS $1.77–2.05 (Q1 FY26 call, 2026-04-22).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: The favorite long in analog — earlier in the recovery than ADI, so more positive revisions ahead, with strong GM torque if the cycle is real (Barclays/Rand desk, briefing 2026-06-03; 2026-05-20). The completed 300mm super-cycle is the FCF story: capex $5bn → $2–3bn while revenue inflects = path to ~$8 FCF/share (Ilan, Q1 FY26 call). Data center is the new growth leg: ~$1.2bn run-rate in 2025 (Q3 FY25 call), +~90% YoY in Q1 FY26 — and TXN claims it can fill "almost every analog socket on the rack." BofA's Vivek Arya makes TXN a Buy with AI sales potentially >3x to $4.5bn LT and calls it best-positioned analog share gainer in the 800V data-center era (BofA, briefing 2026-05-26). Citi reiterates TXN top pick on the 800V transition, expecting DC-power share gains in 2H26 vs Infineon/MPWR/Renesas; internal high-voltage GaN capacity quadrupled since Oct'24 (Citi/Atif Malik, briefing 2026-06-15). Redburn Buy on broadening 5–15% mature-node price rises and AI-DC supply tightness (R&Co Redburn, briefings 2026-05-12 / 2026-05-20).
  • Bear: GS rates TXN Sell (PT framed off 32x normalized $13 EPS), reading ADI's strong print as a risk that pushes TXN estimates up against the Sell call — i.e. valuation is the bear (GS/Schneider, briefing 2026-05-20). The 800V/DC-power tailwind may be over-hyped: channel checks with DC construction PMs show "no plans to do this anytime soon," TXN "specifically vocal about this," 2–3 year lag before it hits earnings, and China can flood GaN/SiC like it did InP (@bubbleboi, briefing 2026-06-15). Stock is technically extended — TXN on the 12m-relative-highs list with semis ~63% above the 200dma, "the largest margin since the Dot-Com bubble" (R&Co Redburn/Adley, briefing 2026-05-12). Loadings/depreciation drag GM when revenue dips (Lizardi, Q3/Q4 FY25 calls), and industrial "ran a little hot" in 2025 on possible tariff pull-ins (Ilan, Q2 FY25 call).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: Two-sided but tilting bull on the cycle. BofA Buy (Arya, raised PT), Citi top pick (Malik), Redburn Buy (Adley), vs GS Sell (Schneider, $415-area on 32x normalized $13) and DB Hold $240 (Seymore, neutral on the CFO transition). The crowded buy-side trade is long TXN / short ADI — TXN earlier in recovery, more numbers upside in industrial; a June BofA-type investor survey shows analog preference shifting to IFX 25% / TXN 25%, displacing ADI 13% (briefing 2026-06-17; Barclays/Rand, briefings 2026-06-03 / 2026-06-15).

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2 FY26 print — call ~2026-07-22 (guide $5.0–5.4bn / EPS $1.77–2.05); first call with new CFO Julie Knecht (DB, briefing 2026-06-03).
  • CFO transition: Rafael Lizardi out 2026-08-01 → Julie Knecht (CAO, 25-yr veteran), advisory through 2026-08-31 (DB/Seymore, briefing 2026-06-03).
  • 2H26 pricing: management flagged mature-node prices "may go up"; Redburn already sees 5–15% rises broadening (Q1 FY26 call; briefing 2026-05-12).
  • 800V DC-power share data points: Citi expects share gains in 2H26 vs IFX/MPWR/Renesas — watch GaN ramp and rack content disclosure (Citi, briefing 2026-06-15).
  • Capex / FCF trajectory: confirmation of the $2–3bn 2026 capex and the bridge to ~$8 FCF/share (Q1 FY26 call).

Risks

  • Cyclicality: rapid demand swings in analog/embedded; large-customer concentration in the top 50 (10-K, 2026-02-06).
  • Underutilization / depreciation: new 300mm fabs lift depreciation; idle/under-loaded capacity is expensed as incurred — direct GM drag in soft quarters (10-K; Lizardi, Q3/Q4 FY25 calls).
  • China: ~20% of revenue from China; ~50% of revenue from products shipped into China — tariff/export-control/embargo exposure (10-K, 2026-02-06). China automotive weak in Q1 FY26.
  • Tariffs / trade: tariffs, export restrictions, sanctions and trade barriers specific to semis; demand pull-in/normalization risk (10-K; Q2 FY25 call).
  • 800V/DC-power timing: adoption may lag hype 2–3 years; potential China GaN/SiC oversupply (@bubbleboi, briefing 2026-06-15).
  • Valuation: trading at 12m relative highs; GS Sell on normalized-EPS multiple (briefings 2026-05-12 / 2026-05-20).

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • JPM (Samik) 'Watts Up! Shift to 800V' _(2026-06-09)_: TXN among DC-power-stack share gainers (Citi's 800V top pick).
  • 'VIVEK on Analog' _(2026-06-09)_ + BofA analog call _(2026-06-09)_: cyclical recovery; valuation the bear (GS Sell).
