CSCO — Cisco Systems

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\CSCO (filings + transcripts) · _equity_calls · _briefings\by-ticker\CSCO.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Snapshot

Cisco is the incumbent enterprise/SP networking franchise re-rating into an AI-infrastructure story. Revenue is grouped into Networking (switching, routing, wireless, servers — the largest bucket and the standout grower), Security (incl. Splunk), Collaboration, and Observability, plus a large services/subscription annuity (FY26-FY10-K, 2025-09-03). Subscription was ~49% of total revenue and software ~$5.7B in Q3 FY26 (Q3 FY26 call, 2026-05-13). The new edge is twofold: (1) AI infrastructure for hyperscalers — Silicon One ASICs/systems + Acacia coherent pluggable optics; (2) a multiyear enterprise campus refresh (switching/WiFi 7/routing). 650 Group frames CSCO as one of three volume-scale data-center switch players alongside NVDA and Arista/Celestica (650 Group, 2026-06-04).

Current state (latest quarter — Q3 FY26, reported 2026-05-13)

  • P&L: record revenue $15.8B, +12% YoY, above high end of guide; product revenue +17%, services -1% (contract-start timing). Non-GAAP EPS $1.06, +10%; non-GAAP op margin 34.2% (Q3 FY26 call).
  • Orders — the headline: total product orders +35% YoY; ex-hyperscaler +19%; enterprise +18%, public sector +27%, SP/cloud +105% (5 top hyperscalers each triple-digit). Networking orders +50%+, 7th straight double-digit quarter. All geos accelerating (Americas +35%, EMEA +39%, APJC +25%) (Q3 FY26 call).
  • AI infra: hyperscaler AI orders $1.9B in Q3 (vs $600M yr-ago); YTD $5.3B already exceeds the prior $5B FY26 target with a quarter left. Management raised FY26 hyperscaler AI orders to ~$9B (4.5x FY25) and ~$4B AI revenue. Acacia >$1B orders in Q3, on track to >200% YoY; 5 new hyperscaler design wins (2 optics, 3 systems incl. first two Silicon One P200 scale-across wins, plus a third in early Q4). Neocloud/sovereign/enterprise AI: ~$300M orders Q3, ~$900M YTD, ~$3B pipeline (Q3 FY26 call).
  • Margins / mix: non-GAAP GM 66.0% (-260bps YoY); product GM 64.3% (-330bps) on mix + memory cost. Mgmt: GM stabilized (Q4 guide midpoint also ~66%); mix (hardware ~30% growth vs software ~1%) is the bigger drag than memory. Op leverage protected — OpEx fell from 34.1% to 31.9% of rev YoY, holding ~34% op margin (Patterson, Q3 FY26).
  • Guidance: Q4 FY26 rev $16.7–16.9B, EPS $1.16–1.18, op margin 34–35%. FY26 rev $62.8–63.0B, EPS $4.27–4.29. Restructuring announced (~4,000 jobs / ~5% of workforce per 2026-05-15 briefing) — up to $1B pretax charge ($450M in Q4), explicitly reallocation toward silicon/optics/security/AI, not a savings play (Patterson, Q3 FY26).
  • FY27 early frame: Patterson — expect ≥$6B hyperscaler AI revenue in FY27; rest of portfolio grows in line with the 4-6% long-term model (Q3 FY26 Q&A). GS sees F2027 framework >$68B rev (Mike Ng, briefing 2026-05-14).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull:
    • Silicon One is the moat — "if you don't have silicon, you're going to struggle to be relevant to hyperscalers" (Robbins, Q3 FY26). Gives supply-chain control: secured silicon through CY26, no decommits, +$11.6B YoY inventory/APC build. 650 Group confirms CSCO has parallel supply vectors vs the Broadcom ecosystem and is "one of the better supply-chain managed companies" (650 Group, 2026-06-04).
    • Acacia/optics inflection: shipped >750k 400G + >40k 800G coherent pluggables; 650 Group "very bullish" on the 1.6T PAM4 receiver — first inside-the-DC organic optic — millions of units, multibillion-$ opportunity" (650 Group, 2026-06-04; JPM/650 Group briefing 2026-06-08).
    • Campus refresh in early innings — record campus orders +25%, wireless +40% (WiFi 7 = half the mix); 650 Group: ~12-18 month cycle with CSCO gaining share at Juniper's expense; revenue outgrowing units on price (650 Group, 2026-06-04). Citi calls campus "early innings" (Malik, briefing 2026-05-14).
    • Sell-side OW/Buy cluster: MS OW PT $120 (Marshall — "AI rev to 4x FY26, ≥50% YoY FY27"); Citi Buy PT $112 at 22x (Malik, FY26-28 EPS +3/7/8%); UBS Buy (Vogt upgrade validated) (briefing 2026-05-14, morning table 2026-06-13).
    • GS framing: print is "Validation of Hyperscaler Capex" + "Networking as the New AI Bottleneck" — reduces AI air-pocket fear (Armbrust/Ng, briefing 2026-05-15). Cramer/firstadopter pushing back on dot-com analogies; buyers are profitable incumbents (Robbins, briefing 2026-05-16).
  • Bear:
    • Valuation + sustainability. Barclays' Tim Long stays EW (PT raised $76→$121) — "AI Orders Drive Beat and Raise, But Sustainability More of a Question"; stock ~25x CY27 EPS vs historical ~15x; implied AI multiple ~2x ANET's; he prefers OW-rated ANET (PT $195) in the campus/AI pair (briefing 2026-05-15, 2026-05-21).
    • Pull-forward risk. Some ex-webscale acceleration (19% vs 10% prior Q) is price, not units — ~4-5pts of the 9pt acceleration was pure pricing (Patterson, Q3 FY26). 650 Group: real price headroom thins once the refresh moves past price-insensitive early adopters (650 Group, 2026-06-04). JPM noted "some grumbling about pull-forward" (Meyers, briefing 2026-05-15).
    • AI orders are nonlinear / lumpy — Q3 $1.9B dipped below Q2's $2.1B; the implied ~$3.7B Q4 hinges on timing of large wins (Rakers Q&A, Q3 FY26). Conservatism on the prior $5B target suggests low visibility.
    • Margin mix drag persists as hardware AI revenue scales (~$4B→$6B+) vs flat software; security still soft; Splunk on-prem→cloud transition a near-term revenue drag into FY27 (Robbins/Patterson, Q3 FY26).
    • Redburn at Neutral (briefing 2026-06-03).
  • Where the sell-side stands: Net constructive post-print — OW/Buy: MS ($120), Citi ($112), UBS, JPM (reit. OW); GS positive; EW: Barclays ($121, valuation). The open debates the whole desk converges on: (1) durability of the AI-order run-rate into FY27, (2) GM trajectory on hardware mix + memory, (3) is the multiple stretched (briefing 2026-05-14/15).

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q4 FY26 print — Wed Aug 12, 2026 (AMC) — first formal FY27 guide; watch whether AI orders sustain a ~$2-3.7B/qtr run-rate and the FY27 ≥$6B AI-rev frame is confirmed (Sami Badri close, Q3 FY26).
  • Cisco Live Las Vegas content (May 31–Jun 4) already digested — Cloud Control, agentic ops, sovereign portfolio (briefing 2026-06-03/08).
  • Silicon One P200 scale-across ramp — early Q4 orders, "at scale" only in FY27 (Robbins, Q3 FY26). CPO into Silicon One 102T/51.2T is the next milestone; OCP (~Oct) is the scale-up show (650 Group, 2026-06-04).
  • Acacia 1.6T PAM4 receiver / 800G LPO ramp inside the data center (650 Group, 2026-06-08).
  • Restructuring execution — remaining charges into FY27; reallocation to silicon/optics/security/AI.
  • Memory market — DDR4→DDR5 conversions, 20+ memory-reduction programs, Nanya 3-yr supply agreement (Patterson, Q3 FY26; JPM, briefing 2026-05-13).

Risks

  • Multiple compression if hyperscaler AI orders prove lumpy/peakish — bulk of the bull case rides on a sustained run-rate the company itself calls nonlinear.
  • Product mix + memory cost weighing on product gross margin; reliance on OpEx discipline to hold ~34% op margin.
  • Hyperscaler concentration in AI orders (5 customers driving SP/cloud +105%); design-win timing opaque.
  • Splunk cloud transition + legacy-security decline continue to drag the software/security line into FY27.
  • Supply: 3nm-class switch ASICs and laser/InP for transceivers are the genuine constraints (memory is costly but available) — incremental orders most exposed (650 Group, 2026-06-04).
  • Competition broadening in DC switching (HPE, Nokia, Juniper gaining in a >$100B TAM) and the structural AI-networking pair-trade vs ANET (650 Group, 2026-06-04; Barclays, briefing 2026-05-21).
  • Tariff/exemption assumptions baked into guidance; macro on enterprise/public-sector IT budgets (10-K risk factors, 2025-09-03).

Consensus estimates (BBG)

CRDO — Credo Technology Group Holding Ltd

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\CRDO (filings + transcripts) · _briefings\2026\* (heavily covered, ~42 hits). Master index: 00_INDEX.md. Themes: optical-cpo · custom-asic-tpu.

Snapshot

Fabless connectivity-IC supplier built to "transform connectivity at scale through fast, reliable and energy-efficient system solutions" for AI data centers (FY26 10-K, 2026-06-15). Sells high-speed copper and optical interconnect up to 1.6T: ZeroFlap (ZF) Active Electrical Cables (AECs), ZF optical transceivers, optical and PCIe retimers/DSPs, SerDes chiplets, OmniConnect memory solutions, and SerDes-IP licensing — all on its own SerDes/DSP technology with an "n-1" (legacy-node) cost advantage. Optimized for Ethernet/PCIe and emerging UALink, ESUN, SUE scale-up fabrics across Front-End, Scale-Out, Scale-Up and Scale-In networks. FY26 (ended ~Apr 2026) revenue $1.3B, +206% y/y (vs $436.8M FY25), non-GAAP GM 68.1%, non-GAAP NI $662M (FY26 10-K; Q4 call 2026-06-01). The story is a copper-to-optical-plus-AEC AI connectivity ramp with extreme customer concentration: top-10 = ~90% of FY26 revenue, two customers >10% (Customer A 49%, Customer B 32% per 10-K) — Amazon is a confirmed customer (holds a Credo warrant) and Microsoft is named in the filing.

Position in the value chain

Credo is a fabless designer between the foundry/IP supply base and the hyperscalers. It owns no fabs — TSMC produces the wafers, third parties package/assemble/test — and it sells AEC active-copper cables, SerDes/DSP retimers, optical DSPs and chiplet connectivity to the large hyperscalers (Amazon, Microsoft) plus a fifth hyperscaler and a growing Neocloud/Tier-2 tail. Its differentiated position is active copper (AECs) for short-reach scale-up plus optical for longer reach — straddling the copper-vs-optical debate rather than betting purely on one (themes optical-cpo, custom-asic-tpu).

Current state (latest quarter)

Q4 FY26 (reported 2026-06-01 AMC; quarter ended ~April 2026), capping FY26:

  • Q4 revenue $437M, +7% q/q, +157% y/y — only ~$5M above ~$432M Street (thin top-line beat). Non-GAAP GM 68.3% vs ~64.9% cons (big margin beat); non-GAAP NI $226.7M; operating margin 49.6%; FCF $177.5M; cash $1.4B (Q4 call 2026-06-01; briefing 2026-06-02).
  • Non-GAAP EPS $1.16 vs ~$1.02 cons (+14%, entirely margin-driven) — Vital Knowledge: "157% y/y growth, only ~$5M above Street... top-line upside isn't enough" near-term (2026-06-01).
  • FY26 full year: revenue $1.3B (+206% y/y), non-GAAP NI $662M (>5x), non-GAAP GM 68.1%, EPS $3.46 (+392%) (Q4 call).
  • Customer concentration: four customers ≥10% of Q4 (34% / 27% / 16% / 10%) — broadening from three; Neoclouds/Tier-2 guided to ~20% of revenue over coming years.
  • Guidance — Q1 FY27: $465–$475M, GM 67–69%, opex $86–$90M. FY27: revenue >80% y/y (raised from >50% at the Q3 call), GM ~flat, opex +~50%, net margin ~50%.
  • Optical raised to >$600M FY27 (from >$500M): optical DSPs + SiPho PICs (Dust Photonics) + ZeroFlap optics, each >$100M and each +80%+ y/y; FY28 optical floor ~$1.2B. Dust Photonics funded with ~$750M net cash in Q1. Supply chain "significant tightness"; 200G/lane ramp light in FY27, inflection 2H.
  • Stock reaction: -11.5% to -15% on the print despite the beat-and-raise (briefings 2026-06-01/02) — buyside bars missed on revenue; "frontier model operator slowing deployments" per JPM 3P checks.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull:
    • Optical is the second leg and it's inflecting. Jefferies (Blayne Curtis) Buy, PT $225 → $270 (2026-06-01): "AEC far from over, but Optics is the real driver"; FY27 optical >$600M, ~doubles in F28; upside case $13 EPS C28, PT $390. Barclays (O'Malley) OW $260: optical DSPs the biggest upside surprise, FY28 optical ~$1.6B (2026-06-01).
    • GM structurally superior — non-GAAP GM ~68% with operating margin ~50% and net margin guided ~50% FY27, rare for a connectivity-IC name; "GM outstripping expectations" (GS Schneider, Buy, 2026-06-01).
    • Straddles copper and optical — @crux_capital_ (2026-06-19) puts CRDO in the "stronger" connectivity bucket (copper+optics) alongside SMTC/ALAB/MTSI/MXL/MRVL, vs the "weaker" pure-play optics (LITE/AAOI/FN/CIEN) exposed to China oversupply. Customer diversification underway (3→4 >10% customers; Neoclouds to ~20%). CRDO at 12-month relative highs per Rothschild (2026-06-19).
  • Bear:
    • Revenue, not margin, drives the stock — and the top line is only OK. JPM desk (Joshua Meyers, 2026-06-01): "-11.5%... rev and guide both missed buyside bars; frontier model operator slowing deployments; show-me territory for a while"; ~$200 = 22.2x F28E $9 (buyside range $8–10). BEP: "the market repriced the quality of the beat — who the revenue comes from, and when the next leg arrives."
    • Copper-vs-optical narrative risk. @Midnight_Captl (repeated May 12–13): "Jensen is telegraphing photonics — Lumentum, Coherent, Corning. Not Credo. Not copper." Jefferies' Jeff Pu flagged "AEC headwinds... long-term physical limits beyond 3.2T... likely valued at a discount" (2026-05-11). The CPO/optical rotation periodically prices AECs as the lower-growth leg.
    • Extreme customer concentration — top-10 ~90%, Customer A 49% / Customer B 32% of FY26 (10-K). A single hyperscaler deployment slip (the "frontier model operator" comment) moves the whole print.
  • Where the sell-side stands (post-Q4): Unanimous bulls — GS Buy · Jefferies Buy $270 · Barclays OW $260 · JPM (Cardoso) OW $250 · SIG Positive (briefing 2026-06-02). Consensus: FY27 optical is real, AEC durable, margins superior; near-term "show-me" mode until revenue re-accelerates in 2H FY27.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q1 FY27 print: ~early September 2026 — first read on the $465–475M guide, optical >$600M trajectory, and whether the "frontier model operator" deployment slowdown reverses.
  • Optical ramp pace: ZeroFlap optics (TensorWave shipping; three more in qual), optical DSP to 1.6T, Dust Photonics SiPho PIC integration — the three >$100M legs.
  • AEC roadmap: PCIe Gen 6 AECs to mass production 1H FY27; 200G/lane (light FY27, inflect 2H); fifth hyperscaler + Neocloud/Tier-2 mix toward ~20%.
  • FY28 step-ups: ALCs (active LED cables) and Weaver/OmniConnect gearbox ramping FY28; FY28 optical floor ~$1.2B (mgmt) to ~$1.6B (Barclays).
  • Scale-up standards: UALink / ESUN / SUE adoption (Blue Heron 200G retimer) vs NVLink/Ethernet — determines copper-vs-optical content mix.

Risks

  • Customer concentration — top-10 ~90% of FY26; two customers >10% (Customer A 49%, B 32%); any hyperscaler design-loss or deployment slip is material (FY26 10-K; JPM "frontier model operator slowing deployments," 2026-06-01).
  • Copper displacement / optical rotation — AEC growth questioned at >3.2T physical limits; CPO/photonics narrative periodically prices copper as the loser ("Not Credo. Not copper.").
  • Foundry/supply dependence — fabless; relies on TSMC for wafers (n-1 node) and OSAT for package/assembly/test; "significant tightness in the supply chain" (Q4 call).
  • Valuation / "show-me" — premium multiple (~22x F28E EPS) on a name where the market is repricing the quality, not size, of beats; revenue must re-accelerate to justify the re-rate.
  • Geopolitics / China — tariffs, customs, trade sanctions and cross-border restrictions; meaningful Hong Kong/Mainland shipment destinations in revenue mix (FY26 10-K).
  • Integration risk — Comira (closed) and Dust Photonics (~$750M, Q1 FY27) acquisitions must deliver the optical/IP roadmap.

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • JPM (J. Meyers) — 'Chips for Breakfast: MRVL Rally, … CRDO, HPE, Computex' _(2026-06-02)_: CRDO featured in the AI-connectivity flow.
  • Sell-side PT table _(company-specific, 2026-06-02)_: Jefferies $270 · JPM (Cardoso) $250 · Barclays $260 · GS Buy · SIG Positive — uniformly long; stock sold ~15% post-print on high buyside bars.

Sources

  • Filings: 10-K FY2026 (2026-06-15); 10-Q Q3 FY26 (2026-03-03, qtr ended 2026-01-31).
  • Transcripts: Q4 FY26 (2026-06-01); Q3 FY26 (2026-03-02); Q2 FY26 (2025-12-01); Q1 FY26 (2025-09-03).
  • Briefings: ~42 hits across E:\briefings\2026\* — Q4 print recap + sell-side PT table (2026-06-01/02), copper-vs-optical FinTwit debate (2026-05-12/13/14), connectivity bucketing + 12m relative highs (2026-06-19). JPM "Chips for Breakfast" thread context.
  • Themes: optical-cpo (CRDO in stronger copper+optics bucket vs pure-play optics); custom-asic-tpu (AI back-end/scale-up interconnect read-through).
  • Outlook / sell-side emails: no Outlook tool available in this environment — none pulled directly. Sell-side stances above are quoted from the on-disk briefing roll-ups (JPM, Jefferies, Barclays, GS, SIG, Vital Knowledge), attributed by bank + date.

ALAB — Astera Labs, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\ALAB (filings + transcripts) · E:\briefings\2026\* · Master index: 00_INDEX.md. Themes: custom-asic-tpu · optical-cpo. Note: IPO'd Mar-2024 — short filing history (~9 filings).

Snapshot

Fabless "Intelligent Connectivity Platform" supplier for AI/cloud infrastructure — purpose-built semiconductors that solve data, memory and networking bottlenecks inside the rack (FY25 10-K, 2026-02-20). Four product families on PCIe/Ethernet/CXL standards plus a COSMOS software suite: Aries PCIe/CXL Smart DSP retimers + smart cable modules (signal conditioning), Scorpio Smart Fabric Switches (P-Series scale-out, X-Series scale-up), Taurus Ethernet smart cable modules, and Leo CXL memory controllers. FY2025 revenue $852.5M, +115% y/y, non-GAAP GM 75.7%, net income $219.1M vs. an $83.4M FY24 loss (10-K, 2026-02-20). The story is a pure AI-infrastructure connectivity play levered to hyperscaler capex, with content per accelerator now >$1,000 and rising and a stated SAM expanding ">10x over five years to $25B" (Mohan, Q4-2025 call, 2026-02-10). Extreme customer concentration: top-5 customers each 11–29% of revenue, manufacturing partners buying on behalf of end-hyperscalers (10-Q, 2026-05-06).

Position in the value chain

Astera is a fabless designer sitting between the foundry/packaging supply base and the hyperscalers/AI-platform builders. It owns no fabs — TSMC fabricates all ICs, with ASE and Amkor for assembly/test (FY25 10-K, 2026-02-20) — and sells retimers, fabric switches, AECs and CXL controllers that attach to GPUs/XPUs (Nvidia, AMD, hyperscaler custom silicon) inside AI racks. The historical base is PCIe signal conditioning (Aries) for scale-out; the growth leg is Scorpio scale-up fabric switching (X-Series), guided to become the largest product line by Q4-2026 (Q1-2026 call, 2026-05-05), plus the 2027 UALink/optical/NVLink-Fusion roadmap.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 2026 (calendar; reported 2026-05-05, qtr ended Mar 31, 2026):

  • Revenue $308.4M, +14% q/q, +93% y/y; above the $286–297M guide. GAAP op income $61.8M on $308.4M rev, GAAP GM 76.3% (10-Q, 2026-05-06). Non-GAAP EPS $0.61, non-GAAP GM 76.4% (+70 bps q/q), non-GAAP op margin 36.2% (transcript, 2026-05-05).
  • Revenue +93% y/y driven by higher unit shipments of Scorpio, Aries and Taurus plus richer mix of hardware modules and Scorpio (10-Q, 2026-05-06).
  • PCIe Gen 6 now >1/3 of total revenue; "millions of Gen 6 ports shipped" (Mohan). Scorpio X-Series (scale-up) shipping initial production volumes; mgmt guides Scorpio to be the largest product line by Q4-2026 (transcript, 2026-05-05).
  • Leo CXL deploying on Microsoft Azure M-series; second CXL design win + a KV-cache custom controller win (ships 2027). Content per accelerator >$1,000 and rising (transcript, 2026-05-05).
  • Guidance: Q2-2026 revenue $355–365M (+15–18% q/q); non-GAAP GM ~73% (incl. ~200 bps drag from the Amazon warrant/customer agreement); non-GAAP EPS $0.68–0.70 (transcript, 2026-05-05).
  • Customer concentration (Q1-2026, % of revenue): A 29% / B 21% / C 16% / D 12% / E 12% — several are manufacturing partners buying for end-hyperscalers (10-Q, 2026-05-06).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull:
    • Scale-up inflection: Scorpio X scale-up fabric switches now in production; UALink switches (higher ASP than PCIe) deploy 2027 alongside Amazon + AMD ASIC/GPU launches — the "key inflection point" narrative (Gajendra, 2026-05-05). MS (J. Moore) raised PT $210 → $240, OW, framed as "buy & ramp before the product cycle kicks in" (briefing 2026-05-06).
    • Content compounding: >$1,000/accelerator and rising as optical (NPO 2027, CPO 2028) and custom (NVLink Fusion, KV-cache controllers) layer on; SAM ">10x to $25B over five years" (Mohan, 2026-02-10). BofA (Arya) PO change on "AI fabric ramp strengthening" (briefing 2026-05-06).
    • Hyperscaler anchoring: $6.5B cumulative Amazon warrant-linked purchase agreement (3.3M warrant shares) across switches/signal conditioning/optical engines (Tate, 2026-02-10); two new US-hyperscaler Scorpio design wins (2026-02-10); Scorpio P to two more hyperscalers late-2026. ALAB +16% the week into JPM TMC (top semis leader), presented Tue 10:05am (briefing 2026-05-19/05-20).
  • Bear:
    • Extreme customer concentration — top customer 29%, top-5 ~90% of revenue, much of it through manufacturing partners that can shift volumes (10-Q, 2026-05-06). Binary on design wins.
    • Competitive encroachment in signal conditioning / AEC: Credo (CRDO) flagged as a direct AEC/retimer rival "growing but commoditizing," with 3.2T physical limits driving discount valuation (Jefferies' Jeff Pu via briefings 2026-05-11/05-12). Hyperscaler-captive and merchant switch competition (AVGO Tomahawk scale-out; GCP using OCS for scale-up) caps parts of the opportunity (briefing 2026-05-20).
    • Optical positioning skepticism: R&Co Redburn (Harrison) "Tripping the Light Fantastic" rated ALAB Neutral alongside CIEN, vs Buys on LITE/COHR/CLS/CRDO (briefing 2026-05-18/05-19) — i.e. less optical conviction than pure-plays. ALAB's NPO/CPO is a 2027/2028 story.
    • Valuation/momentum: AI-semis at ~5σ momentum, 62% above 200dma; buyside reframing the supply chain as a "quarter-by-quarter revision trade, not a multi-year hold" (briefing 2026-05-17). GM headwind from richer hardware-module mix + warrant accounting.
  • Where the sell-side stands (from briefings): MS (J. Moore) OW, PT $240 (raised from $210, 2026-05-06). BofA (Arya) positive PO change on AI fabric ramp (2026-05-06). JPM (Sur) held rating, "strong R/G driven by AI demand" (2026-05-06). R&Co Redburn (Harrison) Neutral on optical framing (2026-05-18).

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2-2026 print: ~early Aug 2026 — watch the $355–365M guide (+15–18% q/q), Scorpio X scale-up ramp, and confirmation Scorpio becomes #1 product line by Q4.
  • Conferences: Jefferies Chicago Semis Conf (Aug 24–25, 2026) — ALAB confirmed presenter (briefing 2026-05-15).
  • 2026: Scorpio P to "two additional major hyperscalers" late-2026; Leo CXL Azure M-series production volumes.
  • 2027 roadmap: UALink-based switch deployments (higher ASP); Amazon + AMD ASIC/GPU launches; NVLink Fusion hybrid-rack revenue; PCIe Gen 7; 1.6T Taurus Ethernet; NPO volume. 2028: CPO deployment.
  • Amazon $6.5B warrant agreement tranche progress (non-cash GM drag begins Q2-2026).

Risks

  • Customer concentration — limited number of end customers drive revenue (top customer 29%; 10-Q 2026-05-06); orders flow through manufacturing partners that can reallocate volumes. Loss/slip at one hyperscaler is material.
  • Design-win dependence — revenue swings on timing/number of multi-year, binary design wins (FY25 10-K).
  • Sole-foundry / supply — TSMC fabricates all ICs; ASE/Amkor assembly/test; small set of module/substrate partners (FY25 10-K). No second source.
  • Standards risk — products built on PCIe/CXL/Ethernet/UALink; UALink in early adoption — if competing standards win or peers diverge, demand suffers (FY25 10-K).
  • Competition — Credo (AEC/retimers), Broadcom (scale-out switching), captive hyperscaler/COT silicon; ASP erosion over product life (FY25 10-K).
  • Margin mix / accounting — richer hardware-module + Scorpio mix dilutes GM (75.7% FY25, -70 bps y/y); Amazon warrant non-cash charge hits GM from Q2-2026.
  • Macro / AI-capex cyclicality & valuation — pure-play on hyperscaler AI capex at stretched momentum; "revision trade" risk (briefing 2026-05-17).

In the inbox (Outlook — recent sell-side flow)

  • Morgan Stanley (J. Moore) _(2026-05-06)_: Overweight, PT $240 — scale-up connectivity (Scorpio) the growth leg.
  • TMTB 'JPM TMT Conf Key Quotes (INTC, ALAB, ANET, DDOG)' _(2026-05-19)_ + Rothschild TMT Daily (Adley) _(2026-05-20)_: ALAB/CRDO in focus; Redburn Neutral ('Tripping the Light Fantastic') the cautious counter.

Sources

  • Filings: 10-K FY2025 (2026-02-20); 10-Q Q1-2026 (2026-05-06).
  • Transcripts: Q1-2026 (2026-05-05); Q4-2025 (2026-02-10); Q3-2025 (2025-11-04); Q2-2025 (2025-08-05). (Two latest read in full; Q3/Q2 saved as summary captures.)
  • Themes: custom-asic-tpu (custom/NVLink-Fusion attach to hyperscaler XPUs); optical-cpo (NPO 2027 / CPO 2028; ALAB rated Neutral vs optical pure-plays).
  • Briefings: E:\briefings\2026\ — 2026-05-06 (MS PT $240 OW; BofA/JPM), 2026-05-11/05-12 (CRDO/AEC competition), 2026-05-15 (Jefferies conf), 2026-05-17/05-18/05-19/05-20 (JPM TMC, R&Co optical Neutral, +16% wk).
  • Outlook / sell-side notes: Outlook/MAPI not available in this environment — no live email flow pulled. Sell-side attribution above is from on-disk briefings only.

CEG — Constellation Energy Corporation

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\CEG (filings + transcripts) · _equity_calls (none) · _briefings\by-ticker\CEG.md (absent). Master index: ../INDEX.md.

🔖 Themes: 800V DC Data-Center Power · AI Data-Center Power & Nuclear

Snapshot

The largest private power producer in the world and the largest clean-energy generator in the US post-Calpine (10-K FY25, 2026-02-24). A fleet of ~55 GW of capacity — nuclear, natural gas, geothermal, hydro, wind and solar — capable of supplying ~27M homes and ~10% of the country's clean energy. The core is the largest nuclear fleet in the US: ~22 GW generating 183 TWh of zero-emission electricity in 2025. Calpine (closed 2026-01-07) added ~23 GW across 72 gas/geothermal/battery assets, strong in Texas, California and the Northeast — making CEG also the largest natural gas and geothermal generator in the country (10-K FY25). Beyond generation, it is one of the largest competitive retail suppliers, serving ~2.5M accounts and three-quarters of the Fortune 100. The structural thesis: selling firm 24/7 carbon-free power to data-center/AI demand via long-term PPAs.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 FY26 (call 2026-05-11): adjusted operating EPS $2.74, a beat of the $2.54 consensus and +$0.60 y/y; GAAP EPS $4.49. FY26 guidance reaffirmed at $11.00–$12.00/share (midpoint $11.50), with a base-earnings growth rate "above 20% through 2029". Repurchased ~1.2M shares at ~$285 ($335M) under the $5B buyback authorization. Free cash flow guided at $8.4B for 2026–2027 and $11.5–$13B for 2028–2029 (~45% growth at the midpoint). ~5,000 MW of new resources (nuclear uprates, gas, battery) submitted to the PJM interconnection queue. Hyperscaler 2026 capex cited as "almost 75% higher than last year" (Dominguez, Q1 FY26).

FY25 context (from the _2026-03-31 outlook stub): Q4 FY25 adj EPS $2.30; revenue $6.07B; FY26 guidance initiated at the 2026-03-31 business outlook, the company's first update of the combined entity post-Calpine. The combined fleet serves 275M MWh of electricity and 800 Bcf of natural gas per year across 40 states (Q1 FY26).

Operating drivers (Q3 FY25, call 2025-11-07): nuclear capacity factor of 96.8%, ~4% above the industry average. Crane Clean Energy Center (formerly Three Mile Island) restart progressing well, "no new challenges emerging"; CEG asked FERC to transfer the Eddystone CIRs to accelerate capacity credit to 2027 (response expected Jun–Jul 2026, Q1 FY26). ~900–1,000 MW of nuclear uprates identified — Byron/Braidwood in the plan (already accretive), LaSalle/Limerick/Calvert Cliffs (190 MW) still out of EPS until 2030 (Q1 FY26). A 1,000 MW demand-response initiative via Grid Beyond — Dominguez compares it, in PJM's ELCC calculation, to "an entire nuclear plant" (Q3 FY25).

Debate / thesis

Sell-side coverage is thin in this corpus: zero equity calls and no briefings roll-up for CEG (see INDEX). Bull/bear below derived mostly from the transcripts; analysts present on the calls: Morgan Stanley, Wolfe, Wells Fargo, Barclays, Jefferies, JPMorgan, Evercore, Scotiabank, Seaport, Melius.

  • Bull: data-center demand described as "hotter than ever" and "past the seventh-inning stretch"; ~$0.75T of capital projected for data centers — "twice the size of the 3 largest listed US utilities" (Dominguez, Q3 FY25). CEG sells the product no one else has ready: firm, clean, 24/7 power, available now vs. new nuclear whose price "is far from set". McHugh: heat rates and spark spreads expanding, with greater price pressure in the outer years than in the near term (Q3 FY25). Uprates are capital efficient — no incremental O&M/fuel, "great investments", organic IRRs >10% (Dominguez/Smith, Q1 FY26). Calpine adds $2/share of EPS one year after close (Smith, Q1 FY26). PJM market reform (white paper "Powering Reliability Through Market Design") shifts revenue toward the energy market and co-optimizes reserves — seen as a positive (Dardis, Q1 FY26).
  • Bear: the big hyperscaler deals keep slipping — promised "before the Q4 call" on Q3 FY25, still not announced as of Q1 FY26; some conversations "paused" awaiting PJM regulatory clarity (Dominguez, Q1 FY26). ERCOT forwards weak despite the data-center pipeline — CEG admits load "is not in the system yet, it's being built" and considers the market undervalued only from 2028–2029 (Q1 FY26): the thesis depends on timing. "Bring-your-own-generation" and new entrants (oil/gas, new techs) threaten the long-term earnings visibility of the fleet — Storozynski (Seaport) presses directly on how many units CEG will manage to contract (Q3 FY25). Lockup of 25M shares held by the Calpine sellers expires 2026-06-30 (another 25M on 2027-06-30) — a technical overhang (Smith, Q1 FY26). Option B of the PJM white paper (differentiated reliability treatment by load) raises "real legal questions" and still needs to be digested (Dardis, Q1 FY26).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: no PTs/ratings in the co-located sources — no equity call and no archived briefing for CEG (INDEX, CEG section: "Equity calls (0)", "Briefings (email): —"). Coverage here is purely from transcripts; the tone of the analysts on the calls is constructive but focused on when (not if) the deals close.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • FERC response on the Eddystone→Crane CIR transfer: Jun–Jul 2026 — unlocks 2027 capacity credit for the Crane restart (Q1 FY26).
  • Calpine lockup expiration: 2026-06-30 (25M shares); the $5B buyback gives flexibility to absorb (Q1 FY26).
  • PJM submission to FERC on capacity-market reforms: targeted June (Q1 FY26); colocation implementation date that PJM indicated as 2029 — CEG wants to pull it forward.
  • Hyperscaler deal announcement(s): repeatedly delayed; Dominguez expects regulatory clarity "by year-end" as the unlock (Q1 FY26).
  • Next print: ~90 days after 2026-05-11, i.e., the Q2 FY26 call in Aug/2026 (Dominguez, Q1 FY26 closing).
  • Byron/Braidwood uprates (in plan, accretive); LaSalle/Limerick/Calvert Cliffs only post-2030.

Risks

  • Data-center contracting slippage — the growth thesis depends on closing long-term PPAs that keep getting delayed by regulatory dependence (PJM/FERC) and third parties in interconnection (Q3 FY25, Q1 FY26).
  • Weakness in ERCOT forwards and the timing of load arrival (10-K FY25; Q1 FY26).
  • PJM regulatory/legislative risk — capacity-market design, colocation/BYOG rules and differentiated load treatment (Q1 FY26).
  • Calpine integration/divestitures — assets to sell for DOJ/FERC approval; natural-gas exposure brings fuel-price volatility and potential future carbon cost (10-K FY25; Q3 FY25).
  • Nuclear operating risk — concentrated fleet; safety/outages; relicensings depend on NRC approval.
  • Calpine share overhang (lockups Jun-2026/Jun-2027).
  • New nuclear cost/execution — CEG remains cautious; significant capital only after a durable PPA + firm prices with proven constructibility (Q3 FY25).

Consensus estimates (BBG)

Sources

  • Filings: 10-K FY25 (2026-02-24) — business overview + Calpine.
  • Transcripts: Q1 FY26 (2026-05-11) · Q3 FY25 (2025-11-07) · stub Q4/Outlook (2026-03-31) (transcript not retrieved; release facts only).
  • Decks: not cited on this page (Analyst Day 2022, Business Outlook 2024 available — see INDEX).
  • Equity calls: 0 attributed (see CEG section in INDEX — power names thin in this corpus).

VST — Vistra Corp.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\VST (filings + transcripts) · _briefings\by-ticker\VST.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

🔖 Themes: 800V DC Data-Center Power · AI Data-Center Power & Nuclear

Snapshot

Vistra is the largest competitive (merchant) power producer in the US: ~41GW+ of generation across ERCOT, PJM, New England, NY and California, spanning natural gas (combined-cycle/peakers), nuclear, coal-to-gas conversions, solar and battery storage, plus a leading competitive retail electricity franchise serving millions of residential and C&I customers. The model is integrated — generation + commercial hedging + retail — which is the core of the bull case: retail and gen offset each other across weather/price regimes (Q1 FY26, 2026-05-07). Two reportable segments: Generation (~$4.29B FY25 adj. EBITDA) and Retail (~$1.62B FY25), per Q4 FY25 (2026-02-26). The stock is now an AI-power / data-center demand play sitting at the intersection of merchant power and hyperscaler capex.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 FY26 (2026-05-07): ~$1.494B adjusted EBITDA, a record calendar-Q1, +~20% YoY and +~85% vs Q1 2024 (Kris Moldovan). Generation $1.426B (strong realized revenue, higher PJM capacity revenue, Lotus contribution); Retail $68M — a deliberate YoY decline against an exceptionally mild ERCOT winter (2nd-warmest Q1 since 1950) and tough comp vs a record 2025. Mgmt reaffirmed FY26 adj. EBITDA and adj. FCF-before-growth guidance and held the FY27 adj. EBITDA midpoint opportunity range — both exclude the pending Cogentrix deal and the Meta PJM-nuclear PPAs, to be updated post-close (Burke). Fitch upgraded VST to investment grade in Q1, the 2nd IG agency after S&P (late 2025), triggering fallaway/lien-release provisions.

Capital returns / framework (Q1 FY26 + Q4 FY25): ~$525M of buybacks in the first 4 months of FY26 plus a ~$75M Q1 dividend = ~$600M returned YTD; 10b5-1 program is designed to accelerate on weakness (it leaned in during Jan-Feb price pressure). Since Nov-2021: ~169M shares retired at ~$37 avg (Q1 FY26). Remaining authorization ~$1.475B. Line of sight to >$10B cash generation over 2026-2027: ~$3B to equity holders, ~$4B to accretive growth (Cogentrix, Permian gas, PJM nuclear uprate, Oak Hill 2), leaving ~$3B unallocated, all at ~2.3x net debt/EBITDA by YE27 (Moldovan).

FCF/share bridge (Q4 FY25, 2026-02-26): >$12.50 adj. FCF-before-growth/share for FY26 on Feb-20 curves; ~$16 with Cogentrix + Meta operating PPAs + a simplifying buyback assumption; mgmt floated $22-25 if all 2026-2030 cash-available-for-allocation were deployed to buybacks at roughly current price (illustrative, not guidance).

Strategic build-out: Cogentrix (5,500MW gas, ~$730/kW net of tax benefits, mid-single-digit FCF/share accretion in 2027, high-single-digit avg 2027-29) on track to close 2H26 (Q4 FY25). ~3.8GW of nuclear now under 20-yr PPAs — Amazon at Comanche Peak (1,200MW, energization 2027, full ramp 2032) and Meta across Perry/Davis-Besse/Beaver Valley (2,176MW operating + 433MW uprates, uprates online 2031-2034). ~3.2GW more nuclear (Beaver Valley + Comanche Peak) still uncontracted. ~4,500MW of organic development in process, majority online by 2028 (Q1 FY26). Post-Cogentrix CCGT fleet = 26GW at ~60% utilization — the key operating-leverage lever (Q4 FY25).

Debate / thesis

Sell-side coverage in the disk sources is thin — only a single morning-briefing datapoint exists (see below); no equity-call notes, no PT/rating roll-up. Bull/bear below is drawn primarily from the two most recent transcripts and the one briefing line. Flagging this explicitly.

  • Bull: Structurally improved, durable demand — first prospective 4-yr sustained US load-growth period since the era ending 2007; US consumption hit ~4,200 TWh in 2025, +2.5% YoY (Q4 FY25). ERCOT load growth of 5-6% to 2030 and PJM 2-3% (Q1 FY26 framing) drive higher utilization of an existing fleet at only ~60% utilization — operating leverage without greenfield risk. Integrated model proven across weather (gen offset mild-weather retail drag in Q1 FY26; thermal was ~93% of ERCOT delivery during the tightest hours of Winter Storm Fern). Contracting de-risks the model: ~half of total adj. EBITDA expected from highly stable sources once signed PPAs ramp (Q4 FY25) — "not trading growth for stability, achieving both" (Burke). Aggressive, value-accretive buyback (~$20B value created since 2021) into a high FCF yield. Hyperscaler capex tailwind: >$700B in 2026, ~+50% YoY (Q4 FY25). Briefing datapoint: MS (Arcaro) flagged the Helix Digital Infrastructure ($10B, with KKR/NVDA/KIA) initiative as a new theme worth ~$23/share from an initial $1B (morning, 2026-06-12).
  • Bear: Mgmt itself is more cautious than the market on pace — load growth is "real but not at the extremely elevated levels in the rapid time frame forecast by many third parties"; meaningful data-center tightening doesn't begin until "late 2027 or early 2028" (Q4 FY25). ERCOT forwards have come off on mild weather; Burke concedes forwards don't even reflect his own 5-6% load view, i.e. earnings hinge on weather/volatility that has been muted since the Aug-2023 peak. Regulatory overhang in PJM: colocation tariff rules, reliability backstop auction (RBA, possibly Sept), connect-and-manage and load-interconnection queues all unresolved — a peer (Constellation) flagged a customer "pause"; VST says no slowdown but admits deals are "complicated" and slow. Retail FY26 EBITDA moderates toward a ~$1.4B medium-term run-rate vs the FY25 record (one-time supply/Energy Harbor tailwinds don't repeat). Battery economics in ERCOT have "returned virtually nothing" (Burke, Q1 FY26). Big inorganic appetite = integration and capital-allocation execution risk.
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: Coverage thin on disk. Only attributable read: MS / David Arcaro — constructive, surfacing the Helix Digital Infrastructure theme as incremental ~$23/share upside (briefing, 2026-06-12). Active questioners on the calls (not ratings) include Wells Fargo (Pourreza), Wolfe (Fleishman), Jefferies (Dumoulin-Smith), JPM (Tonet), UBS (Appicelli), BNP (Sutton), Morgan Stanley (Arcaro), Seaport (Storozynski), Scotiabank (Wiesel). No PTs/ratings available in co-located sources.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Cogentrix close (2H26) — triggers the FY26 guidance + FY27 midpoint update; ~$700-750M potential FY27 EBITDA add from Cogentrix + Meta operating PPAs (Q4 FY25 disclosure math).
  • Next nuclear/gas contract announcement — ~3.2GW Beaver Valley + Comanche Peak uncontracted; mgmt says appetite "as high as ever" (Q4 FY25). Beaver Valley colocation a watch item.
  • PJM regulatory cadence — colocation tariff compliance filings, the reliability backstop auction (potentially Sept), connect-and-manage rulemaking, price-collar extension (Q4/Q1 calls).
  • ERCOT batch 0 / load-interconnection process — VST thinks total ERCOT growth is ~30-40GW by 2030 (~10-15GW data centers), well below the 300-400GW queue noise (Q1 FY26).
  • Helix Digital Infrastructure ($10B w/ KKR/NVDA/KIA) — emerging theme per MS (briefing, 2026-06-12); watch for formal disclosure.
  • Continued buyback acceleration if shares stay weak; possible 3rd IG ratings upgrade "as early as later this year" (Q4 FY25).

Risks

  • Commodity/power-price and basis exposure; merchant earnings depend on volatility — muted weather since Aug-2023 has pressured ERCOT forwards (Q1 FY26).
  • Weather concentration (ERCOT) — mild winters compress retail; extreme events (Fern, Uri-type) cut both ways.
  • Nuclear operational/outage risk and regulatory (NRC license, PTC downside protection relied on in hedging) (Q4 FY25).
  • PJM/FERC/ERCOT regulatory uncertainty on colocation, interconnection, capacity-market design — could delay or reshape data-center contracting.
  • Execution/integration risk on Cogentrix and the organic build pipeline; capital-allocation discipline (mid-teens levered return threshold).
  • Retail margin normalization toward ~$1.4B (Q4 FY25); battery/storage returns weak in ERCOT.
  • _Generic 10-K risk-factor categories (commodity, weather, nuclear, regulatory, cyber, leverage) per VST_10-K 2026-02-27; HTML not text-extractable here, so cited at category level only._

TLN — Talen Energy Corporation

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\TLN (filings + transcripts + decks) · _equity_calls (none) · _briefings\by-ticker\TLN.md (none). Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Coverage note: thin file. No briefing roll-up exists for TLN and no equity-call attributions are logged in INDEX. Bull/bear below is built primarily from the company's own earnings transcripts plus the 10-K — sell-side views are paraphrased only as quoted by name in the Q&A (Wells Fargo, BofA, Wolfe, Jefferies, Morgan Stanley, JPM, Barclays, Evercore, UBS, Seaport, Tuohy). TLN relisted in 2024; history is short.

🔖 Themes: 800V DC Data-Center Power · AI Data-Center Power & Nuclear

Snapshot

Talen is a merchant independent power producer (IPP) repositioning itself as the "powering-AI" infrastructure play in PJM. ~13.1 GW of generation, of which 2.2 GW is nuclear at the single Susquehanna plant (PPL zone, PA); the balance is a dispatchable fossil fleet — CCGTs, intermediate/peaking gas (Montour, Martins Creek) — principally in Mid-Atlantic, Ohio and Montana (TLN 10-K, 2026-02-26). It sells energy, capacity and ancillaries into wholesale markets and is converting merchant assets into contracted infrastructure via long-term data-center PPAs. Anchor contract: the front-of-the-meter ~1.9 GW Amazon/AWS PPA at Susquehanna (revamped/doubled and moved FOTM in June 2025; AA-credit counterparty). The investor framing is the "Talen flywheel" — contract assets, acquire accretively, recycle capital, repeat — driving free-cash-flow-per-share growth (Q4 FY25, 2026-02-26).

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 FY26 (2026-05-05): $473M adjusted EBITDA, $350M adjusted FCF — EBITDA more than doubled and FCF roughly quadrupled YoY on the Freedom & Guernsey acquisitions (~2.8 GW CCGT, closed late Nov 2025), higher spark spreads, capacity/RMR revenues live since June 2025, and the ongoing AWS ramp (Cole Muller, Q1 FY26). Fleet generated ~16 TWh at a 55% capacity factor; Susquehanna Unit 1 refueling outage completed efficiently and re-synced to grid (Terry Nutt, Q1 FY26).

2026 guidance reaffirmed: adj. EBITDA $1.75–2.05bn, adj. FCF $0.98–1.18bn — and explicitly excludes the pending Cornerstone acquisition (3 plants, OH/IN; ~$500M run-rate EBITDA, >$4/sh incremental FCF). Cornerstone close expected "as soon as this summer" pending FERC §203 (HSR cleared March; Indiana order pending) (Q1 FY26).

Balance sheet / capital returns: net leverage 3.1x at 3/31, target <3.5x. Pre-funded Cornerstone with $4bn senior unsecured notes at a blended ~6.25%, and retired $1.2bn of 8.625% senior secured notes — ~$40M/yr interest savings (~$1/sh FCF), cutting secured debt from ~60% to ~30% of total and lifting ratings (Cole Muller, Q1 FY26). $2bn buyback authorization through 2028; $100M repurchased in Q1; mgmt frames buying "at scale over time." Prior buybacks: ~$2bn / ~24% of float at ~$149 avg over 2.5 yrs (Terry Nutt, Q4 FY25).

Preliminary outlook (incl. Cornerstone, marks to 3/31): ~$34/sh FCF in 2027, ~$36 in 2028 base; ~$41 in 2028 with 70% of FCF to buybacks — a 30% lift vs. January, implying ~11% FCF yield. Mgmt flags ~$5/MWh of further spark-spread appreciation already realized since 3/31 not in the numbers (Cole Muller, Q1 FY26).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull (mgmt + the structural setup): Demand growth is real and supply is static — "value of steel in the ground." Q1 PJM weather-adjusted deliveries +3% YoY; PPL zone peak load projected +70% over five years, AEP +30% (Q4/Q1 FY26). Susquehanna's ~1.9 GW AWS PPA gives an AA-credit, long-dated contracted base (35% of gross margin contracted long-term; each incremental 1 GW PPA adds ~15pts, next deal could reach ~50%). FCF/sh compounding via the flywheel, with multiple un-modeled upside levers (spark spreads, AWS acceleration, new 1 GW PPAs, accretive M&A — each "10%+" to FCF/sh). Capacity tailwind: last BRA would have cleared >$500 absent the $330 cap, and the price collar is being extended for two more auctions (Mac McFarland / Terry Nutt, Q4 FY25).
  • Bear / risks to thesis: Single-asset nuclear concentration (one plant, Susquehanna) and merchant commodity exposure — spark spreads and a widening, possibly non-fundamental West Hub–PPL zonal basis swing FCF/sh meaningfully (Chris Morice, Q1 FY26). Regulatory overhang: PJM co-location tariff still unsettled (FERC instructed PJM on allowable configs Dec 2025; PJM filed Feb 23, 2026), and the Reliability Backstop / RVP design, cost allocation and "fair share" of data-center load remain open (10-K; Q4/Q1 FY26). The Montour zoning denial punctured the data-center development narrative — mgmt now refuses to discuss the pipeline publicly, reducing visibility. New-build economics don't clear on a merchant basis ("the gap is pretty large," Cole Muller, Q1 FY26) — growth needs either a 15-yr RVP award or a hyperscaler offtake. Leverage rising into Cornerstone; financing pre-funded ahead of regulatory close (carry cost, execution risk).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: No PTs/ratings captured on disk (no briefings, no equity calls). Sell-side engagement on the calls skews constructive — Q&A pressure is on (i) whether new gen must be 1:1 with new load (mgmt: no, it's a "fifty-hour" adequacy problem solved by batteries/CTs, not baseload), (ii) the basis dislocation, and (iii) buyback cadence and Cornerstone timing (Q1 FY26 Q&A). Treat all PT/consensus figures as unavailable in-file.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Cornerstone close — FERC §203 + Indiana order, expected this summer; triggers 2026 guidance reset (+~$4/sh FCF, ~$500M EBITDA run-rate).
  • PJM RVP / Reliability Backstop rules — PJM signaled timing pulled to this fall; pay-as-bid construct and 15-yr awards are the key unlock for new-build financing.
  • FERC co-location tariff finalization (PJM filing 2026-02-23) — governs how large load attaches to existing generation.
  • Next 1 GW+ PPA signing — the swing factor to ~50% contracted gross margin; mgmt says delivery wouldn't start before ~2028 regardless of signing date.
  • Buyback acceleration post-Cornerstone as leverage trends below 3.5x; capacity auction (BRA) outcomes under the extended price collar.

Risks

  • Single nuclear plant (Susquehanna) — outage/operational concentration; refueling cadence drives quarterly swings.
  • Merchant commodity exposure: power, spark spreads, fuel; West Hub–PPL zonal basis widening (mgmt calls it temporal, but it's in the marks).
  • Regulatory: PJM RVP/co-location/cost-allocation uncertainty; "fair share" of data-center cost still undefined.
  • Concentration in one off-taker (AWS) for the anchor contracted cash flows.
  • Leverage/financing: rising debt into Cornerstone, pre-funded at ~6.25%; interest-rate and capital-markets access risk on future PPAs requiring credit support.
  • Development execution: Montour-style zoning/permitting setbacks; new-build only viable with offtake or RVP award.

GEV — GE Vernova

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\GEV (filings + transcripts + decks) · _equity_calls · _briefings\by-ticker\GEV.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

🔖 Themes: 800V DC Data-Center Power · AI Data-Center Power & Nuclear

Snapshot

GE Vernova is the GE spin-out (April 2024) holding the legacy electric-power franchise across three segments: Power (gas turbines, nuclear/SMR, hydro, steam), Electrification (grid transmission, HVDC, transformers, switchgear, power conversion/storage, grid software), and Wind (onshore + offshore). It is the central pure-play on the AI-driven electricity build-out: data-center load needs both new generation (Gas Power) and grid hardware (Electrification). FY26 revenue guided to ~$44.5–45.5B (Q1 FY26, 2026-04-22). Power is the cash/earnings anchor, Electrification is the emerging growth story, Wind is a managed drag. Largest installed base of gas/steam turbines + generators of any OEM globally (Q1 FY26 transcript), giving a structural services annuity.

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 FY26 (2026-04-22) — a clean beat-and-raise, stock +14% on the day (GEVernova callback, 2026-04-22).

  • Orders $18.3B, +71% y/y, book-to-bill ~2x; equipment orders more than doubled, services +25%. Total backlog $163B (up from $116B at spin), equipment backlog $76B (+67% y/y).
  • Revenue +7% y/y; adj. EBITDA $896M, +87% y/y, margin +390bps.
  • FCF $4.8B in Q1 alone vs. $3.7B for all of FY25 — driven by down-payments on orders/slot reservations.
  • Gas Power: signed 21 GW of new agreements; total GW under contract 83→100 sequentially (44 GW firm backlog, 56 GW SRAs). ~80% traditional customers / ~20% data center. Shipped 25 turbines (+32% y/y). H1'26 orders priced 10–20 pts higher $/kW vs. Q4'25; another 10–20 pts of pricing upside sitting in SRAs. Capacity ramps to ~20 GW annualized in mid-'26, 24 GW by 2028. Now expects $200B total backlog by '27 (pulled forward from '28) and ≥110 GW under contract by year-end '26.
  • Electrification: orders +86% y/y to ~$7.1B (~2.5x revenue); revenue +61% GAAP / +29% organic incl. Prolec; EBITDA margin +590bps to 17.8%. Data-center orders $2.4B in Q1 alone — more than all of FY25. TAM now framed at ~$300B by end of decade (was $150B in March; see Bull). First Energy Management System (EMS) order booked — software/controls inside the data center.
  • Wind: revenue −25%; EBITDA loss $382M (in line). No called inflection in US onshore.
  • Guidance raised: FY26 revenue $44.5–45.5B; adj. EBITDA margin 12–14% (+1pt both ends); FCF $6.5–7.5B (up from $5–5.5B). Power margin 17–19%, Electrification 18–20%, Wind ~−$400M EBITDA. EBITDA second-half weighted, peak in Q4. Prolec (acquired Feb 2026, $5.3B for remaining 50%) ~$3B revenue at >20% margin. Balance sheet <1x gross debt/EBITDA, ~$10.2B cash; $1.4B returned in Q1.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: The stock is an earnings-revisions machine — "GE is going up off the back of revisions… last year up 100% because consensus was up 100%" and PMs are buying it valuation-insensitive on pace of EBITDA growth (GEVernova callback, 2026-04-22). The biggest narrative shift over the last 3–6 months is Electrification flipping from "peaking" to accelerating: management doubled its TAM from $150B (Strazik, London, mid-March) to $300B and pulled forward the $60B+ Electrification backlog / $200B total backlog target to '27 (callback, 2026-04-22). Gas Power supply-chain bottleneck (Howmet/PCP/Doncasters/CPP castings) appears to have eased — GEV "wrote a check," is getting capacity first vs. Siemens/MHI, commands a ~20–30% US price premium vs. ENR, and may raise the 24 GW production target (GEVernova preview, 2026-04-17). Data-center power thesis backed by Jefferies (James Snyder) initiating Buy, 2028E EPS $6.35 (briefing 2026-06-01). Hard-asset/AI-grid positioning — Tiger top-10 holding ~$1.03B (briefing 2026-05-16). US installed only 13 GW of gas in all of 2025 vs. GEV signing ~18 GW over two quarters — demand is "rather dramatic" (callback, 2026-04-22).
  • Bear: Order-peak risk — Q4'25's 24 GW / $22.2B order quarter is the comp to beat; post-analyst-day, hedge funds called the desk arguing "this is the peak, there was pull-forward" (callback, 2026-04-22). Buy-side survey skews cautious: most expected Q1 orders only "slightly above consensus, not a blow-out," and only ~12% saw Section 232 as "impactful" — i.e., bull case largely embedded (preview, 2026-04-17). Wind "is the gift that keeps on giving" — surprise Q2 EBITDA loss guide of $200–300M (desk modeled ~$50M); US halted all offshore wind activity Dec 22 (Q4 FY25, 2026-01-28). Pricing-metric ambiguity — the 10–20 pts $/kW figure ignores mix (peakers/aeros distort it); desk "not quite sure what's going on" (callback). Conversion of SRAs to firm orders gated by permitting/EPC/interconnect, not money — 2030 slots filling ahead of 2029 (callback). Section 232: F-class turbines imported from Belfort, France would face 25% on full value; tariff net impact guided $250–350M for '26 (Q1 FY26 + preview).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: Jefferies (Snyder) Buy, data-center power buildout thesis (briefings 2026-06-01, 2026-06-12). JPM (Rishabh Gupta) OW on the India T&D arm, PT raised to Rs5,050 — read-through to parent grid demand, not the US listing (briefings 2026-05-19/20). Buy-side 2030 EBITDA expectations have re-rated hard: 44% see $18–20B, ~20% see >$20B vs. an August bull pitch of just $14B — the stock is priced off 2030 and estimates keep climbing (preview, 2026-04-17). UBS 800V-power equity call (2026-05-14) ties GEV into the data-center power-density theme.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2 FY26 print (~late July 2026): the order bogey — beat the Q4'25 24 GW / $22.2B; mgmt guided 10–15 GW new contracts in Q2 and April orders already exceeded all of Q1 in dollar value (>$5.6B per callback). Watch $/kW mix (a large aero order would flatter it).
  • Gas Power framework agreements: 30–35 multi-year hyperscaler/utility frameworks in negotiation; none closed to date — first signing would be a catalyst (Q1 FY26).
  • Mid-2026: Gas capacity steps to ~20 GW annualized; productivity from 280+ new machines / 1,800 new US workers should show in Q3.
  • Nuclear/SMR: OPG Darlington Unit 1 basemat install underway; Clinch River (TVA) NRC construction license expected H2'26; up to $40B US/Japan SMR funding for GE Vernova Hitachi (GBA likely first, 10 units).
  • Electrification product ramps: EMS (2nd order already in April), MV-UPS stability block (potential orders H2'26), solid-state transformer (first hyperscaler delivery fall '26 → 6-month test → potential orders H1'27).
  • Section 232 wind/solar tariff outcome — could give US onshore orders clarity in H2'26.

Risks

  • Order/demand cyclicality — long-cycle backlog exposed to a capex/AI-spend reversal; orders peak narrative live.
  • Supply chain / capacity constraints — castings & forgings (Howmet/PCP/CPP) are the true bottleneck; manufacturing capacity constraints flagged as a risk factor (10-K 2026-01-29).
  • Fixed-price / large-project execution — liquidated damages and unanticipated expense if specs/schedules missed, explicitly cited for Wind component delivery (10-K 2026-01-29).
  • Offshore Wind — US offshore activity halted Dec 22; Haliade-X program loss-making; Vineyard/Dogger Bank execution risk into 2027 (Q4 FY25; 10-K).
  • Tariffs (Section 232) — $250–350M net '26 impact; Belfort-imported turbines and Prolec exposed (Q1 FY26).
  • Customer/geographic concentration & commodity prices — macro deterioration, decarbonization-policy shifts (10-K risk factors).
  • Geopolitical — Middle East exposure; LNG-to-power projects in Vietnam (4.8 GW) where one project is being weighed against renewables (Q1 FY26).

Consensus estimates (BBG)

PWR — Quanta Services, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\PWR (filings + transcripts + decks) · _equity_calls · _briefings\by-ticker\PWR.md. Master index: ../INDEX.md.

🔖 Themes: 800V DC Data-Center Power · AI Data-Center Power & Nuclear

Snapshot

Quanta is the leading US/Canada/Australia infrastructure-solutions contractor for electric & gas utilities, power generation, large load centers (data centers), pipelines and communications — design, engineering, procurement, construction, upgrade and repair/maintenance (10-K 2026-02-19). As of the 2025 10-K it reports two segments: Electric Infrastructure Solutions (the old Electric Power + Renewable Energy segments combined) and Underground Utility & Infrastructure Solutions (10-K 2026-02-19). The model is decentralized — multiple operating companies, ~69,500 employees (Q4 FY25, 2026-02-19). The thesis is a single word: it is the labor- and execution-layer pick-and-shovel on the once-in-a-generation US grid build-out — transmission, electrification, gas backup, renewables/storage and data-center power. Data center work is now ~10% of business and the fastest-growing slice of backlog (Q4 FY25, 2026-02-19).

Current state (latest quarter)

Q1 FY26 (call 2026-04-30): revenue $7.9bn, adjusted EBITDA $686m, adjusted diluted EPS $2.68 — all double-digit growth; record backlog $48.5bn (CFO Desai). Mgmt raised FY26 guide: revenue $34.7–35.2bn, adj. EBITDA $3.49–3.65bn, adj. EPS $13.55–14.25; FCF guide unchanged with confidence at the "higher end" (Desai). Earl Austin: backlog growth was "broad-based" — the 765 kV transmission project was "less than 25%" / "less than $1bn" of the increase, so not single-project dependent; expects backlog to "continue to rise." Achieved "30% EPS growth with no acquisitions in the quarter" (Austin). Newly announced $500–700m multi-year investment in high-voltage (345–765 kV) power-transformer manufacturing — meant to attack the "36 months on transformers" lead-time bottleneck and de-risk customers like AEP via a US-based supply chain (Austin). Off-site manufacturing/logistics footprint ~6.7m sq ft. NiSource/Alphabet GenCo opportunity cited at "$5.7bn…growing every day," with announced CCGT/generation work not yet in backlog (air permits pending; expected "later half of the year and beyond").

FY25 context (Q4 call 2026-02-19): record FY revenue $28.5bn (+20%), adj. EBITDA $2.9bn, adj. EPS $10.75 (+20%), FCF $1.7bn; 8 acquisitions / ~11,100 employees added. Year-end backlog $44bn. Framing target: >2x earnings power by 2030, i.e. a 15–20% adj. EPS CAGR through 2030 (Austin/Desai, both calls).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull:
    • Multi-decade demand, not a single cycle. Austin: it took "70 or 100 years to build the grid…you can't double it overnight"; CCGT orders run into 2030 → execution into "2033 and beyond"; demand lasts "a decade plus" (Q1 FY26, 2026-04-30). Even discounting the doubling thesis "50%, it's still doubling the largest infrastructure project in the world"; visibility to 2032 (Q4 FY25, 2026-02-19).
    • Mega-bull free-rider. BofA / Fenske places PWR inside the AI mega-bull framework as one of the cycle's "free-rider winners" alongside the hyperscalers (briefing 2026-05-11). Read-throughs flagged: GEV, NVDA-power, BE/AAOI.
    • Labor + supply-chain moat compounding. "Labor builds labor" — added 5,000–6,000 organically last year, repeat + M&A on top (Austin, Q1 FY26). Vertical integration into transformer manufacturing turns a competitor bottleneck into a Quanta advantage; shift to "5-, 10-year programmatic spend" vs. one-off bids lifts ROIC (Austin, Q4 FY25).
    • Broad-based, not one mega-project. "There is no SunZia" driving backlog (Austin, Q4 FY25) — quality of growth is high.
  • Bear:
    • Indirect, not a sponsored call. The only desk datapoint on disk is a thematic mention — "No active move… indirect read-through from hyperscaler capex theme" (briefing 2026-05-11). No PT, no rating in archive; bull case rests on mgmt + theme.
    • Margin ceiling / regulated-customer constraint. Austin repeatedly steers to "compounding" value over margin-% and cites regulated-utility constraints (Q4 FY25); Underground "has the ability to operate in double digits" only "over time" (Q1 FY26).
    • CCGT risk allocation. Refuses "firm fixed price generation" on combined cycle; will be "prudent," "won't win them all," and won't grow the org beyond the market (Austin, both calls) — caps the gas-gen upside that bulls extrapolate.
    • Lumpy big-ticket awards. No significant 765 kV in backlog at Q4; major awards not expected until "late half of 2027" then over 3–5 years (Austin, Q4 FY25) — air pocket risk on the biggest projects.
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: thin on disk. BofA/Fenske (2026-05-11) thematic mega-bull inclusion; Jefferies/Rankie Wong (2026-05-11) lists PWR in a broad US-infrastructure basket. No ratings/PTs archived. Sell-side coverage breadth is wide on the calls (Evercore, Citi, UBS, Jefferies, GS, KeyBanc, Stifel, Wolfe, Baird, Truist, Bernstein, Vertical, Mizuho, JPM, Cantor) but stances are not captured here.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • 2026 Investor Day — deck on disk PWR_2026InvestorDay_2026-03-31.pdf; flagged on the Q4 call as the venue to outline "strategic paths" (Austin, 2026-02-19).
  • Q2 FY26 print (~late July 2026 on prior cadence) — watch backlog trajectory ("continue to rise"), Underground margin sustainability, and whether NiSource/Alphabet CCGT generation work enters backlog ("later half of the year").
  • Transformer-manufacturing ramp — execution against the $500–700m investment; lead-time relief is the de-risking story for AEP and large-load customers.
  • 765 kV transmission award cadence — major awards skewed to late-2027+ (Q4 FY25); permitting reform a swing factor (Austin).
  • M&A — Austin expects deals "over the next 9 months" and "2 or 3 years," additive to (not in) current guidance (Q1 FY26).

Risks

  • Fixed-price and non-fixed-price construction/engineering contracts — cost-overrun/execution exposure (10-K 2026-02-19).
  • Dependence on skilled labor / experienced operators; craft-labor tightness, especially data-center work, the "tightest market," with transmission and fiber-splicer roles tightening next (Austin, Q4 FY25; 10-K 2026-02-19).
  • Acquisition-integration risk — 8 deals / ~11,100 employees added in FY25 alone (Q4 FY25).
  • Customer/end-market concentration in regulated utilities and a small set of large-load (hyperscaler) customers; regulated-customer pricing constrains margin.
  • Project/award lumpiness on big transmission and CCGT work; permitting and air-permit timing.
  • Commodity/geopolitical noise — mgmt says diesel is "a little piece" and guide "contemplates" such moves (Austin, Q1 FY26), but it remains a swing on input costs.

WMB — The Williams Companies

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\WMB (filings + transcripts + decks) · _equity_calls · _briefings\by-ticker\WMB.md (none — no briefing coverage for this name). Master index: ../INDEX.md.

🔖 Themes: AI Data-Center Power & Nuclear

Snapshot

Williams is a US natural-gas midstream pure-play: interstate transmission, gathering & processing (G&P), storage, and gas marketing. Four reportable segments — Transmission, Power & Gulf; Northeast G&P; West; and Gas & NGL Marketing Services — plus "Other" (upstream, corporate) (10-K, 2026-02-24). The crown jewel is Transco, the largest US interstate gas pipeline by volume, running Gulf-to-Northeast and structurally short of capacity in the markets it serves. The franchise has pivoted hard into two demand-driven legs: (1) gas transmission expansions (Transco, Northwest Pipeline/Mountain West) feeding power-gen and LNG, and (2) a newer Power Innovation (PI) behind-the-meter business — building gas generation directly for hyperscaler data centers at a targeted ~5x EBITDA build multiple. Williams is positioned at the intersection of the AI/power capex cycle and the US LNG export ramp.

Estado atual (Q1 FY2026, call 2026-05-05)

Record quarter. Adjusted EBITDA $2.25bn, +13% YoY (from $1.99bn); adjusted EPS +22% (John Porter, CFO, Q1 FY26). Transmission & Gulf led, +~$150m / ~17% — Transco +~10% YoY on the post-rate-case tariff step-up plus expansions; Deepwater Gulf +60%; storage +35%; West +16% (Haynesville / Louisiana Energy Gateway). Sequent marketing $227m EBITDA. Upstream Haynesville divested to JERA (closed Jan-26; ~$180m book gain excluded from recurring metrics).

Guidance: now pointing to the upper half of original FY26 adjusted EBITDA guide; reaffirmed the Analyst-Day 10%+ EBITDA & EPS CAGR (2025–2030). Management says the contracted base CAGR moved from ~8% (Feb Analyst Day) to ~9% with the new Q1 projects, and called that "still pretty conservative" (Porter, Q1 FY26). Seasonally soft 2Q expected before H2 reacceleration, including the partial Socrates startup in Q3.

Backlog / projects (Q1 FY26): Three new majors plus an upsize. NEO — fifth PI project, 682MW, 12.5-yr contract, ~$2.3bn, ISD H2-2028, 5x build multiple (largest PI deal to date). Atlas — Transco gas-for-backup-power (displacing diesel) at a Northeast data center, up to 164 MMcf/d, 13-yr term, <$50m capex, in service by YE26. Silver Spur — Northwest Pipeline expansion into Idaho, 275 MMcf/d, ~$0.5bn, ISD early-2030 (phase 1 of Rockies Columbia Connector). Power Express (Transco/Virginia) upsized to 750 MMcf/d, online 2030. ~700 MMcf/d of G&P expansions sanctioned in the quarter. Total committed PI capital ~$5.1bn as of Q3-25 (Q3 FY25 call). Growth capex midpoint raised to $7.3bn for FY26.

Balance sheet: Leverage 4.1x, modestly above the 3.5–4.0x target — a 26/27 timing issue per management, with "historic earnings growth" in 28+ resetting capacity. Financing options under review (partnership/JV around PI, capital recycling); specifics promised "in the next couple of months" (Porter, Q1 FY26). Dividend growth explicitly stays intact.

Debate / thesis

  • Bull (JSD equity call, 2026-05-06; corroborated on the Q1 call): Pipelines are a lagging indicator vs. gas-gen, and "Williams is the ultimate manifestation of this thesis" — a long-cycle compounder where the big EBITDA is long-dated (Silver Spur/Power Express run-rate only in 2031–32). JSD argues the financially-levered structure means EPS CAGR should run "north of 20%" — well above the stated 10% — and that the Street floor moving 8%→9% is management breadcrumbing; his desk models ~13% EBITDA CAGR. PI is an affordability/"behind-the-meter" winner that aligns with the political cycle (vs. FTM/IPP losers). Optionality is fungible: if transmission stays hard to build, the BTM window widens; if FTM unlocks, the pipelines expand. Contract durations lengthening (12.5yr → 15–20yr discussions), build multiples ~5x with capex-per-kW now falling = more competitive + lower terminal recontracting risk. Pac-Northwest (Idaho/WA/OR) and a latent Northeast/New England pipeline need are unpriced upside. JSD survey: ~80/20 bullish; consensus sees Williams beating its 10% CAGR by 200–300bps and FID'ing ~$3bn more PI capex this year.
  • Bear (pushbacks captured on the JSD call): Expensive — 13–14x EBITDA, "fundamental/energy guys" see good growth but limited upside; "generals" chasing torque rotate to smaller, higher-operating-leverage BTM/compression names instead. PI terminal-value risk: contracts are "only" 10–12.5yr and what happens at recontracting (vs. grid economics) is an open question — the predominant bear thesis is that PI is lower-quality earnings. Skepticism the 6GW backlog is being replenished (mgmt declined to give a precise post-NEO figure). Mid-term de-rating risk on 2026/2028 election cycles and a gas-on-vibes peak; gas-demand momentum decelerates in H2. Leverage at 4.1x (peaking ~4.3x in 27 per JSD desk) needs the back-end ramp to land.
  • Where the sell-side stands: No broker PTs/ratings in the disk sources (briefing coverage is nil for WMB). Sell-side voices present on the Q1 call: JPMorgan (Tonet), Jefferies (Dumlin Smith), Wells Fargo, BMO, Scotiabank, Citi, Wolfe, BofA, Goldman (McKay), UBS, Seaport, Tuohy — uniformly engaged/constructive, debate centered on PI financing, backlog cadence and contract duration, not on whether the story works. The JSD desk is emphatically long, arguing Street numbers (now ~9% CAGR) are still too low.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Financing announcement — PI partnership/JV or "creative financing" (off-balance-sheet/insurance-money structure per JSD), promised within "a couple of months" of the May call; watch the mid-June industry conference (JSD flagged EIC, Kiawah, June 11).
  • Next PI FID — JSD/Street expect another NEO-sized BTM deal within 6 months (~$3bn FY26 PI capex). Counterparty disclosure on NEO when confidentiality lifts.
  • Socrates partial startup in Q3-26; commissioning learnings feed efficiency/redundancy on follow-ons (Aquila, Apollo).
  • Pac-NW phase 2 (Rockies Columbia Connector WA/OR) commercialization expected this year; storage FIDs (Pine Prairie, Gulf, Mountain West).
  • Permitting/judicial reform in the Senate (401-process and litigation-reform are Williams' two asks).
  • LNG: Woodside Louisiana LNG / Line 200 (3.1 Bcf/d, fully contracted 20-yr; 10% terminal stake, 1.5 MTPA offtake option) — first cargo tracking 2029.
  • Next print: Q2 FY26 (early Aug-26, seasonal trough).

Risks

  • PI terminal value / earnings quality — recontracting economics after 10–12.5yr BTM terms vs. eventual grid access (the core bear point).
  • Leverage timing — 4.1x now, peaking before the 28 ramp; financing must land without diluting or selling high-quality assets cheaply.
  • Gas supply/demand & price — dependence on basin supply and end-market demand; commodity volatility can pressure long-term transport/storage contracts and throughput (10-K risk summary, 2026-02-24).
  • Growth execution — explicit 10-K risk on pursuing and operationally implementing PI projects; supply-chain/turbine and labor cost inflation (capex-per-kW rising before bottoming, per JSD).
  • Permitting/litigation — interstate projects exposed to state 401 vetoes and prolonged litigation (mgmt cited 13 years on Atlantic Sunrise).
  • Counterparty/credit and competition (10-K risk summary). Policy/election risk to the gas-build thesis (JSD pushback).

AGX — Argan, Inc.

Wiki · generated 2026-06-18 · sources: E:\Wiki Felipe\AGX (10-K + transcripts; deck InvestorDay 2025-04-08 not read) · Master index: ../INDEX.md.

Note: no briefings roll-up or equity calls (thin coverage). Bull/Bear thesis derived primarily from the transcripts; sell-side and on-disk briefings coverage is nil.

🔖 Themes: AI Data-Center Power & Nuclear

Snapshot

Argan is an EPC (engineering, procurement, construction) holding company for energy infrastructure, operating primarily through its Gemma Power Systems subsidiary. Three segments: Power (~78% of revenue — gas-fired combined-cycle plants, plus solar/batteries, biofuel, biomass), Industrial (~20% — field services and fabrication of piping/pressure vessels/tanks) and Teledata (~2% — communications/data infrastructure, including data centers and federal installations) (Q1 FY27, 2026-06-04). The Power backlog is worth ~80% of historical consolidated revenue (10-K FY26, 2026-03-26: 80.1% in Fiscal 2026). It is a niche small-cap: management repeats that only "a handful of firms" can execute gigawatt-plus combined-cycle work under fixed-price contracts (Q1 FY27).

Current state (latest quarter — Q1 FY27, ended Apr-30-2026; call 2026-06-04)

  • Record revenue of $291M, +50% y/y (vs. $194M in Q1 FY26), driven by the ramp of recently awarded Power projects (Baugher, Q1 FY27).
  • Consolidated gross margin of 21% (vs. 19% y/y); by segment: Power 23.6%, Industrial 11.8%, Teledata 11%. Adj. EBITDA $56.4M (margin 19.4% vs. 16.3% y/y). Net income $46.1M, diluted EPS $3.24 (vs. $1.60 y/y) (Baugher, Q1 FY27).
  • Backlog $2.8B, a slight decline vs. $2.9B in the prior quarter — management emphasizes that backlog "swings" between the completion of one job and the announcement of the next. Composition: ~79% natural gas, 13% renewable, 8% industrial. Power backlog $2.5B; Industrial $225M; Teledata $8M (Watson, Q1 FY27).
  • Balance sheet: $974M in cash+investments, net liquidity $421M, no debt; stockholders' equity $474M. The business is asset-light (Watson, Q1 FY27).
  • Capital return: quarterly dividend $0.50 ($2/year; +33% in Sep-25, 3rd consecutive year of increase). Buyback raised to $200M (from $150M) and extended to 31-Jan-2030; $116.7M returned via repurchases since 2021. $33.6M returned in Q1 FY27 (Watson, Q1 FY27).
  • Projects: 8 power jobs in execution (6 thermal, 2 renewable). Backlog includes 4 gas plants in the US totaling >4.1 GW. Completed Trumbull (950 MW, Ohio — final completion) and the 3rd/3 Midwest solar+battery, both ahead of schedule. In progress: SLEC 1.2 GW (Texas), CPV 1.4 GW (Texas), 860 MW (Texas), 700 MW (US), Tarbert 300 MW biofuel + 170 MW thermal (Ireland). Industrial: a $125M data-center project (thermal expansion/energy storage tanks) + a new plant in North Carolina ($10-13M capex, completion still in 2026) (Watson, Q1 FY27).
  • FY26 (prior fiscal year, ref.): revenue $944.6M (+8.1%), net income $137.8M, EPS $9.74, EBITDA $162.8M (margin 17.2%) (Q4 FY26 summary, 2026-03-26).

Debate / thesis

  • Bull: structural demand for firm generation — electrification, onshoring, EVs and data centers strain the grid; combined-cycle gas is "the ideal solution" and few competitors execute gigawatt-plus jobs on a fixed-price basis (Watson, Q1 FY27). Long growth runway: management says it is targeting $2B of revenue "down the road" and capacity for 10-12 simultaneous jobs (currently at 8) (Watson, Q1 FY27). Track record of executing above contract — early substantial completion at Trumbull and Midwest drove a Power margin of 23.6% in Q1 (29% in Q4 FY26) (Watson/Baugher). A debt-free balance sheet + $421M net liquidity supports "several billion more" of backlog and provides a bankability/bonding advantage (Watson, Q1 FY27). Industrial is growing optionality: record revenue, $225M backlog and a multi-year runway in data-center tanks (Watson, Q1 FY27).
  • Bear: backlog does not grow linearly — it fell from $2.9B → $2.8B; large new jobs depend on development milestones outside Argan's control (air/water permits, gas access, turbines, financing) and the "10-18 months" window is repeated call after call (Watson, Q1 FY27). Margin is binary and back-end-loaded: management declines guidance, noting that quarterly margins have ranged from 11.4% to 25% over the last 2 years and that new projects still carry "many open risks" — visibility on final margin is low (Baugher, Q4 FY26; Watson, Q1 FY27). Renewable demand has "softened" — solar/battery exposure is less attractive (Watson, Q1 FY27). Concentration: a small-cap dependent on a few large customers and on fixed-price contracts with liquidated damages (10-K FY26).
  • Where the sell-side/buy-side stands: No sell-side note, PT or rating on disk — there are no archived briefings or equity calls for AGX (INDEX: 0 equity calls, 0 briefings). Observable coverage only via analysts present on the calls: CJS Securities (Chris Moore), Goldman Sachs (Ati Modak), Lake Street Capital (Rob Brown), JPMorgan (Michael Fairbanks). The tone of the questions is constructive, focused on capacity, the cadence of new jobs and margin sustainability — there is no PT/rating recoverable from the on-disk sources.

Catalysts / what to watch

  • Q2 FY27 print (next; date not confirmed on disk — the Q1 call indicated "when we report Q2") — watch: conversion of the promised large new jobs over 10-18 months.
  • New Power contract announcements (management guides "a handful" in 10-18 months); each gigawatt-plus moves the backlog materially.
  • Completion of the North Carolina fabrication plant (expected still in 2026) — a proxy for Industrial/data-center growth.
  • PJM emergency capacity auction — Watson is monitoring it; it could pull forward opportunities à la Texas Energy Fund (Q4 FY26).
  • Ramp of SLEC / CPV / Texas projects — where FY27 revenue materializes.

Risks

  • Fixed-price contracts with liquidated damages and re-performance obligations: cost overrun/delay becomes a direct loss on the project (10-K FY26).
  • Reliance on surety/bonding on a project-by-project basis; a change in the surety's risk assessment could limit the ability to compete (10-K FY26).
  • Customer concentration — a meaningful share of annual revenue comes from a few customers; the mix varies year to year (10-K FY26).
  • Timing/cancellation: contracts can be postponed or cancelled after award; start depends on third-party development milestones (10-K FY26; Watson, Q1 FY27).
  • Inflation/supply chain and trade policy (tariffs) on equipment for fixed-price projects; mitigated by early equipment purchasing, but not eliminated (10-K FY26).
  • Margin and backlog lumpiness — low visibility, no margin guidance (Baugher, Q4 FY26).
