Last reported FY (actual): OCF ¥5.5tn · Capex ¥4.9tn · FCF ¥0.6tn.

Sources

  • Filings: TM 20-F FY26 (2026-06-10); TM 20-F FY25 (2025-06-18).
  • Transcripts (6-K Q&A summaries, EN — NOT verbatim): TM FY26Q4 QA (2026-05-08); TM FY26Q2 QA (2025-11-05). Others on disk: TM_FY25Q3-QA_2025-02-05.md, TM_FY25Q4-QA_2025-05-08.md.
  • Decks: FY presentations (EX-99.2) embedded in the summaries above; PDFs in ../TM/ and ../TM/apresentações/.
  • Equity calls: 0 (see INDEX §TM).
  • Briefings: none (_briefings/by-ticker/TM.md does not exist).

Theme — 800V DC Data-Center Power

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · cross-company theme · sources: equity calls (_equity_calls / E:\equity_calls_transcripts), briefing roll-ups (_briefings/by-ticker), earnings transcripts, research library (E:\research_library). Company pages: ../00_INDEX.md · themes index: 00_THEMES.md.

What it is / why it matters

The jump from 48V/415V AC distribution to 800V DC inside the data center is the single biggest power-architecture change in 20+ years, and it is being forced by NVIDIA's roadmap rather than chosen. As GPU racks climb from ~130kW (Blackwell) toward 450kW (Rubin Ultra) and 600kW+ (next-gen "Feynman"/Kyber), the only way to push that power without drowning the rack in copper is to raise the voltage and drop the current (Citi 800V expert, 2026-06-16; UBS 800V expert, 2026-05-14). The transition reshuffles a multi-decade-stable supply chain: it kills low-frequency transformers and legacy UPS, mints a new market for solid-state transformers (SSTs), and re-rates the power-semi and connector content per megawatt. For a buy-sider this is both a content-growth story (analog/power semis, connectors, electrical OEMs) and a disruption story (who adapts vs. who gets disintermediated). Trigger to watch is simple: track the chip ramp and you track the 800V ramp (Citi, 2026-06-16).

State of play (latest)

  • Timeline pulled forward, not delayed. NVIDIA's Kyber/Rubin Ultra rack is the inflection point; expert consensus is the 800V DC standard arrives with that rack in 2H26 (AlphaSense expert call, 2026-06-03). FinTwit flagged it was "pulled ahead to Q3 2026" with NVDA Vera Rubin + GOOG next-gen DCs as first adopters (@aleabitoreddit / Commercial Times, via NVDA briefing 2026-06-15). JPM spec sales models 15–20% adoption for 800V near-term (JPM AI-power pitch, 2026-06-11).
  • It rolls out in phases, not a switch. Three flavors coexist (Citi, 2026-06-16): (1) bipolar ±400V DC sidecar — CSP-driven (Google/Meta "Mt. Diablo"), easiest because it sits inside the ~600V regulatory envelope and reuses EV/auto power electronics, retrofittable in brownfield; (2) monopolar 800V DC sidecar — NVIDIA-driven, needs supply-chain requalification; (3) full 800V SST architecture delivering DC straight to the rack — the eventual end-state, but not mainstream until late 2028–2029 (Citi 2026-06-16; UBS 2026-05-14; AlphaSense puts SSTs ~24–36 months out, 2026-06-03).
  • Power delivery on the board is changing too. Conversion moves from today's multi-stage (800→50→12→1V) toward vertical power delivery this year and integrated voltage regulators (IVR) over the next few years, shrinking VRM-to-processor distance to cut losses; ~11 semi suppliers are fighting across the conversion stages with no clear winner yet (Citi, 2026-06-16).
  • Content per MW is the bull metric. Monolithic Power is the purest play (>50% of sales data-center, guided 65% y/y DC-power growth); TI ~15% of sales; ADI, Infineon, Microchip all guiding 40–100% DC-power growth this year (Citi, 2026-06-16). The debate is whether >20% DC sales mix re-rates the analog group's multiple into the 30s — Citi says yes, "we've seen this movie before in networking."
  • Efficiency is the prize. Reducing conversion stages saves ~4–7% end-to-end efficiency (each conversion is 1–3%); NVIDIA's pitch to customers is "take the 7% and buy more chips" (AlphaSense expert, 2026-06-03; UBS, 2026-05-14). Lab studies cited move AC ~78% efficient to ~94% in the DC end-state.

Key debates

  • Bull / where the money is: Content per MW rises across the stack and re-rates the analog/power-semi group; new SST + DC-protection markets open from zero. TI is Citi's top idea (PT $345, best-positioned on capacity + GaN/WBG), with a positive catalyst watch on ON Semi (vertical GaN, Sept analyst day) (Citi, 2026-06-16). Connectors (Amphenol, TE) win on the copper math — at current architecture a 1MW rack needs ~200kg of busbar copper, "astronomical" and physically displacing compute space, so higher voltage is unavoidable (Citi, 2026-06-16). SST optionality is a free call on GEV/electrical OEMs (Bernstein power names, 2026-06-18).
  • Bear / what could break it: It's slower than the hype. A "very well-known enterprise/hyperscaler crossover" has hard-committed to no DC until 2032; sidecar is a floor-space eater feeding a 36-month chip on 15-year power amortization; SST players "don't even have proof of concept yet" with no field hours (AlphaSense expert, 2026-06-03). Real gating items are DC breakers/protection (DC arc doesn't self-extinguish — only 2–3 makers), insulation/clearance, EMI, and an OCP/UL safety spec not even published yet (UBS, 2026-05-14; AlphaSense, 2026-06-03). Incumbents (Eaton/Schneider) have little financial incentive to disrupt overflowing AC order books (AlphaSense, 2026-06-03).
  • Copper "tax" nuance: absolute copper content actually decreases per rack at 800V vs. 48V (current falls, busbar cross-section shrinks); the offset is the Rubin Ultra TDP step-up, leaving content roughly flat-to-modestly-up gen/gen — not the runaway copper bull some assume (UBS expert Q&A, 2026-05-14).
  • Timeline / inflection points: sidecar production must be ready 2H26; SST proof-of-concept this year → scale manufacturing mid-2027; GEV piloting an SST with a hyperscaler in fall 2026; full SST mainstream 2028–2029; long tail to 1,500V thereafter (UBS, 2026-05-14; Bernstein, 2026-06-18; Citi, 2026-06-16).

Who's exposed (companies)

TickerAngleRead
NVDAArchitect of the transition — Kyber/Rubin Ultra rack forces 800V; sets the supply-chain specNet positive driver. 800V "pulled ahead to Q3 2026"; Infineon/others joining MGX ecosystem (NVDA briefing, 2026-06-15; @qq_timmy)
GEVElectrification: HVDC, transformers, switchgear + SST pilot with a hyperscaler in fall '26Net positive optionality. Best-balance-sheet/scale to win SST; Street not yet crediting it (2029–30 TAM unlock) (Bernstein power names, 2026-06-18)
PWRElectrical infrastructure / buildout labor for the DC + grid interconnectNet positive on the broader power buildout; indirect to 800V architecture itself
VSTIPP / generation feeding the load behind the power-architecture shiftPositive on power demand; 800V is downstream of supply, not direct exposure
CEGNuclear/contracted generation; long-term clean baseload for hyperscalersPositive on power demand; grid-connected asset for contracting (Bernstein, 2026-06-18)
TLNMerchant generation w/ DC contracting capacityPositive on power demand; not a direct 800V play

Key non-universe names (not linked — no wiki pages): Monolithic Power (MPWR) purest analog play (>50% DC sales, +65% y/y); TI (TXN) Citi top idea on GaN/WBG capacity; ADI "late bloomer" via power acquisition; Infineon (IFX) & STMicro (STM) on SiC; ON Semi (ON) vertical GaN catalyst watch; Amphenol (APH) & TE Connectivity (TEL) connectors/copper; Wolfspeed (WOLF) SiC / 10kV; Vertiv (VRT) sidecar + power shelves; nVent (NVT) & Navitas (NVTS) (Rubin Ultra rack GaN); Flex (FLEX) / Celestica contract-manufacture the hyperscaler systems; private/startup SST names DG Matrix (CEO Haroon Inam, ex-charger), SPOC, plus a possible Tesla entry flagged by experts; Bloom Energy (BE) on the 800V-DC-native fuel-cell angle.

Sources

  • Equity calls: ../_equity_calls/Semis/2026-06-16_Citi_800V.md · ../_equity_calls/Semis/2026-05-14_UBS_800V-power.md · ../_equity_calls/Semis/2026-06-11_JPM_AI-power-pitch.md · ../_equity_calls/Overall/2026-06-18_Bernstein_power-names.md
  • Research library: E:\research_library\Semis\2026-06-03_AlphaSense_800V-DC-expert.md (Kyber timeline, sidecar→SST phases, suppliers) · E:\research_library\Semis\2026-06-09_JPM_800V-DC-power.pdf (800V stack, APH/FLEX/TEL/WOLF, copper tax, WBG) · E:\research_library\Overall\2026-05-26_SemiAnalysis_Inside-the-800VDC-Revolution-Pt1.pdf (4-phase transition, rack economics, content/MW) · E:\research_library\Overall\2026-06-16_SemiAnalysis_DG-Matrix-800V.md · E:\research_library\Semis\2026-04-27_Power-Semis-800V-GaN-Mini-Note.pdf
  • Briefings: _briefings/by-ticker/NVDA.md (800V pulled ahead Q3'26; Infineon/MGX; HVDC 2–5%/stage loss), _briefings/by-ticker/GOOG.md, _briefings/by-ticker/LITE.md, _briefings/by-ticker/AAOI.md (SemiAnalysis 800VDC/CPO read-through)

Theme — AI Data-Center Power & Nuclear

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · cross-company theme · sources: equity calls (_equity_calls / E:\equity_calls_transcripts), briefing roll-ups (_briefings/by-ticker), earnings transcripts, research library. Company pages: ../00_INDEX.md · themes index: 00_THEMES.md.

What it is / why it matters

The AI build-out has turned electricity into the binding constraint on compute. Hyperscaler capex is running toward a ~$1.2T buy-side bogey for 2027 ("could be 1.3, could be 1.4," Jefferies power/hyperscalers, 2026-05-08) — and a data center is useless without firm, 24/7 power to plug into. Cantor frames the supply imbalance bluntly: NVIDIA can ship accelerators faster than anyone can build the buildings to house them — ~40 GW of chips into the US between 2025–2030 vs. ~21 GW of data center capacity built over the prior 25 years (Cantor/CoreWeave, 2026-06-18). For a buy-sider, this is the rare theme where the bottleneck is physical — turbines, transformers, interconnects, baseload electrons, and the labor to assemble them — so the money concentrates in whoever owns scarce, contractable supply: nuclear/merchant generation (CEG, VST, TLN), gas-turbine OEMs (GEV), EPC labor (PWR, AGX), and gas midstream (WMB). The single biggest risk to the bull case is not demand — it is the grid, the labor pool, and timing.

State of play (latest)

  • Demand is staggering and accelerating, not peaking. Microsoft backlog +99% y/y to $627B; Oracle "half a trillion"; CoreWeave "pulled a Wayne Gretzky" at $99B backlog and declared itself sold out; 2026 hyperscaler capex is set to accelerate across the board (Jefferies, 2026-05-08). VST cites hyperscaler capex >$700B in 2026, ~+50% y/y (VST Q4 FY25, 2026-02-26); CEG calls hyperscaler 2026 capex "almost 75% higher than last year" (CEG Q1 FY26, 2026-05-11).
  • The grid — not generation — is the bottleneck. Bernstein: "generation we don't see as the constraint, we see the grid as more of the bottleneck," with >2,000 GW of projects in the FERC interconnection queue and a 4–5 year wait to connect anywhere in the US (Bernstein power names, 2026-06-18). Jefferies' real-world read is sharper: frustration with utility interconnection has "reached a boil," pushing customers to behind-the-meter generation, batteries, and "bring-your-own-generation" (Jefferies, 2026-05-08).
  • Power demand inflecting after two flat decades. Bernstein models +3% US power-demand CAGR to 2030 (range heard 2.5–4.5%), with data-center electricity roughly doubling from ~250 TWh to ~500 TWh by 2030 on ~40 GW of incremental DC capacity (Bernstein power names, 2026-06-18). VST sees ERCOT load +5–6% and PJM +2–3% to 2030 (VST Q1 FY26, 2026-05-07).
  • Gas is the bridge; nuclear/geothermal the only long-run baseload displacement. Survey of 50+ DC operators: 98% prefer grid (or grid + on-site backup), with reliability/uptime and speed-to-power the deciding factors — and "three years out is effectively unacceptable" (Bernstein, 2026-06-18). Behind-the-meter stays a 5–10% niche (more expensive than grid), not the 50% some bulls model (Bernstein, 2026-06-18).
  • Nuclear is the scarce premium product. CEG runs the largest US nuclear fleet (~22 GW, 96.8% capacity factor) with ~900–1,000 MW of uprates identified and the Crane (ex-Three Mile Island) restart progressing (CEG Q1 FY26, 2026-05-11). VST has ~3.8 GW of nuclear under 20-yr PPAs (Amazon at Comanche Peak; Meta across Perry/Davis-Besse/Beaver Valley) (VST Q1 FY26). TLN's anchor is the ~1.9 GW front-of-the-meter AWS PPA at Susquehanna (TLN Q1 FY26, 2026-05-05).
  • Gas-turbine OEMs sold out years forward with pricing power. GEV ended Q1 with 100 GW of gas contracts (44 GW firm + 56 GW SRAs), order book filled through 2027 with 2030 slots filling ahead of 2029 (permitting-gated); H1'26 orders priced 10–20 pts higher $/kW y/y, plus a ~20–30% US price premium vs. Siemens (GEV Q1 FY26, 2026-04-22; GEVernova preview, 2026-04-17). Industry-wide turbine capacity ramps 55 GW (2025) → 87 GW (2030), yet the US is still "~10 GW of gas short every year" (GEVernova preview, 2026-04-17).
  • Labor is now the #1 constraint. Both Jefferies desks: labor — not turbines — is the gating factor; projects booked "three years in the future"; construction EPC names (Quanta, MasTec) seeing "shocking positive revisions," with Quanta's near-term growth talked up toward ~30% (Jefferies, 2026-05-08).

Key debates

  • Bull / where the money is: Demand is real, supply is static, and the scarce assets re-rate. CEG: ~$0.75T of data-center capital projected — "twice the size of the 3 largest listed US utilities" — and CEG sells "the product no one else has ready: firm, clean, 24/7 power, available now" (CEG Q3 FY25 / Q1 FY26). GEV is "an earnings-revisions machine," up off pace of EBITDA growth, with Electrification flipping from "peaking" to accelerating — TAM doubled $150B→$300B in a month and the $200B total-backlog target pulled forward to '27 (GEVernova callback, 2026-04-22). WMB: pipelines lag gas-gen, and "Williams is the ultimate manifestation of this thesis," compounding via 5x-build-multiple behind-the-meter gas for data centers (JSD via WMB page, 2026-05-06). PWR is the "pick-and-shovel" labor layer targeting >2x earnings power by 2030 (15–20% EPS CAGR) (PWR Q4 FY25, 2026-02-19).
  • Bear / what could break it: Timing and slippage. Data-center delays are real — EIA, ERCOT and PJM all cut 2026 demand forecasts; "~50% of data centers delayed / canceled" headlines, though Jefferies calls this "normal course" (Jefferies, 2026-05-08). The big hyperscaler nuclear deals keep slipping (CEG promised announcements that haven't landed; some conversations "paused" on PJM regulatory clarity) (CEG Q1 FY26). Management teams are more cautious than the tape: VST says meaningful DC tightening doesn't begin until "late 2027 or early 2028" and load growth is "not at the extremely elevated levels… forecast by many third parties" (VST Q4 FY25). GEV order-peak risk is live — Q4'25's 24 GW / $22.2B order quarter is the comp to beat (GEVernova callback). Bernstein/Cantor procurement expert thinks the build pace is "plateauing" — "I think last year was the peak," with power and people the two gates (Bernstein DC buildout, 2026-06-11). And policy/rate-payer politics bite in an election year: PJM bills rose ~$20/mo, IPPs that signed rate-payer-protection pledges can't contract existing resources, and IPPs "are not participating in the rally" (Jefferies, 2026-05-08).
  • Timeline / inflection points: Behind-the-meter and gas-gen scramble now (band-aid, 5–10% of market); large H-class turbines stay under-supplied through 2030 (relative positive), while diesel gensets and small on-site gen move to over-supply by 2027–2029 (Bernstein power names, 2026-06-18). Nuclear restarts/uprates land 2027–2034. Grid/electrification is the multi-decade compounder beyond 2030 (Bernstein, 2026-06-18). The cleanest near-term tell on supply: track the chip ramp and the order/backlog cadence at GEV (Q2 FY26 print, ~late July).

Who's exposed (companies)

TickerAngleRead
NVDADemand driver — accelerator shipments are the root of the power scrambleThe cause of the scarcity: ~40 GW of chips into the US 2025–2030 vs. ~21 GW DC built over prior 25 yrs (Cantor, 2026-06-18); DC ~90%+ of revenue, Q2 FY27 guide $91B (NVDA Q1 FY27, 2026-05-20)
CEGLargest US nuclear fleet; firm 24/7 carbon-free PPAs to hyperscalersNet positive, timing-dependent. ~22 GW nuclear @96.8% CF; ~900–1,000 MW uprates; Crane restart; hyperscaler deals keep slipping on PJM clarity (CEG Q1 FY26, 2026-05-11)
VSTLargest merchant IPP; ~3.8 GW nuclear under 20-yr PPAs (AMZN/META) + 26 GW CCGT at ~60% utilNet positive operating leverage; mgmt cautious on pace — tightening "late 2027/early 2028" (VST Q4 FY25; Q1 FY26)
TLNMerchant IPP "powering-AI" play; ~1.9 GW FOTM AWS PPA at SusquehannaPositive on FCF/sh "flywheel"; single-plant nuclear concentration + PJM co-location rules unresolved (TLN Q1 FY26, 2026-05-05)
GEVGas turbines + grid Electrification — the pure-play on generation and the gridNet positive, order-peak risk live. 100 GW contracted, +10–20pts $/kW, ~20–30% premium; Electrification TAM $150B→$300B (GEV Q1 FY26, 2026-04-22; preview 2026-04-17)
PWREPC / labor layer — transmission, gas backup, DC interconnect; DC ~10% of workNet positive secular. Record $48.5B backlog; targeting >2x earnings by 2030; labor is the moat and the constraint (PWR Q1 FY26, 2026-04-30; Jefferies 2026-05-08)
AGXSmall-cap EPC for gigawatt-plus combined-cycle gas plants (Gemma)Positive niche; "handful of firms" can do fixed-price GW-scale; >4.1 GW US gas in backlog, but backlog is lumpy (AGX Q1 FY27, 2026-06-04)
WMBGas midstream (Transco) + behind-the-meter Power Innovation gas-gen for DCsNet positive; ~5x-build-multiple BTM gas, 10%+ EBITDA CAGR to 2030; bear = PI terminal-value/earnings-quality after 10–12.5 yr terms (WMB Q1 FY26, 2026-05-05)

Key non-universe / read-through names (no wiki pages): Entergy — vertically integrated regulated winner building Meta's Hyperion (Louisiana, +5 GW added), called "one of the biggest winners" alongside NiSource (Jefferies, 2026-05-08). Bloom Energy (BE) — fast-to-connect on-site fuel cells; ~5 deals @25 MW, scaling to ~2 GW by YE26, no-water/modular, but valuation-driven and announcement-led (Bernstein power names, 2026-06-18). CoreWeave / Nebius / IREN — neoclouds renting GPU capacity into the same power-scarce world; IREN has "a ton of power" and undisclosed ERCOT batch capacity (Cantor, 2026-06-18). Caterpillar / Cummins / Baker Hughes (TurbineX) — gas/mobile gensets doubling order books (Jefferies, 2026-05-08; Bernstein DC buildout, 2026-06-11). Fluence (FLNC) — grid-scale batteries, +~100% off lows on DC-storage interest (Jefferies, 2026-05-08). Siemens Energy / Mitsubishi Heavy / Doosan — the rest of the turbine oligopoly under GEV's pricing umbrella. Vistra's Helix Digital Infrastructure ($10B w/ KKR/NVDA/KIA) flagged as emerging theme (MS, briefing 2026-06-12).

Sources

  • Equity calls (E:\equity_calls_transcripts\Overall\): 2026-05-08 Jefferies — power & hyperscalers · 2026-06-11 Bernstein — datacenter buildout (procurement expert) · 2026-06-18 Bernstein — power names (initiation) · 2026-06-18 Cantor — CoreWeave / DC power · 2026-04-17 GEVernova preview · 2026-04-22 GEVernova callback.
  • Company pages (synthesized; carry their own transcript/10-K/briefing attribution): CEG · VST · TLN · GEV · PWR · WMB · AGX · NVDA.
  • Briefings (_briefings/by-ticker): GEV (Jefferies/JPM, May–Jun '26) · VST (MS Helix theme, 2026-06-12) · PWR (BofA mega-bull, 2026-05-11). _No briefing roll-ups on disk for CEG, TLN, WMB, AGX (coverage gap)._
  • Related theme: 800V DC Data-Center Power (the power-architecture / SST angle inside the rack).

Theme — CoWoS / Advanced Packaging

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · cross-company theme · sources: equity calls (../_equity_calls/Semis), briefing roll-ups (../_briefings/by-ticker), earnings transcripts, company pages. Company pages: ../00_INDEX.md.

What it is / why it matters

CoWoS (chip-on-wafer-on-substrate) is the 2.5D advanced-packaging technology that stitches GPU/ASIC logic dies and HBM stacks onto a silicon interposer — it is the physical gate on every leading-edge AI accelerator, and through 2027 it is as binding a constraint as 3nm wafers themselves. The buy-side reason to care: CoWoS (plus its 3D sibling SoIC and the substrate/OSAT layers around it) sits upstream of the entire AI compute P&L. TSMC owns the bulk of the capacity, so its expansion cadence rate-limits NVDA/AVGO/AMD shipments; but the more interesting move is that value is now visibly migrating out of TSMC toward OSATs (ASE, Amkor), substrate vendors, and — as a wildcard — Intel's EMIB panel-level packaging. A former TSMC sales exec put the punchline bluntly: CoWoS was "a technology of the future" that TSMC nearly killed for a decade because it wasn't profitable, and is now "something everybody's depending on" (TSMC former exec, 2026-04-14).

State of play (latest)

  • Capacity trajectory raised repeatedly through 1H26. JPM (Gokul) now models total CoWoS capacity at ~115K / 175K / 220K wpm by end-26 / 27 / 28E, with total CoWoS consumption +86% this year and +30% next (Gokul/JPM CoWoS update, 2026-05-27). Jefferies (Rankie Wong) independently moved its forecast to 180K / 220K wpm by end-27E / 28E (NVDA briefing, 2026-06-05). Earlier waypoints: Gokul had 145K/175K end-26/27 in mid-May, and 115K/155K/175K on 2026-04-13 — the numbers have only inched up.
  • The non-TSMC share is the real story. The portion of CoWoS not done at TSMC is being raised hard — to roughly 35K wpm (~25–30% of supply) by end-27, up from only ~3–5% / 10–12K today, mostly ASE + Amkor, a ~4x jump in OSAT full-stack process in 2027 (Gokul, 2026-05-27). ASE skews to AMD (Venice CPU) + some NVDA; Amkor to NVDA (CoWoS-R for Vera) + some Broadcom.
  • SoIC is the fastest-ramping leg. 3D hybrid-bonding capacity goes from ~10–14K today to ~40K end-27 → ~65K wpm end-28E (Gokul, 2026-05-27). Driver is migration of high-end AI accelerators to SoIC — NVDA Feynman, AMD MI, Google TPU V9, Trainium 4 — plus a slice for silicon-photonics/CPO integration. TSMC's new AP7 fab phases are being dedicated to SoIC while CoWoS is increasingly outsourced.
  • Substrate is the next bottleneck. Substrate (ABF/CCL) supply can grow only ~15%/yr against >15% demand; spot ABF +30%, contract +5–10%, with first LTAs locking ~40–50% price hikes off end-25 base (Gokul/JPM Computex team — Jerry, 2026-06-11; NVDA briefing dnystedt, also flags Ajinomoto pondering 30% hike). Because EMIB-style packaging has no interposer, value that TSMC used to capture accrues directly to substrate vendors (Gokul, 2026-05-27).
  • TSMC is building packaging in Arizona. The US buildout now contemplates ~5 CoWoS assembly/test factories alongside the wafer fabs, plus the first TSMC R&D fab outside Taiwan (TSMC former exec, 2026-04-14; TSM page).

Key debates

  • Bull / where the money is: Shortage is "the name of the game" and persists into 2027+ across 3nm wafers, memory and advanced packaging (Gokul, Computex, 2026-06-11). The cleanest expressions are the picks-and-shovels layers that capture spillover regardless of who wins the GPU war — OSATs (ASE/KSE), bonder/inspection equipment (BESI for hybrid bonding, plus AMAT/KLAC on advanced-packaging tools), and substrate (Unimicron, Japanese vendors Ibiden/Shinko). KSE and the OSATs were "quite constructive" on raising capex; clean-room construction itself is now a constraint (Gokul, 2026-06-11).
  • Bear / what could break it: Value migration away from TSMC is the structural risk to the capacity owner — CoWoS-R/-S increasingly farmed to ASE/Amkor, and Intel EMIB as a genuine second source. Demand-double-counting also lurks: customers' forecasts historically sum to multiples of the real end market, which TSMC itself discounts (former exec, 2026-04-14). And specific programs slip — Rubin cut to ~2.0–2.2M units this year on HBM4 issues (from 2.8–2.9M); AMD MI450 CoWoS-L pulled down for 2026 on the 2nm ramp delay before a strong 2027 (Gokul, 2026-04-13 / 2026-05-27).
  • Intel packaging as trojan horse. EMIB (and EMIB-T for the rumored 4-die Rubin Ultra SKU) is the credible non-TSMC route — SK Hynix is doing joint 2.5D/EMIB R&D with Intel amid the CoWoS crunch (NVDA briefing jukan05/dnystedt, 2026-05-11/12). The flagship test case is Google TPU V9 "Puma Fish" (MediaTek): plan A is Intel "EMEM"/EMIB with ~25–28 silicon bridges (yield unproven at that complexity), plan B is TSMC CoWoS-L; Google has pre-paid Intel to reserve 2028 substrate capacity, decision expected late-26/early-27 (Gokul, 2026-05-27). One bull case sizes Feynman I/O-die packaging at ~$4.8B with "Intel w/ EMIB the only game in town" if 4-die wins (NVDA briefing bubbleboi). Counter-view: Jeff Pu argues 3DIC, not CoPoS/4-die, breaks the area limit (NVDA briefing, 2026-05-16); JPM frames the Intel piece as "mostly packaging, not TSMC replacement" (Samik Chatterjee, 2026-06-09).
  • CoPoS pushed out. Panel-level CoPoS now looks like 2029–2030, not 2028 — TSMC keeps extending CoWoS (14X reticle in 2028; AMD MI500 already targeting ~14–15X). NVDA Feynman on CoPoS is "touch and go" (Gokul, 2026-05-27). ~60–70% of CoPoS equipment is shareable with CoWoS; the delta is glass/TGV/panel tooling.

Who's exposed (companies)

TickerAngleRead
TSMCapacity owner — CoWoS + SoIC + interposer; ~70% foundry shareNet positive on volume (consumption +86% '26 / +30% '27, Gokul) but value migration to OSATs/substrate/EMIB is the structural watch; FY26 capex ~10–20% to packaging/test.
NVDALargest CoWoS consumer (Rubin/Blackwell CoWoS-L, Vera CoWoS-R)Gated by packaging, not demand — Rubin cut to ~2.0–2.2M units '26 on HBM4; Vera CoWoS-R increasingly farmed to Amkor/ASE; '27 upside if more CoWoS-R capacity found (Gokul).
AVGOCustom-ASIC consumer — Google TPU (CoWoS-S/-L), Tomahawk 6 networkingBiggest '27 upgrade was Broadcom (~400–425K wpm, mostly TPU); CoWoS-based Meta/OpenAI volumes ramping slower; exposed to the V9 EMIB-vs-CoWoS decision via MediaTek (Gokul, 2026-04-13/05-27).
AMDGPU/CPU consumer — MI450 CoWoS-L + Venice CPU; $10B TW supply-chain invest'26 cut to ~77K (from ~90K) on MI450 2nm slip; '27 raised to ~180K (MI450 + Venice). Migrating Venice CPU to ASE, broad shift to 2.5D packaging (Gokul, 2026-05-27).

Also in prose: AMAT / KLAC / LRCX (advanced-packaging deposition/inspection/etch tools — repeated briefing read-throughs as CoWoS expands), BESI (hybrid-bonding bonder for SoIC, with Shibaura a potential second source), ASE / Amkor / KSE / Powertech (OSAT capacity build), Unimicron / Ibiden / Shinko (substrate), and Intel Foundry (EMIB as the trojan-horse second source — see [no dedicated page yet]).

Sources

  • Equity calls (../_equity_calls/Semis): 2026-04-13 Gokul/JPM — CoWoS; 2026-05-27 Gokul/JPM — CoWoS update; 2026-06-11 Gokul/JPM — Computex takeaways; 2026-04-14 TSMC former exec — foundry.
  • Briefings (../_briefings/by-ticker): NVDA — Jefferies CoWoS 180K/220K end-27/28 (2026-06-05), Rubin Ultra EMIB-T / 3DIC debate (2026-05-16), SK Hynix–Intel EMIB R&D (2026-05-11/12), ABF +30% spot (dnystedt); AVGO — MediaTek adds Intel EMIB alongside TSMC, AVGO also a CoWoS client (2026-06); Google COT ambition read-through.
  • Company pages: TSM, NVDA, AVGO, AMD.

Theme — Custom ASIC / TPU (vs merchant GPU)

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · cross-company theme · sources: equity calls (../_equity_calls/Semis), briefing roll-ups (../_briefings/by-ticker), earnings transcripts, company pages. Company pages: ../00_INDEX.md.

What it is / why it matters

Every frontier-model builder and hyperscaler is now running a two-track silicon strategy: buy merchant GPUs (NVDA) for flexibility and time-to-market, and co-design custom accelerators (ASIC/XPU) for their highest-volume, most-predictable workloads to claw back margin, power efficiency and supply control. The buy-side reason to care is that this is the single biggest swing factor in how the ~$3–4T/yr end-of-decade AI infra TAM (Jensen's framing, NVDA Q1 FY27, 2026-05-20) gets split — and it pits a high-margin merchant monopoly (NVDA ~75% GM) against a lower-margin but stickier custom-silicon complex (AVGO, MRVL, Google's internal COT team). The fight is not winner-take-all: the consensus shape is a durable split (Arcuri/UBS pegs ~70/30 GPU/ASIC long-term, 2026-06-04), but the marginal gigawatt increasingly skews custom, and that marginal mix is what moves the stocks.

State of play (latest)

  • AVGO is the clearest ASIC long, and FY28 is the real number. JPM (Harlan Sur) frames Broadcom's bottoms-up order book at ~6.0–6.5M TPU units for FY27 ≈ ~$100B of TPU revenue alone, +~$45B networking → >$150B AI revenue FY27; FY28 scales to ~20–22GW of deployment → $250–300B AI revenue, ~$35–38 EPS vs Street ~$25 (Sur, 2026-06-05 / 2026-06-17). Capstone's internal model (Felipe, 2026-06-08) lands similarly: 2026E rev $115B / 2027E $190B / 2028E $315B, with Anthropic the largest AI client by GW in 2028 (../AVGO.md).
  • The Google 5-yr LTA is the anchor datapoint. Broadcom–Google signed a 5-year agreement (through 2031) in April with guaranteed, rising annual revenue commitments spanning the next four TPU generations — V8, V9, V10, V11 (Sur, 2026-06-17). Sur's read: Google would not lock rising guaranteed payments through 2031 if it intended to phase Broadcom out. Broadcom has 12 years / 14 of Google's most complex compute ASICs behind it and an ~18-month lead over the internal team.
  • TPU roadmap, concretely. Current high-volume runner is the V8i "Sunfish" (Broadcom-designed inferencing chip, 2 compute die / 8 HBM stacks), qualified mid-2025, starts shipping in volume ~now (~30 days, per Sur 2026-06-17) and is ~70% of Google's FY27 TPU demand. Next is V9 2nm (4 compute die, 16 HBM stacks, 400G/lane SerDes) — "one of the most complex chips Broadcom is designing today," IP started 1H25, design 2H25, ramps calendar 28, no delay per JPM checks. V10 IP development has already begun. Jefferies separately tags TPU v10 "Icefish" (../GOOG.md, 2026-06-12).
  • AWS Trainium is the price/perf play. Amazon custom silicon >$20B run-rate, +40% q/q; Trainium commitments >$225B; Trainium3 "nearly fully subscribed" (AMZN Q1 FY26, 2026-04-29). Bernstein (Shmulik, 2026-04-30): Trainium 30–40% better price/perf vs comparable GPUs, letting AMZN hold capex flat while peers raised — and Jassy flags a "good chance in the next couple of years" of selling Trainium racks externally (../AMZN.md). Trainium4 is in design with multiple scale-up variants (UALink-native + NVLink; MRVL/Alchip as ASIC partners — Sur, 2026-06-17).
  • NVDA's counter: custom is a long, slow migration, not a near-term share cliff. Per UBS's NVDA callback (Arcuri, 2026-05-21), NVDA argues Anthropic/OpenAI face a "very long intermediary step" — they must stand up their own data centers before meaningful ASIC infra lands, and in the interim are growing as NVDA customers (Anthropic's latest NVDA deal is all inference). NVDA also points to networking share gains and a re-segmented Data Center ($75B of $82B, Q1 FY27) that has out-grown hyperscale capex five straight quarters (../NVDA.md).
  • Frontier-lab ASICs run through Broadcom. All of Google's external TPU customers — Anthropic, Meta (which signed a deal to rent Google TPUs), and others — use the Broadcom Sunfish part, not the COT/MediaTek chip (Sur, 2026-06-17). The Apollo/Blackstone 20GW XPU SPV finances Anthropic + OpenAI compute through 2028 (first $35B tranche), with Anthropic ~3.5GW and OpenAI ~1.3GW in FY27 (../AVGO.md).

Key debates

  • Bull / where the money is: Custom silicon wins the marginal gigawatt because it gives frontier labs better compute-per-dollar and per-watt on their highest-volume workloads, and labs will cut discretionary off-the-shelf GPU buys before their strategic ASIC programs when capex tightens (Sur, 2026-06-17). AVGO is the cleanest expression — guaranteed rising Google revenue through 2031, V8i ramping now, V9 2nm into C28, plus a near-monopoly (~70–75%) on the attached scale-up/scale-out/scale-across networking that rides on every TPU rack regardless of who designs the compute die (Sur, 2026-06-05; 2026-06-04). AMZN's Trainium adds a second, price/perf-led leg with optionality on external rack sales.
  • Bear / what could break it: Arcuri (UBS) would buy NVDA over AVGO if forced to choose (2026-06-04): the Google-diversification drumbeat "only gets louder," AVGO's business is lumpier/more concentrated (Google + a few labs), and AVGO's multiple "still has more to come down" while NVDA's has already collapsed. NVDA's degrees of freedom are real — it can drop a $20B Vera CPU program "out of thin air" and get supply, while AVGO shows back-end/substrate/packaging constraints (Arcuri). Share-in-a-growing-market is a weak bear: Arcuri argues share only matters in a mature market, and NVDA is gaining share at Anthropic, the recognized frontier leader.
  • The Google COT / MediaTek wildcard. Google's internal CLT/COT team (using MediaTek for ~10% of the design) builds the training track — the V8T "Zerafish" (1 compute die / 6 HBM, ~2x less complex than Broadcom's V8i). Sur's checks say it still needs further optimization and likely another respin of both compute and I/O die, and that Google's intent is supplier optionality (6–8 ASIC programs running at any time), not replacing Broadcom on the highest-complexity inferencing roadmap (2026-06-17). Bear read (briefings): longer-term COT-model bypass risk is the structural overhang on AVGO (../AVGO.md). Note the inversion vs. intuition: inferencing is now the more complex/higher-ASP track (agentic, more SRAM, embedded ARM cores), which is the track Broadcom keeps (Sur, 2026-06-05).
  • Why Hock won't raise FY27. Despite better visibility, AVGO did not raise its ">$100B" FY27 AI guide — the omission, not the print, drove the -12.6% post-Q2 reaction. Sur reads this as (a) Hock's style (won't ratchet every quarter, a dig at MRVL) and (b) multi-GW greenfield programs now gated by power/data-center readiness, not chip supply (2026-06-17; Arcuri concurs, 2026-06-04).
  • Timeline / inflection: V8i Sunfish volume now → V9 2nm ramps C28 → OpenAI ASIC tapes out late-2026, ships 2H27, big ramp 2028 (Arcuri, 2026-06-04). The ASIC-vs-GPU mix debate "is not going to go away — it gets louder and louder" (Arcuri, 2026-06-04).

Who's exposed (companies)

TickerAngleRead
AVGOPrimary custom-silicon partner — Google TPU (V8i/V9/V10/V11), Anthropic/OpenAI/Meta ASICs, + attached networking (Tomahawk/Jericho)Net positive, the cleanest ASIC long: ~$100B FY27 TPU + ~$45B networking → >$150B AI; FY28 $250–300B / $35–38 EPS scenario; 5-yr Google LTA through 2031 with rising guaranteed revenue (Sur, 2026-06-05/17). Watch GM compression + COT optionality + FY27 not-raised.
GOOGTPU owner/operator — internal silicon + emerging merchant TPU supplier (external sales late C26)Net positive — full-stack power/perf/ROIC edge; backlog $462B now includes TPU hardware sales; Blackstone 500MW TPU JV; v10 "Icefish." Custom-silicon mix supports durable high-20s/30s cloud margins (GS, 2026-04-30).
AMZNTrainium/Graviton — price/perf-led custom silicon, Anthropic anchorNet positive — custom silicon >$20B run-rate (+40% q/q), Trainium commitments >$225B, T3 nearly fully subscribed, 30–40% better price/perf (Shmulik, 2026-04-30); optional external Trainium rack sales = unpriced upside.
NVDAMerchant-GPU incumbent at risk — the share the ASICs are takingNet negative at the margin but the bear case is contested: ~70/30 GPU/ASIC split holds, NVDA gaining share at Anthropic, $1T Blackwell+Rubin visibility C25–C27, out-grew hyperscale capex 5 straight Qs. Risk is sentiment/multiple (still a "funding short"), not near-term demand (Moore/Arcuri).

Also in prose: MRVL / Marvell — JPM's #2 semis pick; owns the Microsoft Maia and AWS Trainium ASIC roadmaps Broadcom does not (Sur defended both as un-lost, 2026-06-17), plus CXL, Ethernet scale-up, and a possible Google LPU win — the second-source beneficiary of the ASIC theme. Anthropic and OpenAI are the frontier-lab demand drivers behind the Apollo/Blackstone 20GW SPV (Anthropic ~3.5GW / OpenAI ~1.3GW FY27, ~7GW each FY28 per Sur), both routed through Broadcom Sunfish silicon today; OpenAI's own first-gen ASIC tapes out late-2026, ramps 2028. Meta MTIA plus its TPU-rental deal with Google add a further ~1–3GW of custom demand (Sur, 2026-06-17). Apple — Broadcom is doing a server-CPU ASIC, tapes out 2027 (Sur, 2026-06-05).

Sources

  • Equity calls (../_equity_calls/Semis): 2026-06-17 Sur/JPM — AVGO/Marvell, V9 2nm & 5-yr Google LTA, COT debate; 2026-06-05 Sur/JPM — post-AVGO callback, FY28 ~$300B AI, Sunfish 70%; 2026-06-04 Arcuri/UBS — AVGO networking, 70/30 GPU-ASIC split, prefers NVDA; 2026-05-21 Arcuri/UBS — NVDA recap, Anthropic ASIC migration "long intermediary step".
  • Briefings (../_briefings/by-ticker): AVGO — 20GW SPV, FY27-not-raised reaction, COT/MediaTek; GOOG — external TPU sales, Blackstone JV, v10 "Icefish"; AMZN — Trainium commitments/price-perf; NVDA — ASIC share-erosion debate, funding-short status.
  • Company pages: AVGO, GOOG, AMZN, NVDA.

Theme — HBM / Memory Super-Cycle

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · cross-company theme · sources: equity calls (../_equity_calls/Semis), briefing roll-ups (../_briefings/by-ticker), earnings transcripts, company pages. Company pages: ../00_INDEX.md · themes index: 00_THEMES.md.

What it is / why it matters

Memory has gone from the cyclical victim of the semi complex to its tightest, most profitable corner. HBM — the stacked DRAM that feeds AI accelerators — is sold out for all of CY26 on price and volume, including HBM4 (MU page; Mehrotra, Micron Q1 FY26, 2025-12-17), and that scarcity has spread back into conventional DRAM and NAND because every HBM die displaces ~3x the wafer of a commodity DDR5 die. The result is a pricing melt-up that has run the memory names parabolic (MU +30% in a week, $748→$971; Susquehanna 2026-05-30 via MU briefing) and re-rated the equity story from "trough multiple on cyclical earnings" toward an earnings-durability framework underwritten by multi-year contracts. The buy-side question this theme resolves: is this a structurally longer up-cycle (LTAs + supply discipline + AI memory-intensity), or the textbook commodity top that always follows astronomical ASPs? The on-disk evidence (May–Jun 2026) is two-sided and the debate is no longer direction but duration.

State of play (latest)

  • HBM sold out CY26; pricing into 2027-28. Micron's entire CY26 HBM supply, HBM4 included, is committed on price and volume; HBM TAM ~$35B (2025) → ~$100B by 2028 (~40% CAGR), pulled in two years vs prior (MU page, Q1 FY26, 2025-12-17). HBM blended ASP +~30% for next year (Samsung/Hynix) = ~10% like-for-like + mix from HBM4, but HBM4 ramp pushed to 2H so more of the blended ASP effect spills into 2027; HBM price negotiations only begin July after the June quarter (JPM/Jay, memory-China call, 2026-05-28).
  • DRAM/NAND pricing still ripping. 2Q26 DRAM ASP +50-60% q/q, NAND +75-100% q/q; 3Q26 DRAM +~20% q/q (memory ests, briefing 2026-05-29, MU). Micron Q2 FY26 already printed DRAM pricing "mid-sixties %" q/q and NAND "high-seventies %", 75% gross margin, with guidance to ~81% GM next quarter (MU page, 2026-03-18). JPM models full-year server-grade DRAM +~25%, with 3Q DRAM/NAND +~60% q/q and upside to 20% if LTAs aren't signed in 2Q (JPM/Jay, 2026-05-28).
  • HDD pulled into the same tractor beam. With NAND tight, Seagate/WDC are running ~90% sequential incremental gross margins and, unlike NAND, managing price up "more on a steady basis" to give predictability — and no longer giving back generation-change discounts in a supply-constrained market (Samik/JPM, memory-HDD preview, 2026-06-12). Memory cost pass-through is now a visible gross-margin headwind across the hardware complex (Calix, Motorola, Flex — same call).
  • Demand fuel beyond HBM: CPU + KV-cache. NVIDIA Vera standalone CPU ~500k units this year, ~3M in 2027; AI-CPU demand could lift total server DRAM bit demand >15-20%, and Vera/in-house CPUs use higher-content LPDDR5X SO-DIMM/SoCAM, draining low-power DRAM supply (JPM/Jay, 2026-05-28; mechanism corroborated Arcuri/Micron, 2026-05-15). Agentic inference grows context ~30x/yr, inflating the KV-cache across the full memory hierarchy — HBM → expansion DRAM (SoCAM/DDR) → a brand-new NAND-flash context tier (245TB SSDs) (Sathya Kumar/Micron via UBS, 2026-05-15).

Key debates

  • Bull / where the money is — duration, not direction. The cleanest bull is UBS Arcuri on MU: PT $535→$1,625, "path to >$100 EPS," C27/28/29E $155/$167/$117, LTAs locking ~30% of industry DDR bits and ~$400bn FCF C27-29E (MU briefing, 2026-05-26/31 — flagged "most controversial UBS note recently"). Supporting voices: DB (Weathers, Buy) "DRAM tightness extends to 2028+" (2026-06-17); GS (Schneider) "ongoing tightness through CY27, significant upside to Street" (2026-06-09); Citi 8x C27 EPS from 5x (2026-05-19); Susquehanna $1,750 (2026-05-30). Mechanism: HBM's ~3:1 die displacement + node-driven bit decline + multi-year cleanroom/EUV lead times keep supply short while LTAs/SCAs convert the equity into an earnings-durability story (JPM, 2026-05-28). Suppliers are now optimizing for durability over sequential momentum — most will be at ~80% gross margin by year-end and are "securing the durability of earnings rather than seeking additional sequential upside" (JPM/Jay, 2026-05-28).
  • Supply discipline is the load-bearing assumption. Capex has not yet ramped meaningfully because it takes 12-18 months to source tools and find fab space; the industry mis-forecast a mid-26/early-27 down-cycle and is only now turning the crank (Micron ex-VP, procurement call, 2026-06-10). The 2028 governor is EUV: ASML's ~90 tools by end-CY27 are insufficient once Micron/Samsung/Hynix each add ≥100k wafers/month — Samsung/Hynix need ~20-25 tools each, Micron ~10-15, TSMC already ~40 (JPM/Jay, 2026-05-28). DRAM↔HBM mix is fungible overnight (no equipment change), so makers keep starving commodity DRAM to feed HBM — the "die penalty" that sustains the shortage (JPM, 2026-05-28; ex-VP, 2026-06-10).
  • Bear / what could break it — the commodity snap-back. The sharpest bear on disk is the ex-Micron VP of Procurement (2026-06-10): the market is pricing peak earnings as sustainable — "a highly catastrophic assumption." HBM differentiation is temporary (once a 2nd/3rd vendor qualifies at each customer "it already has become a commodity"); Micron's cost structure is worse than Samsung's and ~level with SK Hynix; and "your $900 stock right now will become a $50 stock again," possibly normalizing "within this calendar year or early next" as Micron/SK/Samsung bring disruptive new-fab capacity online ~a year out. He frames the AI buildout as a multi-layer bull-whip ("beer game") removed from end demand that can't amortize ~$6-700B of infra spend. The XP GPU specialist independently calls HBM "comoditizado" — once multiple vendors are NVIDIA-qualified the timing advantage decays to a commodity (XP/GPU-memory, 2026-05-27).
  • Second-source qualification & China. Customers force-qualify Chinese DRAM (CXMT) on cheap LPDDR Micron/Samsung/Hynix won't supply at current ASPs — "once they're in, they're in," a lasting share leak at the low end (ex-VP, 2026-06-10). CXMT filed its IPO at ~50% net margin, ASP +~80-85% q/q, but can't hit server or Apple grade; bottleneck is cleanroom + equipment, not policy — and US discussion of banning all equipment sales to China could cap it (JPM/Jay, 2026-05-28). HBM4E adds a wildcard: customer-supplied custom logic base die would de-commoditize HBM — but the ex-VP doubts it scales on warranty/yield/liability grounds (2026-06-10).
  • Timeline / inflection. Near-term catalyst is Micron's F3Q26 print (June 24): Street rev ~$34.7B vs guide $33.5B, FY27/28 EPS pegged ~$111/$116 (MU briefing, 2026-06-12). The structural inflection both camps point to is ~mid-2027 to 2028, when new fabs land — the bull says EUV scarcity caps that supply; the bear says it breaks the price.

Who's exposed (companies)

TickerAngleRead
MUPure-play — only US-listed scaled DRAM/NAND maker; #2-3 in HBM, credible 3rd HBM4 source given Samsung's product issuesMaximum leverage to the cycle. Q2 FY26 blowout (rev $23.9B, 75% GM); Q3 guide ~81% GM, EPS $19.15; CY26 HBM sold out; near-unanimous sell-side bull (PTs to $1,625-1,750). Bull = duration to 2028+; bear (own ex-VP) = commodity snap-back to a fraction of the price.
NVDAHBM consumer — largest HBM4 buyer (Rubin/Vera); memory is a cost line, not a revenue lineNet positive but two-edged: memory shortage is a supply governor on Rubin, and HBM4E roughly doubles the memory BOM — GM mid-70s defensible into FY28 per Arcuri, possibly aided by HBM consignment. Vera standalone CPU ($200B TAM claim) is itself a driver of the DRAM/SO-DIMM demand surge (JPM, 2026-05-28; NVDA page).

Also in prose: SK Hynix — HBM leader, reinforced by the NVIDIA multiyear tech partnership; the relative-performance long vs Samsung, lifted by Avera listing + HBM4 progress + passive/single-stock-ETF flows (JPM/Jay, 2026-05-28; Digitimes via MU briefing, 2026-06-09). Samsung — laggard: HBM4 qualified but volume not ramped, product still trails on power/speed; catalysts are HBM4 volume start, AMD chiplet wins, and shareholder-return policy (JPM, 2026-05-28; ex-VP, 2026-06-10). Kioxia — NAND pure-play re-rating on LTAs (P/E 3-5x → 7-8x, some bulls to 10-11x), June-quarter price +60-65% q/q, dividend/overhang the watch items (JPM/Mio, 2026-05-28). SNDK (SanDisk) — NAND beneficiary; led the LTA disclosure that set the framework, SSD TAM expectations raised to ~500 EB (~50-60% CAGR) vs mid-400s prior (JPM/Jay, 2026-05-28); appears in the memory-supercycle est-raise basket alongside MU/Samsung/SK Hynix (briefing 2026-05-29).

Sources

  • Equity calls (../_equity_calls/Semis): 2026-05-28 JPM — memory/China (Jay/Anila/Mio — LTAs, HBM ASP, CPU demand, EUV bottleneck, CXMT); 2026-05-15 Arcuri/Micron — agentic memory (Sathya Kumar — HBM4, KV-cache, memory hierarchy); 2026-06-10 Micron ex-VP — procurement (the bear — commoditization, capex lag, $50 stock); 2026-06-12 Samik/JPM — memory/HDD preview (HDD pricing, memory cost pass-through); 2026-05-27 XP — GPU/memory (HBM = HBM in the GPU, "comoditizado").
  • Briefings (../_briefings/by-ticker): MU — Arcuri $1,625 / ~$400bn FCF (2026-05-26/31), DRAM +50-60% / NAND +75-100% q/q 2Q (2026-05-29), Susquehanna $1,750 (2026-05-30), GS/DB tightness-to-2027/28 (2026-06-09/17), F3Q26 preview (2026-06-12).
  • Company pages: MU (pure-play), NVDA (HBM consumer).

Theme — Optical / CPO (Co-Packaged Optics & AI Interconnect)

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · cross-company theme · sources: equity calls (../../../equity_calls_transcripts/Semis), briefing roll-ups (../_briefings/by-ticker), earnings transcripts, company pages. Company pages: ../00_INDEX.md · themes index: 00_THEMES.md.

What it is / why it matters

AI interconnect is the bandwidth plumbing that decides how big a model you can train and how efficiently you can serve it — and it is in the middle of a copper-to-optics phase change. The mechanism is simple physics: every time you double interconnect bandwidth, the reach a high-fidelity electrical signal can travel halves, so as scale-up fabrics get both faster (800G → 1.6T per port) and physically larger (e.g. Rubin Ultra NVL576 = eight NVL72s in one fabric), copper runs out of room and the signal has to go photonic (Mike/Rothschild-Redburn, 2026-05-19). That pulls optics from a niche outside-the-box product into the heart of the rack, opening an incremental TAM that these vendors' products were never inside before — which is why a basket that "used to trade in the teens" now trades 25–30x CY28 (Meta Marshall/MS, 2026-05-07). The buy-side reason to care: the spend is being levered three ways at once — scale-out (between racks), scale-up (inside the rack), and scale-across (between data centers/buildings) — and the binding constraint through 2028 is not demand but laser/InP supply plus manufacturing ramp. This is a picks-and-shovels, supply-gated up-cycle, and the cleanest expression is the light source, not the module.

State of play (latest)

  • Demand is not the debate; supply is. Across every call the refrain is identical: orders strengthen weekly, the question is capacity. Chinese transceiver leaders said by end-Q1 they were "confident to fulfill the entire 2027 demand" and are struggling only to expand capacity; the upstream chokepoint is the EML laser / CW laser shortage, plus DSPs and even passive components (Andy/MS optical panel, 2026-05-07). Most Chinese transceiver names missed Q1 on delivery despite linear demand — a supply miss, not a demand miss, with procurement inventory building on balance sheets (Andy/MS, 2026-05-07).
  • 800G → 1.6T transition is the volume event, and it's bigger than 400G. Ciena expects to exit 2026 at or near #1 share in 800G coherent pluggables (was late to 400G, ~30% share); the 800G market is larger than 400G because port counts hold while price-per-plug rises, and 1.6T high-performance modems are already shipping (Ciena is sole vendor with a 1.6T modem vs the market's 1.2T) (Ciena/Brodi, 2026-06-12). Cisco/Acacia is pushing a 1.6T PAM4 receiver — its first organic inside-the-DC receiver — that 650 Group calls a multi-billion-dollar, "millions of units" opportunity on vendor-diversity demand (650 Group, 2026-06-04).
  • InP is the structural chokepoint and it does not clear. Lumentum guides InP-laser demand growing ~85% CAGR to end of decade while world InP capacity only 2x–4x over three years — so the imbalance persists; LITE + COHR are "completely sold out this year, next year and most of 2028," with supply response gated by multi-year fab conversions (Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19). Lumentum confirms take-or-pay LTAs locked into 2028 and "increasingly confident" into 2029; it is auctioning incremental units above committed take-or-pay at healthier pricing (Lumentum/Michael, 2026-05-07).
  • NVDA is forcing the CPO pace; AVGO/Cisco are measured. NVDA pushes CPO hardest because it needs the manufacturing volume in place ahead of the inevitable scale-up transition; Broadcom and Cisco have native switch silicon in-house and don't need to be early, so expect a higher CPO mix at NVDA next year than at AVGO/Cisco even for equivalent products (650 Group, 2026-06-04). Cisco's first CPO is expected in Silicon One on a ~102T/51.2T switch for scale-out (650 Group, 2026-06-04).
  • NVDA–Corning partnership reframed the CPO supply chain. Corning entered a commercial + technology + equity deal with NVDA (NVDA warrant up to ~$3.2B of stock; a direct multi-billion-dollar prepayment) to 10x US connectivity capacity and +50% fiber, explicitly aimed at "what's next going inside of the box" CPO — underpinning Corning's plan to build a $10B photonics business by 2030 (Corning IR, 2026-05-12). NVDA struck a parallel laser purchase-agreement with Lumentum; LITE reads Jensen "buying fibers" as a tell that NVDA is preparing to own scale-up cluster wiring (Lumentum/Wupen, 2026-05-07).
  • Scale-across has inflected from a standing start. 650 Group sizes scale-across going from ~$500M last year to a ~$10B market; Ciena is seeing a second major hyperscaler place scale-across coherent-pluggable orders, where RLS cuts fiber-pair count ~64x and 800G halves the modem count vs 400G (~50k vs ~100k modems for a 22-petabit link) (650 Group, 2026-06-04; Ciena, 2026-06-12).

Key debates

  • Bull / where the money is — own the light source. In a CPO module the laser is ~30–40% of BOM vs only 10–15% in a pluggable, because CPO lasers are far less commoditized (more IP, higher complexity) — so the differentiated money is in lasers, not module assembly (Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19). CPO TAM ~$15B by 2030 (Rothschild), which Coherent itself now calls "conservative"; scale-across ~$7B; OCS and scale-up Ethernet each ~$6B (Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19). Corning's content/GPU rises from low-hundreds of dollars toward 1.3–1.5x in scale-up, and a fully-optical scale-up world needs ~10x the fiber of today's 16 fibers/GPU (Corning, 2026-05-12).
  • Bear / what could break it. (1) Capacity flooding — any rampant-growth cycle invites overbuild fear, and China oversupply + valuation re-rating is already driving a "weaker pure-play optics" narrative (LITE, AAOI, FN, CIEN, IQE) vs "stronger" copper+optics names (SMTC, GLW, ALAB, CRDO, MRVL) (@crux_capital_, company-specific briefing 2026-06-19). (2) Multiples — the basket re-rated from teens to 25–30x CY28; MS flags least control of output = most caution, ranking by destiny-control: Lumentum > Corning > Coherent > Ciena (Meta Marshall/MS, 2026-05-07). (3) CPO-in-scale-out is modest — replacing scale-out transceivers with CPO saves only ~2–3% of cluster power and you lose the field-replaceability of a pluggable, so scale-out CPO penetration should lag scale-up materially (Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19).
  • CPO vs pluggables vs LPO — coexistence, not winner-take-all. The Feb panic that pluggables were "older generation / going to be very bad" has not shown up in demand — pluggables see no negative impact, even stronger demand; shortage in everything (Andy/MS, 2026-05-07). The likely split: CPO penetration much higher in scale-up than scale-out; pluggables/IMDD persist for scale-out and DCI; LPO and linear re-drivers fill copper-to-optics edges. Teradyne sees SiPh and EML coexisting (Jevons-paradox adoption as SiPh commoditizes) rather than one replacing the other (Teradyne/Arcuri, 2026-05-04).
  • OCS — Google is the anchor, externalization is the catalyst. OCS keeps signals optical via MEMS mirror arrays (Lumentum) or liquid-crystal (Coherent), avoiding power-hungry O-E-O conversions. Google historically built OCS in-house and is now sourcing externally — a natural buyer for Lumentum's MEMS arrays; LITE targets $400M OCS in 1H-FY27 and ~$1–2B in 2027, gated by test-time/throughput not demand, with the roadmap "fracturing" into high-Radix (training), low-Radix (inference) and scale-up variants (Lumentum/Michael+Wupen, 2026-05-07). Mike/Rothschild has more confidence in LITE's field-tested MEMS than COHR's less-proven liquid-crystal (2026-05-19).
  • Timeline for optics inside the rack. Scale-out CPO is the near wave (now); scale-up arrives in stages — uplinks/multi-rack first (OCP/October-26 timeframe for industry awareness), then true inside-the-rack GPU-to-GPU "realistically the very end of this decade," because copper stays cheaper and known until you're forced off it (650 Group, 2026-06-04). The disaggregation bet matters: hyperscalers are forcing Broadcom/Marvell to build switch chips (next-gen Tomahawk) that support an open CPO ecosystem, which is what lets Ciena/Nubis (Vesta CPO, Nitro linear re-driver) play against captive solutions (Ciena, 2026-06-12).

Who's exposed (companies)

TickerAngleRead
LITEPurest play (~90% theme exposure) — InP/EML/CW lasers, CPO lasers, OCS (MEMS), pumps for scale-acrossNet positive, top of the pack. Sole-source on key 1.6T transceiver SKUs at primary customer near-term; sold out into 2028, take-or-pay into 2028 / "confident into 2029"; OCS $400M 1H-FY27, capacity (San Jose→Caswell→Greensboro) the gating item, not demand (Lumentum, 2026-05-07; Meta Marshall, 2026-05-07).
COHR#2 laser/CPO play (~2/3 theme exposure) — lasers, OCS (liquid-crystal), 6-inch InP transitionNet positive but more capacity-constrained and less destiny-control than LITE; gross-margin uplift hinges on 3"→6" InP yields (timing/yield watch); also a large customer of LITE's lasers, so its orders are a leading indicator of demand (Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19; Lumentum, 2026-05-07).
AAOILaser → module → ELS TAM-expansion story; potential AMD customer/investment chatterSpeculative, early — revenue ramp not until 2027 H1/H2 ("extremely early stage"); high-conviction-but-volatile in the social corpus; sits in the "weaker pure-play optics" / China-oversupply-risk bucket (company-specific briefing 2026-06-19).
CIENCoherent systems/pluggables (~50% theme exposure), RLS/HyperRail, DCOM, Nubis CPO/re-driverNet positive on scale-across + 800G share gains (exiting '26 ~#1 in 800G), >70% photonics share, $7.7B backlog >100% coverage; but MS flags it as least-in-control of output (supply-chain dependent) and richest valuation (peg ~2.5x) — most-cautious near-term ranking (Ciena, 2026-06-12; Meta Marshall, 2026-05-07; Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19).
GLWFiber + connectivity + new photonics business; NVDA partner + 3 hyperscaler LTAsNet positive, high destiny-control (#2 ranking). $10B photonics by 2030, 10x US connectivity / +50% fiber capacity funded by NVDA prepay + warrants; thesis is content expansion not price; internal CPO-penetration view 10–50% by 2030 (Corning, 2026-05-12; Meta Marshall, 2026-05-07).
ANETEthernet switching scale-out/scale-across; benefits from optics-rich, higher-Radix networksIndirect/net positive — front-end network intensity rising (1 port per 4 GPUs vs 1:8), Ethernet-friendly architectures (Google Virgo white paper); supply-constrained on 3nm switch ASICs + transceivers; deferred-rev accounting quirk vs peers (650 Group, 2026-06-04).
AVGONative switch silicon (Tomahawk 6) + own CPO; custom-ASIC/TPU networkingNet positive — talking networking attach rates up (vs NVDA's flat ~15–16%), which is upside risk to CPO TAM; takes a measured CPO pace given in-house silicon; hyperscalers forcing its switch chips to support open CPO ecosystem (Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19; Ciena, 2026-06-12; 650 Group, 2026-06-04).
NVDASets the CPO pace; largest optics pull; pushing optics into scale-upNet positive demand driver — pushing CPO hardest to pre-build volume for scale-up; struck laser (LITE) + fiber/photonics (GLW) supply agreements signalling scale-up cluster wiring; consensus models its networking attach flat (debate vs AVGO) (650 Group, 2026-06-04; Lumentum, 2026-05-07; Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19).

Also in prose: Cisco/Acacia (1.6T PAM4 receiver, Silicon One CPO for scale-out — 650 Group, 2026-06-04); Marvell (Celestial AI; strong IMDD, moving to CoLED for inside-the-DC — Ciena, 2026-06-12); Credo / Astera Labs (AEC→optical pivot, ZeroFlap optics; ALAB Scorpio for Trainium scale-up — Mike/Rothschild, 2026-05-19); Teradyne / Advantest (SiPh test, 4 insertions, ~$100M test TAM this year, Quantifi/Fi-Con Tech — Teradyne/Arcuri, 2026-05-04); Fujikura / Sumitomo / Furukawa (Japan fiber/cable + CW/EML/DFB laser capacity — MS optical panel, 2026-05-07).

Sources

  • Equity calls (../../../equity_calls_transcripts/Semis): 2026-06-04 650 Group — networking; 2026-05-07 Meta Marshall/MS — optical; 2026-05-19 Rothschild-Redburn (Mike) — optical; 2026-05-12 Corning IR — optical; 2026-06-12 Ciena — optical; 2026-05-07 Lumentum — follow-up; 2026-05-04 Teradyne/Arcuri (UBS) — CPO/SiPh test. Laterals: 2026-05-18 Samik — Lumentum; 2026-04-09 Semtech — optical/copper.
  • Briefings (../_briefings/by-ticker): LITE, COHR, AAOI, CIEN, GLW, ANET. Daily company-specific roll-up 2026-06-19: AAOI AMD-customer chatter (Rosenblatt via @crux_capital_); @crux_capital_ connectivity fragmentation framework (stronger copper+optics vs weaker pure-play optics, China oversupply); BESI CPO-upside read-through (MS, OW €300).
  • Company pages: LITE, COHR, AAOI, CIEN, GLW, ANET, AVGO, NVDA. Related theme: CoWoS / Advanced Packaging (SoIC slice for SiPh/CPO integration).

Theme — Semicap / WFE (Wafer-Fab Equipment)

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · cross-company theme · sources: equity calls (../../../equity_calls_transcripts/Semis), briefing roll-ups (../_briefings/by-ticker), earnings transcripts, research library (E:\research_library). Company pages: ../00_INDEX.md · themes index: 00_THEMES.md.

What it is / why it matters

WFE is the toolset that builds the fabs — litho, deposition, etch, metrology/inspection, advanced packaging and test. We are in the early-to-mid innings of a multi-year up-cycle whose ceiling keeps getting raised: the bull-case WFE TAM is now ~$200B for CY27 and ~$250B for CY28 (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15), i.e. roughly +$50B added each of the next two years off a base the Street still models at high-$160s/$170s. The driver is memory capex (DRAM, HBM) plus a foundry/logic node-transition wave (3nm/2nm and the first sub-2nm) and an Intel/Terafab greenfield leg layered on top. For a buy-sider the read is simple: order visibility is unprecedented (Applied cited eight quarters of customer visibility — Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15), positioning is less crowded than the tape implies, and the equipment names have barely flexed pricing/margin upside.

State of play (latest)

  • WFE TAM marching up. UBS holds $200B CY27 / $250B CY28, going higher again in CY29; Arcuri stresses the buy side is "not there" — he says these numbers in every meeting and people "look at me like I have two heads" (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15). More conservative house views have ratcheted up too: JPM/Tokyo desk now hears CY26 +25–30% and CY27 +20–25%, up from prior 21%/18% (Mio Shikona, JPM memory call, 2026-05-28).
  • The large caps raised guides this print. AMAT took systems growth from ~20% to 30%+ for the year; all three US names (AMAT/LRCX/KLAC) saw a step-up in orders over the prior 90 days. KLA is the only one to commit publicly that CY27 WFE > CY26; AMAT now flags CY28 as another growth year — a notable stake in the ground from a conservative company (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15).
  • Arcuri thinks AMAT is sandbagging H2. AMAT's "up 30%" implies shipments barely grow in Oct/Jan quarters after +$900M sequential steps; Arcuri sees "zero evidence" of a decel in the slots and models shipments +40%, reading the guide as conservatism (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15).
  • Segment leadership rotates by year. CY26 favors the deposition/etch-intensive names (LRCX, AMAT) as DRAM/HBM bulk capacity ramps; CY27 shifts toward the new-node leaders (KLAC, ASML) as sub-2nm programs fire — TSMC A14, Intel 14A, plus 2nm expansion at Samsung/Rapidus and initial Terafab orders (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15).
  • Advanced packaging re-accelerating. After a soft 2025 (tough HBM-WFE compare from Q4-2024), LRCX and AMAT both guide advanced packaging +50%+ this year, KLA ~60%; AMAT advanced packaging becomes a ~$2B business, nearing 10% of systems (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15). AMAT's EnexX (panel-level plating, ex-ASMPT) and earlier Tango (panel PVD) buys are CoPoS plays for 510–600mm panels (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15).
  • Litho is the choke point. ASML EUV capacity of ~90 tools by end-2027 looks insufficient against Samsung/Hynix/Micron each adding ~100k wfr/mo from 2028 (needing 20–25 tools each, Micron 10–15) plus TSMC already near 40 tools — EUV is "really, really the bottleneck" (Jay/JPM memory call, 2026-05-28). UBS counters that ASML will "march to that drum" and add capacity rather than cap the cycle (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15).

Key debates

  • Bull / where the money is: memory is the engine. DRAM ASP +50–60% Q/Q in 2Q26, +20% Q/Q in 3Q26; NAND +75–100% Q/Q in 2Q (briefings, 2026-05-29). Agentic AI lifts the GPU:CPU ratio and DRAM content — AI-CPU demand alone (Vera 500k units CY26 → 3M CY27, Gokul/JPM) could add 15–20%+ to server bit demand (Jay/JPM memory call, 2026-05-28). CPU is far more WFE-intensive than GPU, so a rotation toward inference/CPU racks is more WFE per dollar (Arcuri/UBS, 2026-05-15). DRAM is the dollar driver of the incremental $50B/yr, favoring high-DRAM-share toolmakers (AMAT ~25% of every DRAM WFE dollar; AMAT PVD ~80%+ share at ~70% gross margin).
  • KV-cache → NAND, the new structural leg: agentic inference grows context ~30x/yr, inflating the KV-cache that must be stored and served across a memory hierarchy — HBM → DRAM/CXL expansion → and crucially a new NAND-flash "context memory" tier (tens-to-hundreds of TB), an entirely new data-center use case for NAND. SSD data-center demand is growing faster than HDD (Sathya Kumar, Arcuri-Micron memory call, 2026-05-15). Supportive of NAND-WFE durability even though Applied still sees little greenfield NAND.
  • Bear / what could break it: (1) buy-side bars are already very high — AMAT's guide-up "wasn't enough" to clear them and the stock sold off on the print despite a strong number (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15); (2) the H2 decel AMAT itself guided to, if real rather than conservatism, would dent the slope; (3) China ICAP overcapacity caps mature-node tool demand (AMAT: don't expect China growth); (4) NAND greenfield is still absent — first greenfield NAND wafer-outs not until CY28 (Micron Singapore), so the NAND leg is technology-migration-only near term (Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15); (5) a China equipment-export ban under US discussion would hit CXMT's expansion (Jay/JPM, 2026-05-28) — bearish for tool sales into China, marginally bullish for ex-China supply tightness.
  • Timeline / inflection points: CY26 = depo/etch + DRAM bulk capacity; H2-26 = memory mix richens, EUV-capacity confirmation from ASML is the catalyst the Street is waiting on; CY27 = sub-2nm node wave + first Terafab/Intel orders; CY28 = first greenfield NAND, +$50B WFE; CY29 = UBS sees the TAM higher again. Near-term datapoint: MU reports F3Q26 on 2026-06-24 (briefings, 2026-06-12).

Who's exposed (companies)

TickerAngleRead
ASMLLitho near-monopoly; EUV/High-NA + immersion DUV. The cycle's choke point.Net positive, capacity-gated. JPM (Deshpande) FY27/28 rev EUR55.4B/63.8B (+17%/+22% vs cons), PT EUR1,900; 90 EUV units 2027, 200 immersion DUV (+35% vs cons) (briefings, 2026-06-03). Terafab + High-NA + DRAM capex the upside; capacity/Europe-China discount the pushback.
MUBoth a memory-capex spender (drives WFE) and the demand-side beneficiary (DRAM/HBM/NAND pricing + KV-cache NAND tier).Strongly positive. UBS (Arcuri) PT $1,625, path to >$100 EPS; ~$400bn FCF C27–29E; LTAs lock ~30% of industry DDR bits (briefings, 2026-05-26/05-31). DRAM tightness extends to 2028+. EUV access (post-Idaho fabs) a watch item.
TSMFoundry capex — the logic leg of WFE; ~40 EUV tools and rising; A14 in the sub-2nm wave.Positive. Anchor customer for the CY27 node transition; capex/EUV pull a primary WFE swing factor (Jay/JPM, 2026-05-28; Sur/JPM, 2026-05-15).

Not in the universe but central to the theme (prose): AMAT (Applied) — depo/PVD/CMP leader, ~25% DRAM WFE share, ~80%+ PVD share at ~70% GM, advanced packaging ~$2B; systems guide raised to +30%, UBS/JPM both OW. LRCX (Lam) — etch/deposition leader, the highest-conviction WFE share-gainer; UBS pairs LRCX + AMAT as "the two stocks you want to own." KLAC (KLA) — metrology/inspection, levered to early-stage new-node ramps; only large-cap to commit CY27 WFE > CY26, advanced packaging +~60%. TER (Teradyne) — test, with AI/compute and CPO leverage. On the memory-maker side, SK Hynix / Samsung / Kioxia / SanDisk / CXMT are the capex spenders setting DRAM/NAND tool demand.

Sources

  • Equity calls (../../../equity_calls_transcripts/Semis/):
    • 2026-05-15_Harlan_semicap-AMAT.md — Sur/JPM: AMAT print, 8-qtr visibility, segment leadership by year, adv. packaging, NAND greenfield CY28.
    • 2026-05-15_Arcuri_AMAT-recap.md — Arcuri/UBS: $200B/$250B CY27/28 WFE, H2 sandbag thesis, PVD margins, CoPoS M&A, EUV-bottleneck rebuttal, prefers AMAT/LRCX.
    • 2026-05-28_JPM_memory-China.md — Jay/Mio/JPM: WFE +25–30% CY26 / +20–25% CY27, EUV bottleneck math, CXMT/China export-ban risk, CPU/Vera DRAM uplift.
    • 2026-05-15_Arcuri-Micron_memory.md — Sathya Kumar (Micron): agentic inference, KV-cache memory hierarchy, NAND "context memory" tier.
  • Briefings (../_briefings/by-ticker/): ASML.md, MU.md — PT/estimate revisions, DRAM/NAND ASP cadence, Terafab.
  • Research library (E:\research_library, catalog 00_CATALOGO.md): SemiCap_2025_Actuals_and_Outlook.pdf (2026-04-28, ASML/AMAT/LRCX/KLAC/TER/Hynix, WFE & memory cycle [06]); semicap_logic_and_memory_process_maps_10_slides.pptx (2026-05-21, litho/depo/etch/metrology/packaging/test maps [06]).

Theme — Tokenmaxxing / AI Monetization

Wiki · generated 2026-06-19 · cross-company theme · sources: equity calls (../../equity_calls_transcripts), briefing roll-ups (../_briefings/by-ticker), morning briefings (E:\briefings\2026), research library (E:\research_library). Company pages: ../00_INDEX.md · themes index: 00_THEMES.md.

What it is / why it matters

"Tokenmaxxing" was the 2025 reflex of enterprises (and their employees) maxing out token consumption to look AI-forward; the 2026 sequel is the pullback — firms now demanding control over token spend, routing cheaper tasks to cheaper models, and testing open-source/DeepSeek substitutes. The buy-side question this resolves: does economic value keep accruing to frontier models (Anthropic/OpenAI pricing power, and the GPU capex that underwrites it), or does model substitution and cost discipline cap the monetization curve? The on-disk evidence (Jun 2026) is two-sided: spend is still climbing fast and the frontier still captures ~90% of revenue, but a cost-control backlash is real on the margin and is starting to move enterprise behavior, vendor pricing (MSFT Copilot → usage), and the read-through to hyperscaler demand.

State of play (latest)

  • The frontier is still winning the revenue, even as tokens diffuse. BG2Pod (Gerstner/Baker, 2026-06-12): "Frontier might be 90% of the economic value; open source might be 80% of tokens" — the early-2025 bear case that cheap open-source tokens would close the gap on frontier has been "decisively wrong, probably more than 90%." Long-running agentic tasks (Fable 5 / Mythos / GPT-5.5) are the unlock driving more token consumption "as far as the eye can see."
  • But enterprises have flipped from max-spend to cost-control. Big Technology Podcast / Ramp lead economist Eric Kharazian (research_library, 2026-06-18): "We went through the token-maxing era… now businesses are saying we need to actually rein in token spend or at least understand it." Per-business AI token spend up 15x Jan-2025→May-2026; still +14% m/m last month — the pullback is "on the margin" and "not happening fast enough to pull back the rising slope."
  • Spend levels remain small / early. Ramp data (2026-06-18): top-1% of firms spend $7,449/employee/month on AI (software engineers ~$15-16k); top-10% $611; median just $11. Even for the top quartile, AI is only ~2% of non-payroll spend. 54% of US firms now pay for AI in some form. Read: huge runway, not a saturation/air-pocket story yet.
  • Multi-vendor is the new normal — the opposite of SaaS lock-in. Top-1% AI spenders use 8 vendors on average vs. ~2 for the median (Ramp, 2026-06-18). "Should I buy OpenAI or Anthropic?" is the wrong question — advanced users buy both and route per-task.
  • MSFT is repricing the seat model toward usage. Axios via morning briefing (2026-06-17): Microsoft moving Copilot Cowork to usage pricing and considering a hosted DeepSeek V4 as a cheaper option — "fits the tokenmaxxing pullback narrative." Earlier (briefing 2026-06-18, @zerohedge): MSFT weighing DeepSeek to replace expensive OpenAI/Anthropic APIs, raising MSFT-OpenAI partnership ROI questions. Jefferies/Brent Thill NDR (2026-06-12): MSFT framing "per user + consumptive" agentic pricing as the "best TAM ever."
  • DeepSeek/open-source is rising but overrated. Ramp (2026-06-18): DeepSeek among fastest-growing vendors, but only ~0.4% of businesses use it (up from 0.1%); open-source platforms at 5% of firms (up from 1% a year ago). History rhymes — DeepSeek spiked in early 2025 then collapsed to ~0.1% once OpenAI/Anthropic cut prices; security/reputational concerns cap Western adoption.
  • The cost-control product gap = a wedge for Google. Ramp (2026-06-18): neither OpenAI nor Anthropic has built tools that let firms manage/cap token spend (80% of their business revenue is token-based, so they're incentivized the other way). Google is "underrated" — diversified revenue means it can offer routing/cost-control and even run AI as a loss-leader to pull Cloud; Gemini already distributed via Workspace.
  • Model-routing is becoming standard practice. BG2Pod (2026-06-12): enterprise survey of 300 companies — firms routing "back-of-house" tasks (KYC, customer service) to cheaper/open-source models while keeping high-value work (coding) on frontier; Harvey used a router + RL on an open-source model to beat Opus 4.7/4.8 "at a lower cost." Allen Roush (equity_calls, 2026-04-28): frontier labs are profitable on per-token API pricing and lose money on the $20/$200 subscriptions — "I don't think they're overpricing."
  • The "tokenmaxxing" label also has an employee-behavior edge. Amazon set weekly AI-usage targets for >80% of devs, tracked on internal leaderboards, with employees using "MeshClaw" to automate pointless tasks to pad stats (FT via briefings, AMZN.md 2026-05-12); @zerohedge tied it to Anthropic ARR padding and Claude's alleged ~$500M "mystery bill" (2026-05-29). META cracking down on employee token usage on surging cost, pivoting to in-house models (Vitalknowledge via META.md).

Key debates

  • Bull / where the money is: Frontier captures the value and long-running agents expand token demand structurally — BG2Pod (2026-06-12): "two things can be true… majority of economic value accrues to the frontier, majority of tokens are open source." Bullish for the frontier labs and — counterintuitively — for compute: "the better open source does, the better it is for compute providers" because frontier labs capturing less margin means more spend on raw compute (Baker, 2026-06-12). NVDA bull "tokenmaxx" thesis cited by MS Sales (NVDA.md 2026-06-11).
  • Bear / what could break it: (1) Capital-intensive price war. Vital Knowledge (NVDA.md 2026-06-11): "OpenAI considering token price war while being capital-intensive — a price war in a capital-intensive industry is historically toxic"; Oracle F27 capex/sales >100% is "why NVDA and MU trade at such low PEs." (2) Substitution + routing caps frontier ARR if open-source closes the long-running-task gap (Jensen's model-routing argument, BG2Pod 2026-06-12). (3) AWS read-through: Bernstein flagged a "tokenmaxxing hangover" — non-AI cloud growth flattening as compute is reallocated to AI labs, a 4Q risk (AMZN.md). (4) Chanos (NVDA.md 2026-06-14): if GPU data-center economics are as good as claimed, why not raise prices 3-4x — implying either the premium is overstated or rent is being left on the table.
  • Timeline / inflection: Next 12 months is "the most indicative" of whether closed-source keeps its stranglehold or open-source grabs the long tail (Clark Tang, BG2Pod 2026-06-12). Watch: MSFT Copilot usage-pricing rollout + hosted-DeepSeek decision; whether OpenAI's mooted price cuts dislodge stickiness; Ramp's next data prints on DeepSeek/open-source share.

Who's exposed (companies)

TickerAngleRead
MSFTRepricing Copilot to usage; weighing hosted DeepSeek; MAI in-house models for cost/perf controlNet neutral-to-positive on monetization optionality, but DeepSeek substitution + OpenAI ARR risk is the key bear flag (Axios 2026-06-17; @zerohedge 2026-06-18). Usage pricing = "best TAM ever" per JEF (2026-06-12) but exposes seat economics.
GOOGDiversified revenue lets it offer cost-control/routing + AI as loss-leader to pull CloudUnderrated winner of the cost-discipline shift — Gemini distributed via Workspace; cheap Flash models popular; Cloud +63% (Ramp 2026-06-18; GOOG Q1 FY26).
AMZNBedrock TaaS (Anthropic) monetization; AWS pricing power as compute reallocates to AI"Tokenmaxxing hangover" — AWS pricing-power tailwind but 4Q non-AI cloud growth at risk; Bedrock+Anthropic added $21B net-new ARR in a quarter (Bernstein; SemiAnalysis 2026-06-03).
ORCLOCI capex/sales >100% = the poster child for AI-monetization-sustainability doubtBear's exhibit A — capital intensity vs. uncertain FCF if a token price war hits (Vital Knowledge 2026-06-11).
NVDADemand read-through: tokens consumed → GPUs; "tokenmaxx" bull thesis vs. price-war bearNet positive on volume (open-source diffusion raises compute demand), but low PE reflects FCF-sustainability doubt; Jensen: compute cost is dynamic, cheaper compute → more demand (NVDA.md 2026-06-11/13).
AMDInference-tilted demand read-through; cheaper-model substitution favors inference volumeIndirect positive — token-routing/open-source shifts workloads toward cheaper inference where AMD competes; no direct datapoint, demand-derived.

Private (no wiki page): Anthropic and OpenAI are the direct objects of the theme. Both are profitable on per-token API but lose money on flat subscriptions (Allen Roush, 2026-04-28); ~80% of their business revenue is token-based, leaving them structurally unincentivized to build cost-control tooling (Ramp, 2026-06-18). Anthropic overtook OpenAI in US business adoption (~41% vs ~39.5% of firms) and is the stickiness leader via Claude Code despite frequent compute-constrained outages; the Fable 5 export-control "ban" may accelerate adoption via a forbidden-fruit effect (Big Technology / Ramp, 2026-06-18).
